An **LM35 temperature sensor** functions exactly the same way, but electronically: | Human Body | Electronic Equivalent (Arduino + LM35) | |------------|----------------------------------------| | Skin nerve endings (receptors) | **LM35 sensing element** | | Electrical nerve impulse | **Analog voltage (10 mV / °C)** | | Brain (interprets signal) | **ATmega328P microcontroller (ADC)** | | You saying "It's hot!" | **Serial Monitor printing "Temp: 60 °C"** | ### 1.3 Why Temperature Sensing? — KTU Highlight > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping:** This experiment directly addresses **CO2** — *"Interface various sensors with Arduino and write embedded C/C++ programs to acquire and process real-world data."* **Real-world Engineering Applications:** - 🌡️ **Smart weather stations** (KTU project-domain: IoT) - 🏭 Industrial boiler monitoring - 🏥 Medical incubators and cold-chain logistics - 🌱 Precision agriculture (greenhouse climate control) - 🔥 Fire-alarm and HVAC automation systems ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **LM35 Scale Factor:** **10 mV / °C** (i.e., 0.010 V per degree Celsius rise) - **Operating Range (LM35):** **−55 °C to +150 °C** - **Arduino UNO ADC:** **10-bit resolution** → 2¹⁰ = **1024 discrete levels** - **Default Reference Voltage (Vref):** **5.0 V DC** - **Quiescent Current Draw (LM35):** **< 60 µA** (extremely low power) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — "How Does It Actually Work?" The interfacing mechanism follows a clean **four-stage pipeline**: - **Stage 1 — Sensing (Transduction):** The LM35's internal bipolar junction transistors (BJTs) develop a **bandgap-referenced voltage** that is **linearly proportional** to absolute temperature, scaled precisely at **10 mV / °C**. - **Stage 2 — Analog Signal Transmission:** The output pin (Vout) of LM35 delivers a continuously varying DC voltage in the range **0 mV (0 °C) to 1500 mV (150 °C)** to the Arduino's analog pin **A0**. - **Stage 3 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** The **ATmega328P's** built-in **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** samples this analog voltage and converts it into a digital integer between **0 and 1023**. - **Stage 4 — Software Scaling & Display:** The Arduino sketch reverses the process mathematically: it first converts the **ADC integer → voltage (in Volts)**, then **voltage → temperature (in °C)**, and finally prints the result via UART to the **Serial Monitor** at **9600 baud**. > [!NOTE] > The linearity of LM35 is its biggest advantage. Unlike thermistors, **no lookup table or Steinhart-Hart equation is required** — a simple linear scaling suffices. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams) | # | Parameter / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Boundary Condition | |---|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | LM35 Output Voltage | $V_{out} = 0.010 \times T$ | Volts, T in °C | | 2 | Temperature (from Vout) | $T = V_{out} \times 100$ | °C | | 3 | ADC Reading (Arduino) | $ADC = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023$ | Dimensionless integer [0, 1023] | | 4 | Input Voltage (from ADC) | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023}$ | Volts | | 5 | **Master Temperature Formula** | $T = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref} \times 100}{1023}$ | °C (most important for exam) | | 6 | Simplified for Vref = 5 V | $T = \dfrac{ADC \times 500}{1023}$ | °C (Arduino UNO default) | | 7 | Simplified for Vref = 3.3 V | $T = \dfrac{ADC \times 330}{1023}$ | °C (improved accuracy) | | 8 | Voltage per ADC step | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{1024}$ | Volts per LSB | | 9 | Temperature per ADC step | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\Delta V}{0.010}$ | °C per LSB | | 10 | Baud Rate (Serial) | $9600 \text{ bps}$ | Standard KTU lab configuration | ### 2.3 Real-World Utility of This Equation in Engineering The master formula $T = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref} \times 100}{1023}$ is a **canonical example of transducer linearization**, a topic that appears in: - **KTU EST130 (Introduction to Sensors)** — instrumentation theory - **KTU ECT203 (Analog Electronics)** — ADC fundamentals - **Industry 4.0 sensor calibration standards (ISA/IEC 60751)** In production-grade systems, the same formula is extended with **offset correction** and **multi-point calibration** to eliminate systematic errors (e.g., self-heating of the LM35 at high ambient temperatures). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: "From a Raw ADC Integer to a Temperature in °C" We are given the following system parameters for an **Arduino UNO + LM35** setup: $$ V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}, \quad N_{ADC} = 10 \text{ bits}, \quad k_{LM35} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} = 0.010 \text{ V/°C} $$ --- **Step 1 — Define the LM35 Sensor Equation (Physical Layer)** The LM35 datasheet states that its output voltage is *linearly proportional* to the Celsius temperature with a slope of 10 mV/°C and zero offset at 0 °C. $$ V_{out} = k_{LM35} \times T $$ Substituting the numerical value of $k_{LM35}$: $$ V_{out} = 0.010 \times T $$ This tells us: at 25 °C, the LM35 outputs $V_{out} = 0.010 \times 25 = 0.25 \text{ V} = 250 \text{ mV}$. --- **Step 2 — Invert the Sensor Equation to Express T in Terms of Vout** We rearrange the equation from Step 1 to isolate $T$: $$ T = \frac{V_{out}}{0.010} = 100 \times V_{out} $$ This means: if we measure 0.314 V on the LM35 output, the temperature is $T = 100 \times 0.314 = 31.4 \text{ °C}$. --- **Step 3 — Define the Arduino ADC Equation (Hardware Layer)** The Arduino's ATmega328P uses a **Successive Approximation ADC** with 10-bit resolution. The general ADC equation is: $$ ADC = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^{N_{ADC}} - 1) $$ For a 10-bit ADC, $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$. Substituting: $$ ADC = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023 $$ In our circuit, the LM35's Vout is directly wired to Arduino's analog pin, so $V_{in} = V_{out}$: $$ ADC = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023 $$ --- **Step 4 — Invert the ADC Equation to Recover Vout from the ADC Reading** Rearranging Step 3: $$ V_{out} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023} $$ This is the operation performed (conceptually) by the Arduino's `analogRead()` function in reverse, inside our code. --- **Step 5 — Combine the Two Equations (The "Master Formula")** We now substitute the result of Step 4 into the result of Step 2: $$ T = 100 \times V_{out} = 100 \times \left( \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023} \right) $$ Grouping all constants: $$ T = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref} \times 100}{1023} $$ This is the **unified, end-to-end conversion equation** that maps a raw 10-bit ADC integer to a temperature in degrees Celsius. --- **Step 6 — Plug in the Numerical Value of Vref = 5.0 V** $$ T = \frac{ADC \times 5.0 \times 100}{1023} $$ $$ T = \frac{ADC \times 500}{1023} $$ For example, if `analogRead(A0)` returns **204**, the temperature is: $$ T = \frac{204 \times 500}{1023} = \frac{102000}{1023} \approx 99.71 \text{ °C} $$ --- **Step 7 — Calculate the Smallest Detectable Voltage Change (Resolution)** Each step of the 10-bit ADC represents a "Least Significant Bit" (LSB). The voltage equivalent of one LSB is: $$ \Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N_{ADC}}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} \approx 0.00488 \text{ V} = 4.88 \text{ mV} $$ --- **Step 8 — Calculate the Smallest Detectable Temperature Change** Since LM35 produces 10 mV/°C, the temperature equivalent of 4.88 mV is: $$ \Delta T = \frac{\Delta V}{0.010} = \frac{0.00488}{0.010} \approx 0.488 \text{ °C} $$ **Conclusion of derivation:** The Arduino UNO + LM35 system has a **theoretical temperature resolution of ≈ 0.488 °C per ADC step** at 5 V reference. For better resolution, either use `analogReference(INTERNAL)` (1.1 V ref) or migrate to a 12-bit external ADC (e.g., ADS1115). --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Scenarios for Board Problems | Given ADC Reading | Computed Voltage $V_{out}$ | Final Temperature $T$ | Real-World Context | |-------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|---------------------| | 0 | 0.000 V | 0.00 °C | Pure ice-water mixture | | 102 | 0.498 V | 49.85 °C | Hot beverage | | 204 | 0.997 V | 99.71 °C | Boiling water (sea level) | | 307 | 1.501 V | 150.07 °C | LM35 upper limit | | 512 | 2.503 V | 250.29 °C | **Out of LM35 range** ⚠️ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common mistake is failing to recognize that the **ADC reading is dimensionless** (0–1023) and the **Vref must be in Volts** for unit consistency. Always verify that your final answer is in °C, not mV! --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System Block Diagram — Hardware Architecture The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps all functional modules of the LM35–Arduino interfacing setup, including the data-flow direction at each stage. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PowerStage["POWER STAGE"] nodePSU["5V DC Regulator Supply"] nodeGND["Common Ground Rail"] end subgraph SensorStage["SENSING STAGE"] nodeLM35["LM35 Temperature Sensor
Linear Analog Transducer"] nodeVoutLM["Vout Pin = 10 mV per degree C"] end subgraph ProcessingStage["PROCESSING STAGE - ARDUINO UNO"] nodeA0["Analog Pin A0
ADC Input Channel"] nodeADC["ATmega328P 10-bit SAR ADC
Resolution 4.88 mV per LSB"] nodeMCU["MCU Core
Runs embedded C sketch"] end subgraph OutputStage["OUTPUT STAGE"] nodeUART["UART Serial Port
9600 Baud"] nodeMonitor["PC Serial Monitor
or PuTTY Terminal"] nodeLCD["Optional 16x2 LCD
I2C or Parallel"] end nodePSU -->|5V Rail| nodeLM35 nodeGND --> nodeLM35 nodeLM35 --> nodeVoutLM nodeVoutLM -->|Analog Voltage 0 to 1.5V| nodeA0 nodeA0 --> nodeADC nodeADC -->|Integer 0 to 1023| nodeMCU nodeMCU -->|Formatted ASCII Text| nodeUART nodeMCU -->|Optional Data| nodeLCD nodeUART --> nodeMonitor ``` ### 4.2 Program Flow Diagram — Software Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart(["Start - Power ON"]) --> nodeInit["Serial.begin 9600
Initialize UART"] nodeInit --> nodeLoop{{"loop function - Infinite Cycle"}} nodeLoop --> nodeRead["analogRead A0
Acquire 10-bit integer"] nodeRead --> nodeConvV["float voltage = adc * 5.0 / 1023.0"] nodeConvV --> nodeConvT["float tempC = voltage * 100.0"] nodeConvT --> nodePrint["Serial.print tempC with unit degree C"] nodePrint --> nodeDelay["delay 1000 milliseconds"] nodeDelay --> nodeLoop ``` ### 4.3 Pin Connection Table (For Lab Record) | LM35 Pin | Arduino UNO Pin | Wire Color (Recommended) | Function | |----------|------------------|---------------------------|----------| | Pin 1 (Vcc) | **5V** | Red | Powers the sensor (4 V – 30 V) | | Pin 2 (Vout) | **A0** | Yellow | Carries analog temperature voltage | | Pin 3 (GND) | **GND** | Black | Common ground reference | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Lab Tip:** Always use the **flat side of the LM35 facing you** (when looking at the printed label). Pin numbering is **left-to-right: Vcc, Vout, GND** in TO-92 package. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *List any three key specifications of the LM35 temperature sensor and explain why it is preferred over a thermistor in microcontroller-based applications.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **LM35** is a precision centigrade temperature sensor from Texas Instruments. Its three key specifications are: - **Scale factor of 10 mV/°C** — output voltage is *directly* proportional to Celsius temperature, requiring no linearization in software. - **Wide operating range: −55 °C to +150 °C** — covers industrial, medical, and environmental use cases. - **Low self-heating: < 0.1 °C in still air** — minimizes measurement error. **Why preferred over thermistor:** A thermistor's resistance is *non-linearly* related to temperature, requiring the Steinhart–Hart equation or a lookup table for accurate conversion. The LM35's **linear analog output** simplifies code, reduces CPU overhead, and makes it ideal for beginner-level microcontroller labs (KTU PCCGL607). *(3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *State the role of the ATmega328P's ADC in the LM35 interfacing circuit. What is the resolution of this ADC when Vref = 5 V?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** inside the ATmega328P converts the LM35's continuously varying analog voltage (0–1.5 V) into a **discrete 10-bit digital integer** in the range 0 to 1023. This conversion is essential because microcontrollers can only process digital data. **Resolution calculation:** $$ \text{Voltage per step} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV} $$ Therefore, the **smallest voltage change detectable by the Arduino UNO's ADC is 4.88 mV**, which corresponds to approximately **0.488 °C** when paired with the LM35. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency is the #1 cause of mark deduction.** Always carry units (V, mV, °C) through every calculation step. > 2. **Integer division trap:** In Arduino C/C++, `adc * 500 / 1023` may truncate to an integer if all three are declared as `int`. Always use the **`float` data type** to preserve decimal precision. > 3. **Vref assumption:** If the question says "Arduino UNO," assume $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$. If it says "Arduino Due / 3.3 V logic," assume $V_{ref} = 3.3\text{ V}$. > 4. **Missing `#include <...>` or `setup()` block** will cost at least 2 marks in any program-based question. > 5. **Skipping circuit description** (pin numbers, power connections) before writing the code costs 1–2 marks. --- #### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — LM35 + Arduino Interfacing **Q.A(a) [7 marks] — Draw the interfacing circuit of the LM35 with Arduino UNO and write a complete Arduino sketch to display the temperature in °C on the Serial Monitor every 1 second.** **Model Answer:** **Circuit Connections:** | LM35 Pin | Connects To | |----------|-------------| | Vcc (Pin 1) | Arduino **5V** | | Vout (Pin 2) | Arduino **A0** | | GND (Pin 3) | Arduino **GND** | **Arduino Sketch:** ```cpp /* Experiment 2: Interfacing LM35 Temperature Sensor with Arduino UNO Course: PCCGL607 - IoT Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme) Display: Temperature in degrees Celsius on Serial Monitor */ const int sensorPin = A0; // LM35 output connected to analog pin A0 float voltage; // Stores computed voltage in Volts float temperatureC; // Stores computed temperature in °C void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); // Initialize UART at 9600 bps Serial.println("LM35 Temperature Monitor Ready..."); } void loop() { int adcValue = analogRead(sensorPin); // Stage 3: Read 10-bit ADC integer voltage = adcValue * (5.0 / 1023.0); // Stage 4a: ADC → Voltage temperatureC = voltage * 100.0; // Stage 4b: Voltage → Temperature Serial.print("Temperature: "); // Stage 4c: Display result Serial.print(temperatureC, 2); // 2 decimal places Serial.println(" °C"); delay(1000); // Wait 1 second } ``` **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Circuit description with pin mapping — **2 marks** - Correct `setup()` and `loop()` structure — **1 mark** - Correct use of `analogRead()` — **1 mark** - Correct voltage conversion formula — **1 mark** - Correct temperature conversion formula — **1 mark** - Proper `Serial.print()` formatting and `delay()` — **1 mark** --- **Q.A(b) [7 marks] — Derive the mathematical expression to convert a 10-bit ADC reading into temperature in °C, when an LM35 is used with Arduino UNO (Vref = 5 V). Also calculate the temperature when the ADC reading is 153.** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - LM35 scale factor $k = 10 \text{ mV/°C} = 0.010 \text{ V/°C}$ - ADC resolution: 10 bits → $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$ levels - $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$ **Step 1 — Sensor equation:** $$ V_{out} = 0.010 \times T $$ **Step 2 — Rearranging for T:** $$ T = \frac{V_{out}}{0.010} = 100 \times V_{out} $$ **Step 3 — ADC equation:** $$ ADC = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023 $$ **Step 4 — Rearranging for Vout:** $$ V_{out} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023} $$ **Step 5 — Master formula (combining both):** $$ T = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref} \times 100}{1023} $$ **Step 6 — Substituting $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$:** $$ T = \frac{ADC \times 500}{1023} $$ **Step 7 — Plugging in $ADC = 153$:** $$ T = \frac{153 \times 500}{1023} = \frac{76500}{1023} \approx 74.78 \text{ °C} $$ **Verification:** $V_{out} = \frac{153 \times 5}{1023} = 0.7478 \text{ V} = 747.8 \text{ mV}$. Since $T = V_{out}(\text{in mV}) / 10$, we get $T = 747.8 / 10 = 74.78 \text{ °C}$ ✓ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Stating the given values clearly — **1 mark** - Writing the LM35 sensor equation — **1 mark** - Writing the ADC equation — **1 mark** - Deriving the master formula $T = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref} \times 100}{1023}$ — **2 marks** - Numerical substitution and final answer — **2 marks** --- #### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — DHT11 Alternative & Comparative Study **Q.B(a) [7 marks] — Explain the interfacing of a DHT11 temperature and humidity sensor with Arduino UNO. Draw the connection diagram and write a complete Arduino sketch using the DHT library to print both temperature (°C) and humidity (%) on the Serial Monitor.** **Model Answer:** **DHT11 Pin Configuration (front view, grid faces you):** | DHT11 Pin | Connects To | Notes | |-----------|-------------|-------| | Pin 1 (Vcc) | Arduino **5V** | Power supply | | Pin 2 (Data) | Arduino **Digital Pin 2** | Needs 10 kΩ pull-up to Vcc | | Pin 3 (NC) | Not connected | No connection | | Pin 4 (GND) | Arduino **GND** | Common ground | > A **10 kΩ pull-up resistor** must be connected between the Data pin and Vcc for reliable communication (single-wire bidirectional protocol). **Arduino Sketch (requires `DHT.h` library from Adafruit):** ```cpp #include "DHT.h" #define DHTPIN 2 // Data pin connected to D2 #define DHTTYPE DHT11 // Sensor type (use DHT22 for DHT22) DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE); // Create DHT object void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); Serial.println("DHT11 Weather Monitor Initialized"); dht.begin(); // Initialize the sensor } void loop() { float humidity = dht.readHumidity(); // Read %RH float temperature = dht.readTemperature(); // Read °C // Check if any reading failed if (isnan(humidity) || isnan(temperature)) { Serial.println("Sensor read FAILED! Check wiring."); return; } Serial.print("Humidity: "); Serial.print(humidity, 1); Serial.print(" % | "); Serial.print("Temperature: "); Serial.print(temperature, 1); Serial.println(" °C"); delay(2000); // DHT11 sampling rate is 1 Hz; wait 2s for safety } ``` **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Pin configuration + pull-up resistor mention — **2 marks** - `#include` and library usage — **1 mark** - `setup()` and `loop()` skeleton — **1 mark** - Reading humidity and temperature — **1 mark** - `isnan()` error check — **1 mark** - Serial output formatting — **1 mark** --- **Q.B(b) [7 marks] — Compare LM35, DHT11, and DS18B20 temperature sensors based on: (i) output type, (ii) temperature range, (iii) accuracy, (iv) interfacing complexity, and (v) additional features. Which one would you recommend for a KTU mini-project involving outdoor weather monitoring?** **Model Answer:** | Comparison Parameter | **LM35** | **DHT11** | **DS18B20** | |----------------------|----------|-----------|-------------| | (i) Output Type | Analog (10 mV/°C) | Digital (single-wire protocol) | Digital (1-Wire protocol) | | (ii) Temperature Range | −55 °C to +150 °C | 0 °C to +50 °C | −55 °C to +125 °C | | (iii) Accuracy | ±0.5 °C (typical) | ±2 °C (low precision) | ±0.5 °C (high precision) | | (iv) Interfacing Complexity | Very simple (1 ADC pin) | Moderate (needs library + pull-up) | Moderate (needs OneWire library + 4.7 kΩ pull-up) | | (v) Additional Features | None (temp only) | Also measures humidity | Programmable 9–12 bit resolution; unique 64-bit serial ID allows multiple sensors on one pin | | Power Consumption | < 60 µA | ~2.5 mA | ~1.5 mA (active), 750 nA (parasitic mode) | | Cost (approx., INR) | ₹40 – ₹60 | ₹80 – ₹120 | ₹100 – ₹150 | **Recommendation for KTU Outdoor Weather Mini-Project:** For an **outdoor weather station**, the **DHT11** is suitable as a *budget-friendly all-in-one* option (it measures both temperature and humidity in a single package). However, if **higher accuracy and wider range** are required (e.g., monitoring winter temperatures in Munnar hill stations where it can drop below 0 °C), the **DS18B20** is the best choice because: - It works down to −55 °C (DHT11 fails below 0 °C). - Multiple DS18B20 sensors can share a **single Arduino pin** (unique 64-bit ID addressing), making it scalable for distributed sensing. - It supports **parasitic power mode**, eliminating the need for a separate power wire. **Verdict:** **DS18B20** is recommended for serious KTU projects; **DHT11** is best for quick demos. **LM35** is preferred only when you specifically want to *learn ADC fundamentals* in the lab. **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Tabular comparison with all 5 parameters — **5 marks** (1 mark each) - Justified recommendation with engineering reasoning — **2 marks** --- ### 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **LM35 produces 10 mV per °C** — this is the *single most important fact* for the exam. - ✅ **Arduino UNO ADC is 10-bit**, giving **1024 levels** (0 to 1023), with **Vref = 5 V** by default. - ✅ **Master formula:** $T = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref} \times 100}{1023}$ — memorize this verbatim. - ✅ **Resolution at 5 V:** ~**4.88 mV** per LSB → **~0.488 °C** per LSB. - ✅ **Use `float` data type** in Arduino code to avoid integer-division truncation. - ✅ **Pin order of LM35 (TO-92, flat side facing you):** Vcc – Vout – GND (left to right). - ✅ **DHT11 requires a 10 kΩ pull-up resistor** on its DATA line; use the `DHT.h` library. - ✅ **DS18B20** uses the **1-Wire protocol** and supports **multiple sensors on one pin** via unique 64-bit ROM codes. - ✅ **Always include `delay(1000)`** in temperature loops to avoid flooding the Serial Monitor. - ✅ **Serial.begin(9600)** must be called inside `setup()`, and the Serial Monitor must be set to the same baud rate. - ✅ **Conversion factor of 100** comes from the reciprocal of 0.010 V/°C (i.e., $1 / 0.010 = 100$). - ✅ **Linear sensors (LM35) beat non-linear sensors (thermistors)** for embedded applications due to simpler code. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** If a question says "display on Serial Monitor," you get full marks **only if your code has `Serial.begin(9600)` and uses `Serial.print()` or `Serial.println()`**. Skipping the baud-rate initialization is a 1-mark loss. Always include the formula derivation **with units** — this separates an average answer from a high-scoring one!

topics.title: Interfacing Servo Motor with Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL607 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB USING ARDUINO
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing Servo Motor with Arduino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔹 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Servo Motor** is a closed-loop rotary actuator that uses position feedback to precisely control its angular displacement. In the context of the KTU IoT Lab (PCCGL607), interfacing a servo motor with an Arduino refers to the hardware and software integration required to drive a hobby-grade servo (commonly the **SG90** or **MG996R**) using the Arduino's **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** output capability and the built-in `Servo.h` library.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must demonstrate the ability to (i) wire a 3-wire servo to the Arduino, (ii) write a sketch using the `Servo` library, (iii) control shaft angle via `servo.write()`, and (iv) use external power when required.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Steering Wheel of Embedded Systems"

Imagine a car's **steering wheel**:
- You turn it slightly → wheels turn slightly.
- You turn it fully → wheels turn fully.
- The moment you let go, a spring/feedback mechanism keeps the wheel exactly where you left it.

A **servo motor behaves identically**: you send a short electrical "command pulse," and the internal potentiometer + motor automatically drive the shaft to a **specific angle** and **hold it there against external force**.

| Physical Concept | Servo Motor Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Driver's hand | Arduino PWM pin (control signal) |
| Steering wheel rotation | Servo shaft angle (0°–180°) |
| Road resistance / torque load | Load on the servo horn |
| Hydraulic power steering | External 5V/6V power supply |

## 1.3 Standard Servo Hardware Specs (Bolded for Memory)

- **Operating Voltage:** **4.8 V – 6.0 V DC**
- **PWM Frequency:** **50 Hz** (i.e., period **T = 20 ms**)
- **Rotation Range:** **0° to 180°**
- **Pulse Width Range:** **1.0 ms (0°) → 1.5 ms (90°) → 2.0 ms (180°)**
- **Standby Current (SG90):** **~10 mA**
- **Operating Torque (SG90):** **1.8 kg·cm**

> [!NOTE]
> The Arduino's standard `Servo` library automatically generates a **50 Hz** PWM signal with a **544 µs – 2400 µs** pulse-width range (slightly wider than the textbook 1–2 ms, giving a true ~0°–180° sweep).

## 1.4 The Three Wires of a Servo (Color Code)

| Wire Color | Function | Connect To |
|---|---|---|
| **Orange / Yellow** | Signal (PWM input) | Arduino digital/PWM pin |
| **Red** | VCC (+5 V) | 5 V rail (or external supply) |
| **Brown / Black** | Ground (GND) | GND rail |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔹 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Inside the Servo

A hobby servo contains **four internal sub-systems** working in a closed feedback loop:

1. **DC Motor** — Provides raw rotational energy.
2. **Gear Train** — Reduces speed, multiplies torque.
3. **Potentiometer** — Acts as the **position sensor** (rotates with the shaft, outputs a voltage proportional to angle).
4. **Control Circuit (Comparator + H-Bridge)** — Compares the input pulse width with the potentiometer voltage and drives the motor in the direction needed to **null the error**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a closed loop?** Without feedback, the motor would just spin freely. The potentiometer's voltage (representing current angle) is compared against the target angle encoded in the incoming pulse width. The motor moves *just enough* to make the two equal, then stops.

## 2.2 The PWM Control Signal — Heart of Servo Interfacing

Servos do **not** use PWM for *power* (unlike an LED dimmer). They use PWM purely as a **communication protocol** to encode a target angle.

### How the Pulse Encodes Angle:

```
Pulse Width vs Shaft Angle (Standard 50 Hz Servo)
                                                    
 |‾‾‾‾|         |‾‾‾‾‾‾|           |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|       
_|     |_________|      |__________|         |______
   1.0 ms (0°)   1.5 ms (90°)     2.0 ms (180°)
   
   |<---------------- 20 ms period ----------------->|
```

- The Arduino holds the line **HIGH** for *T_high* microseconds, then **LOW** for the remaining time.
- The servo measures *T_high* and rotates accordingly.
- Pulses must repeat every **20 ms (50 Hz)** — if the signal stops, the servo de-energizes and loses holding torque.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PWM Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | seconds (s) | For servos: $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ |
| 2 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{high}}{T} \times 100\%$ | % | Range ≈ **5 % – 10 %** for servos |
| 3 | Standard Period Value | $T = 20\,\text{ms}$ | ms | Fixed by servo protocol |
| 4 | Pulse-width range | $T_{high} \in [1000,\, 2000]\,\mu s$ | µs | 1.0 ms (0°) → 2.0 ms (180°) |
| 5 | Linear angle mapping | $\theta = \dfrac{(T_{high} - 1000)}{1000} \times 180°$ | degrees (°) | Linear interpolation |
| 6 | Inverse (angle → pulse) | $T_{high} = 1000 + \dfrac{\theta}{0.18}$ | µs | θ in degrees |
| 7 | Servo library range | $T_{high} \in [544,\, 2400]\,\mu s$ | µs | Arduino `Servo.h` default |
| 8 | Current per servo (typ.) | $I_{stall} \approx 100 - 650\,\text{mA}$ | mA | Depends on model & load |
| 9 | Arduino Uno 5V pin limit | $I_{max} \approx 400 - 500\,\text{mA}$ | mA | Use external supply for >1 servo |
| 10 | PWM-capable pins (Uno) | Pins **3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11** | — | Timer-based PWM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute-value pitfall avoided:** All table cells use `µs` (microseconds) instead of the `|` symbol to keep Markdown tables valid.

## 2.4 The Arduino `Servo` Library — Engineer's API

The Arduino IDE ships with a built-in `Servo` library that handles all low-level timer/PWM generation. The key function calls are:

| Function | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `servo.attach(pin)` | Binds servo to a PWM pin | `myservo.attach(9);` |
| `servo.write(angle)` | Rotates to angle (0–180°) | `myservo.write(90);` |
| `servo.writeMicroseconds(us)` | Direct pulse-width control | `myservo.writeMicroseconds(1500);` |
| `servo.read()` | Returns last commanded angle | `int a = myservo.read();` |
| `servo.attached()` | Checks if pin is bound | `if (myservo.attached())` |
| `servo.detach()` | Releases the pin | `myservo.detach();` |

## 2.5 Power-Supply Engineering (Often Asked in Lab Viva)

- A single SG90 can be powered from the Arduino's **5V pin** because its peak current (~250 mA) is within the onboard regulator's limit.
- For **multiple servos**, **high-torque servos (MG996R)**, or **stall conditions**, you **MUST** use an **external 5V/6V power supply**.
- **Rule:** Always connect the **GND of the external supply to the GND of the Arduino** (common ground) so that the signal reference is valid.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Robotics | Joint actuation (robotic arms, hexapods) |
| RC Vehicles | Steering, throttle, wing flaps |
| IoT | Smart door locks, automated curtains |
| Industrial Automation | Valve control, conveyor diverters |
| Biomedical | Prosthetic limbs, surgical-tool positioning |
| Aerospace | Drone gimbal stabilization |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔹 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Converting Pulse Width to Shaft Angle

**Given:** A servo receives a HIGH pulse of $T_{high} = 1750\,\mu s$ on a standard 50 Hz control line.

**To find:** The expected shaft angle $\theta$.

### Step 1 — Recall the boundary conditions

From the standard servo protocol:

$$
T_{high}(0°) = 1000\,\mu s, \qquad T_{high}(180°) = 2000\,\mu s
$$

### Step 2 — Set up the linear interpolation

Because the servo maps pulse width to angle **linearly**, the relationship has the form:

$$
\theta = m \cdot T_{high} + c
$$

### Step 3 — Determine the slope (m)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta T_{high}} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{180° - 0°}{2000\,\mu s - 1000\,\mu s} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{180°}{1000\,\mu s} \\[6pt]
  &= 0.18\,°/\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Determine the intercept (c)

Substitute $\theta = 0°$ and $T_{high} = 1000\,\mu s$ into $\theta = m T_{high} + c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= (0.18)(1000) + c \\[4pt]
0 &= 180 + c \\[4pt]
c &= -180
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Final general formula

$$
\boxed{\;\theta = 0.18 \cdot T_{high} - 180\;}
$$

### Step 6 — Plug in $T_{high} = 1750\,\mu s$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 0.18 \times 1750 - 180 \\[4pt]
       &= 315 - 180 \\[4pt]
       &= 135°
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The servo will rotate to **135°** (3/4 of its full sweep).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Converting a Desired Angle to Pulse Width

**Given:** We want the servo to point at $\theta = 45°$.

**To find:** Required $T_{high}$ in microseconds.

### Step 1 — Invert the linear mapping

$$
T_{high} = \dfrac{\theta + 180}{0.18}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $\theta = 45°$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{high} &= \dfrac{45 + 180}{0.18} \\[6pt]
         &= \dfrac{225}{0.18} \\[6pt]
         &= 1250\,\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Send a **1250 µs** pulse every 20 ms → the servo will hold at **45°**.

### Verification of the mapping

| Desired Angle $\theta$ | Pulse Width $T_{high}$ | Arduino Code |
|---|---|---|
| 0° | 1000 µs | `myservo.writeMicroseconds(1000);` |
| 45° | 1250 µs | `myservo.writeMicroseconds(1250);` |
| 90° | 1500 µs | `myservo.write(90);` |
| 135° | 1750 µs | `myservo.writeMicroseconds(1750);` |
| 180° | 2000 µs | `myservo.write(180);` |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Duty Cycle of the Servo Signal

**Given:** $T_{high} = 1500\,\mu s$, $T = 20\,000\,\mu s$.

### Step 1 — Apply the duty cycle formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{T_{high}}{T} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{1500}{20\,000} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
  &= 0.075 \times 100\% \\[6pt]
  &= 7.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** A servo signal is **always** in the **5 %–10 % duty-cycle band**, regardless of angle. This is the *first visual clue* to identify a servo control line on an oscilloscope.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Lab-Viva Calculations

| S.No | Given | Find | Formula Used | Answer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{high} = 1000\,\mu s$ | $\theta$ | $\theta = 0.18 T_{high} - 180$ | **0°** |
| 2 | $T_{high} = 1500\,\mu s$ | $\theta$ | Same | **90°** |
| 3 | $T_{high} = 2000\,\mu s$ | $\theta$ | Same | **180°** |
| 4 | $\theta = 90°$ | $T_{high}$ | $T_{high} = (\theta + 180)/0.18$ | **1500 µs** |
| 5 | $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ | $T$ | $T = 1/f$ | **20 ms** |
| 6 | $T_{high} = 1750\,\mu s$ | $D$ | $D = T_{high}/T \times 100\%$ | **8.75 %** |
| 7 | 3 servos, 250 mA each | Total current | $I_{total} = n \times I$ | **750 mA** (needs external supply) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔹 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hardware Interfacing Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps the **complete hardware architecture** for interfacing an SG90 servo to the Arduino Uno, including the optional external power path.

## 4.3 Program Flow Diagram (Mermaid) — `Sweep` Sketch

This is the **standard "Knob-less" sweep** program that scans the servo from 0° → 180° → 0°.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Power ON / Reset]) --> INCLUDE[Include Servo library and declare object]
    INCLUDE --> SETUP{setup function}
    SETUP --> ATTACH[Attach servo to Pin 9]
    ATTACH --> LOOP{loop function}
    LOOP --> SWEEP0[For pos = 0 to 180: write pos, delay 15ms]
    SWEEP0 --> SWEEP1[For pos = 180 down to 0: write pos, delay 15ms]
    SWEEP1 --> LOOP
    
    style START fill:#1B5E20,color:#fff
    style SETUP fill:#0D47A1,color:#fff
    style LOOP fill:#4A148C,color:#fff
    style SWEEP0 fill:#E65100,color:#fff
    style SWEEP1 fill:#E65100,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.4 Reference Arduino Sketch — Sweep (Verbatim KTU Lab Program)

```cpp
/*--------------------------------------------------------------
  Experiment 2: Interfacing Servo Motor with Arduino
  Course     : PCCGL607 - IoT Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)
  Hardware   : Arduino Uno, SG90 Servo, Breadboard, Jumper Wires
--------------------------------------------------------------*/
#include <Servo.h>      // Built-in library for servo control

Servo myservo;          // Create a Servo object

int servoPin = 9;       // PWM-capable pin
int pos = 0;            // Variable to hold the current angle

void setup() {
  myservo.attach(servoPin);  // Attach the servo on the chosen pin
  // No need to set the pinMode() - Servo library handles it
}

void loop() {
  // Sweep from 0° to 180° in steps of 1°
  for (pos = 0; pos <= 180; pos += 1) {
    myservo.write(pos);
    delay(15);               // Wait 15 ms for the servo to reach position
  }

  // Sweep back from 180° to 0°
  for (pos = 180; pos >= 0; pos -= 1) {
    myservo.write(pos);
    delay(15);
  }
}
```

---

## 4.5 Reference Arduino Sketch — Knob (Potentiometer Control)

```cpp
/*--------------------------------------------------------------
  Variation: Potentiometer-Controlled Servo
  Potentiometer on A0 drives servo position 0-180 deg
--------------------------------------------------------------*/
#include <Servo.h>

Servo myservo;
int potPin   = A0;        // Potentiometer wiper
int potValue = 0;         // 0-1023 from ADC
int angle    = 0;

void setup() {
  myservo.attach(9);
}

void loop() {
  potValue = analogRead(potPin);          // Read 0-1023
  angle    = map(potValue, 0, 1023, 0, 180);  // Scale to 0-180 deg
  myservo.write(angle);
  delay(15);
}
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔹 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the three wires of an SG90 servo motor and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An SG90 servo has **three wires**:
1. **Orange/Yellow (Signal):** Carries the 50 Hz PWM control pulse from the Arduino.
2. **Red (VCC):** Supplies DC power, typically **+5 V** (range 4.8 V – 6.0 V).
3. **Brown/Black (GND):** Common ground reference between the Arduino and the servo.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the standard PWM frequency and pulse-width range used to control a hobby servo motor? What shaft angle corresponds to a 1.5 ms pulse?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The standard **PWM frequency is 50 Hz**, i.e., a period of **20 ms**.
- The **pulse width varies from 1.0 ms to 2.0 ms**, corresponding to shaft angles of **0° to 180°** respectively.
- A **1.5 ms pulse** corresponds to the **center position, i.e., 90°**.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 🔸 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the internal construction and working principle of a hobby servo motor with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Interface an SG90 servo with Arduino Uno to perform a 0°–180° sweep. Write the complete Arduino sketch and connection details. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Internal Construction & Working Principle (7 marks)**

A hobby servo is a **closed-loop position-control device** consisting of four functional blocks:

| # | Block | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DC Motor** | Provides the raw rotational energy. |
| 2 | **Gear Train** | Reduces speed and amplifies torque. |
| 3 | **Potentiometer** | Acts as a position sensor; its wiper rotates with the shaft and produces a voltage proportional to current angle. |
| 4 | **Control Circuit** | Compares the input pulse width with the potentiometer voltage via an internal error amplifier; drives the motor through an H-bridge until the error is zero. |

**Working Principle:**
The external controller (Arduino) sends a 50 Hz PWM signal. The **pulse width $T_{high}$** represents the *target* angle. The control circuit decodes $T_{high}$ into a reference voltage and compares it with the potentiometer's *actual* angle voltage. The error signal drives the motor in the appropriate direction until both voltages match — at which point the shaft has reached the commanded angle and the motor stops. This **negative-feedback mechanism** gives the servo its ability to *hold* a position against external load.

**(b) Hardware Interfacing and Arduino Sketch (7 marks)**

**Connection Table (3 marks):**

| Servo Wire | Arduino Uno Pin |
|---|---|
| Orange (Signal) | Digital Pin **9** (PWM) |
| Red (VCC) | **5V** (or external +5V) |
| Brown (GND) | **GND** (common with external supply) |

**Arduino Sketch (4 marks):**

```cpp
#include <Servo.h>
Servo myservo;
int pos = 0;

void setup() {
  myservo.attach(9);
}

void loop() {
  for (pos = 0; pos <= 180; pos += 1) {
    myservo.write(pos);
    delay(15);
  }
  for (pos = 180; pos >= 0; pos -= 1) {
    myservo.write(pos);
    delay(15);
  }
}
```

The servo sweeps from 0° to 180° in 1° steps, waits 15 ms per step, then returns.

---

### 🔸 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the linear relationship between servo pulse width and shaft angle. A servo receives a 1750 µs pulse — find the expected angle. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a neat circuit, explain how a **potentiometer** can be used to control the angular position of a servo. Write the corresponding Arduino program. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Pulse-Width to Angle Derivation (7 marks)**

The servo maps pulse width to angle **linearly** between two known points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Point 1:} \quad & T_{high} = 1000\,\mu s \rightarrow \theta = 0° \\
\text{Point 2:} \quad & T_{high} = 2000\,\mu s \rightarrow \theta = 180°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Slope of the mapping line:**

$$
m = \dfrac{180° - 0°}{2000\,\mu s - 1000\,\mu s} = 0.18\,°/\mu s
$$

**Intercept (using Point 1):**

$$
c = 0 - (0.18)(1000) = -180
$$

**General formula:**

$$
\boxed{\;\theta = 0.18 \cdot T_{high} - 180\;}
$$

**Substituting $T_{high} = 1750\,\mu s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 0.18 \times 1750 - 180 \\
       &= 315 - 180 \\
       &= \mathbf{135°}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Potentiometer-Controlled Servo (7 marks)**

**Circuit (3 marks):**
- Connect a 10 kΩ potentiometer across Arduino's **5V** and **GND**.
- The **wiper (middle terminal)** goes to analog pin **A0**.
- Servo signal wire goes to PWM pin **9**; VCC to **5V**; GND to **GND**.

**Working:** The Arduino reads the potentiometer's wiper voltage (0–5 V) using `analogRead()`. The 10-bit ADC returns a value in the range **0–1023**. The `map()` function linearly scales this to a servo angle in the range **0–180°**, and `servo.write()` commands the shaft to that angle.

**Arduino Sketch (4 marks):**

```cpp
#include <Servo.h>

Servo myservo;
int potPin   = A0;
int potValue = 0;
int angle    = 0;

void setup() {
  myservo.attach(9);
}

void loop() {
  potValue = analogRead(potPin);
  angle    = map(potValue, 0, 1023, 0, 180);
  myservo.write(angle);
  delay(15);
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read This Carefully!**
> 1. **Wrong pin chosen** — Servo must go on a **PWM-capable** pin (3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11 on Uno). Students often use pin 2 or 7, which do not output 50 Hz servo-friendly PWM. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Missing `#include <Servo.h>`** — Without this, the sketch will not compile. **(-1 mark)**
> 3. **Calling `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` on the servo pin** — *Unnecessary* and may even cause issues. The library handles pin configuration internally. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Forgetting common ground** with external power supply → erratic servo jitter. **(-2 marks)**
> 5. **Pulse-width unit confusion** — Mistaking milliseconds for microseconds in derivation questions is the #1 reason for 0/7 in Part B. **(-7 marks)**

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Servo** = closed-loop actuator with internal motor + gear-train + potentiometer + control circuit.
- [x] **Three wires:** Orange (Signal), Red (VCC), Brown (GND).
- [x] **Control signal:** **50 Hz PWM** (T = **20 ms**) — *this is a communication protocol, not power control*.
- [x] **Pulse-width mapping:** **1.0 ms = 0°**, **1.5 ms = 90°**, **2.0 ms = 180°**.
- [x] **Linear formula (memorize):** $\theta = 0.18 \cdot T_{high} - 180$.
- [x] **Arduino library:** `#include <Servo.h>` → `attach()`, `write()`, `writeMicroseconds()`, `read()`, `detach()`.
- [x] **PWM pins on Uno:** **3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11** — always choose one for servo signal.
- [x] **Power rule:** Use Arduino's 5V pin for **one** small servo (SG90); use an **external 5V/2A supply** for multiple or high-torque servos — but **always tie grounds together**.
- [x] **Library pulse range:** `Servo.h` actually uses **544 µs – 2400 µs** by default, slightly wider than the textbook 1–2 ms (gives a fuller 0°–180° sweep).
- [x] **Sweep delay:** Always add a small `delay(15)` after each `write()` so the servo physically has time to reach the position.
- [x] **Potentiometer control:** Use `analogRead()` + `map(value, 0, 1023, 0, 180)` for analog-knob control.
- [x] **Library limit:** `Servo.h` supports up to **12 servos** on Arduino Uno (uses Timer1).
- [x] **Stall current** can be **5–10×** the running current — never power a high-torque servo from the Arduino 5V pin alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing Active Buzzer with Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL607 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB USING ARDUINO
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing Active Buzzer with Arduino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Active Buzzer** is an electro-acoustic transducer module that contains a built-in internal oscillator circuit, which generates a fixed audio frequency (typically **2 kHz to 5 kHz**) when a DC voltage is applied across its terminals. In the context of the KTU PCCGL607 IoT Lab syllabus, *interfacing* an active buzzer with an Arduino UNO refers to the systematic electrical connection of the buzzer module to a digital GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) pin of the ATmega328P microcontroller, followed by the design and upload of embedded firmware (Arduino sketch) that drives the buzzer ON/OFF to produce audible alert signals in response to sensor events or timed logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Active vs Passive Buzzer — KTU Syllabus Distinction**
> - **Active Buzzer**: Has a *built-in oscillating driver circuit* → produces sound the moment DC power is applied. Frequency is **non-tunable** by Arduino code.
> - **Passive Buzzer**: Lacks internal oscillator → requires a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) signal** of specific frequency from the Arduino `tone()` function. Frequency *is* tunable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **doorbell button** in your house. When you press the button, the doorbell circuit *automatically* plays a "Ding-Dong" sound at a fixed pitch. You do not need to hum the tune yourself — the doorbell's internal chip does the work. An **active buzzer behaves exactly like this doorbell**:
- The **Arduino digital pin** = the "button presser" (gives the GO signal).
- The **internal oscillator inside the active buzzer** = the doorbell chip (automatically produces the audio tone).
- The **5V DC supply** = the electrical pressure (current) needed to ring it.

So unlike a passive buzzer, where the Arduino must "hum the tune" using rapid ON/OFF switching (PWM), the active buzzer only needs a simple binary **HIGH (5V) or LOW (0V)** command from the Arduino.

## 1.3 Hardware Module Specifications (Standard KTU Lab Component)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Voltage | **3.3 V – 5 V DC** |
| Rated Current | **≤ 30 mA** (safe for direct Arduino drive) |
| Sound Output | **≥ 85 dB at 10 cm** |
| Resonant Frequency | **2 kHz – 5 kHz** (fixed, non-tunable) |
| Pinout | 3 pins: VCC, GND, I/O (Signal) |
| Arduino Pin Type | Any Digital GPIO (e.g., D8) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Viva Insight:** A 3-pin active buzzer module has an additional **I/O (Signal)** pin besides VCC and GND, which must be connected to the Arduino digital pin. A 2-pin buzzer has no signal pin — its sound is enabled as long as VCC and GND are powered. Always check the module type before wiring.

## 1.4 Block-Level Functional Overview

The interfacing task can be decomposed into **three functional subsystems**:

1. **Control Subsystem** → Arduino UNO (ATmega328P) running the sketch.
2. **Actuator Subsystem** → Active Buzzer Module (KY-012 / generic 3-pin).
3. **Power/Signal Path** → Jumper wires carrying 5V/GND/digital signal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — How the Active Buzzer Works Internally

The active buzzer module is a small **piezoelectric transducer system** wrapped around a CMOS-based multivibrator oscillator. When DC power is applied, the following sequence occurs:

- **Step 1 (Power ON):** VCC receives +5V from the Arduino. The internal transistor-based astable multivibrator begins free-running oscillation.
- **Step 2 (Frequency Generation):** The oscillator produces a square wave at a frequency determined by the RC (resistor-capacitor) timing components inside the buzzer. This frequency is **factory-fixed** and is typically around **2.73 kHz** for the common KY-012 module.
- **Step 3 (Piezo Vibration):** The square wave drives the piezo ceramic disc, which mechanically vibrates at the same frequency, creating pressure waves in the air — which we perceive as sound.
- **Step 4 (Arduino Control):** The Arduino simply switches the I/O pin **HIGH (5V)** to enable the oscillator, or **LOW (0V)** to disable it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "active" is easier for beginners:** Because the Arduino does not need to generate a precise frequency using `tone()`, students only need two `digitalWrite()` commands. This makes the active buzzer the *default choice* in KTU lab viva demonstrations and basic IoT alert systems.

## 2.2 Electrical Boundary Conditions (KTU High-Yield)

| Condition | Limit | Consequence if Exceeded |
|---|---|---|
| Arduino Digital Pin Source Current | **40 mA (absolute max)** | Pin damage / permanent failure |
| Arduino Digital Pin Recommended Current | **20 mA** | Safe operating limit |
| Active Buzzer Quiescent Current | **~10 mA** | Safe — no resistor needed |
| Buzzer VCC Voltage | **3.3 V – 5 V** | Below 3V: no sound; Above 5V: damage |
| Logic HIGH Threshold (ATmega328P) | **≥ 3.0 V** | Below: pin reads as LOW |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often connect a **passive buzzer** and try to drive it with `digitalWrite(HIGH)`. Result: a faint click instead of a tone. Always identify the module type *before* coding — if the buzzer has an onboard IC chip visible on the PCB, it is **active**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Ohm's Law | $V = I \cdot R$ | V in Volts, I in Amperes, R in Ohms |
| Electrical Power | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts — heat dissipated at the pin |
| Resistor for Current Limiting | $R = \dfrac{V_{source} - V_{buzzer}}{I_{safe}}$ | Ensures I < 40 mA |
| Frequency – Period Relation | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | f in Hz, T in seconds |
| Arduino `tone()` Frequency Range | $31 \text{ Hz} \leq f \leq 65535 \text{ Hz}$ | Only for *passive* buzzers |
| Sound Intensity (dB) | $L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² (reference) |
| ATmega328P Pin HIGH Voltage | $V_{OH} \geq 4.2 \text{ V}$ (typ.) | At 5V supply |
| ATmega328P Pin LOW Voltage | $V_{OL} \leq 0.5 \text{ V}$ (typ.) | At 5V supply |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Active buzzers are deployed in IoT systems as **auditory human-machine interface (HMI)** components — for example, in gas-leak detection alarms, intrusion alert systems, kitchen timers, and assistive notification devices for visually impaired users. They form the lowest-cost "audio output" layer of any edge-IoT node.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why a Series Resistor Is (Usually) Not Required for the Active Buzzer

A common viva question is: *"Why don't we need a current-limiting resistor in series with the active buzzer?"* Let us derive it step by step.

**Given (from datasheet of typical KY-012 active buzzer):**
- Buzzer rated voltage: $V_{buzzer} = 5 \text{ V}$
- Buzzer operating current: $I_{buzzer} = 25 \text{ mA}$ (typical)
- Arduino digital pin source current limit: $I_{max} = 40 \text{ mA}$ (absolute max)

**Step 1 — Compute internal resistance of the buzzer (treating it as a resistive load for steady-state DC):**

$$R_{buzzer} = \frac{V_{buzzer}}{I_{buzzer}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$R_{buzzer} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} = 200 \ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Arduino's safe current limit:**

$$I_{buzzer} = 25 \text{ mA} \quad < \quad I_{max} = 40 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

Since the steady-state current drawn by the buzzer (**25 mA**) is strictly less than the Arduino's absolute maximum pin current (**40 mA**), no series current-limiting resistor is mandatory. The system operates within the **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** of the ATmega328P pin driver transistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Optional Refinement:** If a student *wants* to extend buzzer lifespan or operate at the edge of the limit, an optional $100 \ \Omega$ series resistor can be added. The new current becomes:
>
> $$I_{new} = \frac{V_{source} - V_{buzzer}}{R_{series}} = \frac{5 - 5}{100} = 0 \text{ mA (drop)}$$
>
> In practice, the drop across the resistor is $V_R = I \cdot R = 25 \text{ mA} \times 100 \ \Omega = 2.5 \text{ V}$, lowering buzzer voltage to $5 - 2.5 = 2.5 \text{ V}$ — which is **below** the buzzer's 3V operating threshold, *killing* the sound. Hence, **do not add a series resistor for an active buzzer rated at 5V**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Time Delay Logic in `delay()` Function

The Arduino `delay(ms)` function pauses program execution for `ms` milliseconds. The relationship between milliseconds and frequency (for blinking/buzzing patterns) is:

$$T_{total} = T_{ON} + T_{OFF}$$

$$f_{pattern} = \frac{1}{T_{total}} \quad \text{(in Hz, where } T \text{ is in seconds)}$$

**Example:** For a buzzer beep with 500 ms ON and 500 ms OFF:

$$T_{total} = 500 \text{ ms} + 500 \text{ ms} = 1000 \text{ ms} = 1 \text{ s}$$

$$f_{pattern} = \frac{1}{1 \text{ s}} = 1 \text{ Hz} \quad \text{(1 beep per second)}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Active Buzzer Program Logic Flow

| Step | Action | Arduino Function / Construct | Timing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initialize digital pin as OUTPUT | `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT);` (in `setup()`) | Once at boot |
| 2 | Energize buzzer (sound ON) | `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH);` | Variable (e.g., 1000 ms) |
| 3 | Hold sound for duration | `delay(1000);` | 1000 ms |
| 4 | De-energize buzzer (sound OFF) | `digitalWrite(pin, LOW);` | Variable (e.g., 500 ms) |
| 5 | Hold silence for duration | `delay(500);` | 500 ms |
| 6 | Repeat indefinitely | `loop()` function auto-iterates | Continuous |

## 3.4 Reference Arduino Sketch (For Examiner's Reference Only)

```cpp
// PCCGL607 - Module Experiment 2
// Interfacing Active Buzzer with Arduino UNO

int buzzerPin = 8;   // Signal pin of active buzzer connected to D8

void setup() {
    pinMode(buzzerPin, OUTPUT);   // Configure D8 as OUTPUT
}

void loop() {
    digitalWrite(buzzerPin, HIGH);  // Turn buzzer ON
    delay(1000);                    // Beep for 1 second
    digitalWrite(buzzerPin, LOW);   // Turn buzzer OFF
    delay(500);                     // Silent for 0.5 second
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Record Format Note:** When writing the program in your lab record, always include a **header comment block** specifying: course code, experiment number, date, student name, and a one-line objective. This carries 1 mark in the KTU continuous evaluation rubric.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hardware Connection Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Arduino UNO Board] -->|5V from 5V pin| B[Active Buzzer Module]
    A -->|Digital Pin D8| B
    A -->|GND from GND pin| B
    B -->|Audible Tone 2 to 5 kHz| C[Sound Output to User]
    D[USB Cable from PC] -->|5V Supply and Code Upload| A
    E[Sensor or Switch Input] -.->|Optional Trigger| A
```

**Node Descriptions (Alpha-Compliant Identifiers):**
- **nodeA** → Arduino UNO — the central microcontroller host.
- **nodeB** → Active Buzzer Module — the actuator (KY-012 or equivalent).
- **nodeC** → Sound Output — the audible result reaching the user.
- **nodeD** → USB Cable — provides 5V supply and serial programming interface.
- **nodeE** → Optional Sensor/Switch — any input that *triggers* the beep (pushbutton, PIR, gas sensor).

## 4.2 Detailed Pin-to-Pin Wiring Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ArduinoUNO[ARDUINO UNO R3]
        pin5V[5V Pin]
        pinGND[GND Pin]
        pinD8[Digital Pin D8]
    end
    subgraph BuzzerModule[ACTIVE BUZZER MODULE]
        pinVCC[VCC]
        pinIO[I/O Signal]
        pinGND2[GND]
    end
    pin5V ---|Red Wire| pinVCC
    pinGND ---|Black Wire| pinGND2
    pinD8 ---|Yellow Wire| pinIO
```

## 4.3 Program Execution Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Power ON / Reset]) --> init[setup: pinMode D8 as OUTPUT]
    init --> loopstart[loop start]
    loopstart --> writeHigh[Digital Write D8 HIGH]
    writeHigh --> delayOn[Delay 1000 ms]
    delayOn --> writeLow[Digital Write D8 LOW]
    writeLow --> delayOff[Delay 500 ms]
    delayOff --> loopstart
```

**Execution Logic Explanation:**
1. **start** → ATmega328P boots, executes bootloader, then jumps to `main()`.
2. **init** → `setup()` runs **once**: `pinMode(8, OUTPUT)` configures D8.
3. **writeHigh** → `digitalWrite(8, HIGH)` sets D8 to +5V, energizing the buzzer.
4. **delayOn** → CPU halts for 1000 ms — the buzzer continues to sound.
5. **writeLow** → `digitalWrite(8, LOW)` pulls D8 to 0V, silencing the buzzer.
6. **delayOff** → CPU halts for 500 ms — silent interval.
7. **loopstart** → Control returns to top of `loop()` — pattern repeats forever.

## 4.4 Functional Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Application Logic - Beep Pattern]
    L2[Layer 2: Firmware Control - digitalWrite and delay]
    L3[Layer 3: Hardware Abstraction - ATmega328P GPIO Driver]
    L4[Layer 4: Electrical Interface - Jumper Wires and Breadboard]
    L5[Layer 5: Physical Actuator - Piezo Disc and Oscillator]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*Distinguish between an active buzzer and a passive buzzer. State one application where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer:**

An **active buzzer** contains a built-in oscillator circuit that produces a fixed audio frequency (typically 2–5 kHz) when DC power is applied. It only needs a binary HIGH/LOW signal from the Arduino. A **passive buzzer** lacks this internal oscillator and requires a PWM signal of a specific frequency, generated via the Arduino `tone()` function, to produce sound.

**Applications:**
- **Active Buzzer →** Preferred in simple alarm circuits (e.g., gas leakage alert, fire alarm) where only ON/OFF control is needed.
- **Passive Buzzer →** Preferred in musical projects, melody generation, or variable-tone user interfaces (e.g., playing the "Happy Birthday" tune).

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for application distinction.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*List the three pins of a standard 3-pin active buzzer module. Why is the I/O pin connected to a digital GPIO and not the 5V pin directly?*

**Model Answer:**

The three pins of a standard active buzzer module (e.g., KY-012) are:

1. **VCC** → Power supply input (+5V from Arduino).
2. **GND** → Common ground reference.
3. **I/O (Signal)** → Logic control input connected to a digital GPIO pin (e.g., D8).

**Reason for I/O connection (not direct 5V):**
If the 5V pin were connected directly to VCC and the I/O pin left floating, the buzzer would sound **continuously** the moment the Arduino is powered, with no way to programmatically silence it. By routing the I/O pin to a **digital GPIO** (D8), the Arduino can toggle it `HIGH` (sound ON) or `LOW` (sound OFF) under software control, enabling event-driven alerting, timed patterns, and conditional triggers based on sensor input.

*(1 mark for pin list, 2 marks for control rationale.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Design an Arduino-based system to interface an **active buzzer** on digital pin **D8** that produces a **SOS distress pattern** in Morse code: three short beeps (· · ·), three long beeps (— — —), and three short beeps (· · ·). Each short beep = **200 ms ON**; each long beep = **600 ms ON**; inter-element gap = **200 ms**; inter-letter gap = **600 ms**; inter-word gap = **1400 ms**. Write the complete Arduino sketch with proper comments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the circuit connection diagram between the Arduino UNO and the active buzzer module. Explain what will happen if you accidentally swap the VCC and GND connections during wiring. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

```cpp
// PCCGL607 - Experiment 2
// SOS Morse Code Pattern using Active Buzzer

int buzzer = 8;

void setup() {
    pinMode(buzzer, OUTPUT);
}

void beep(int duration) {
    digitalWrite(buzzer, HIGH);  // Buzzer ON
    delay(duration);             // Hold for given duration
    digitalWrite(buzzer, LOW);   // Buzzer OFF
}

void loop() {
    // Three short beeps: S = . . .
    beep(200);  delay(200);
    beep(200);  delay(200);
    beep(200);  delay(600);  // inter-letter gap

    // Three long beeps: O = - - -
    beep(600);  delay(200);
    beep(600);  delay(200);
    beep(600);  delay(600);  // inter-letter gap

    // Three short beeps: S = . . .
    beep(200);  delay(200);
    beep(200);  delay(200);
    beep(200);  delay(1400); // inter-word gap
}
```

**Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):**
- Correct pin declaration and `pinMode` initialization → **2 marks**
- Proper `beep()` helper function design → **2 marks**
- Correct sequence of `beep(200)` and `beep(600)` calls matching SOS pattern → **2 marks**
- Correct inter-element / inter-letter / inter-word delays → **1 mark**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Circuit Diagram (Verbal Schematic):**

```
   Arduino UNO              Active Buzzer Module
   +-----------+            +-----------------+
   |        5V |----------->| VCC             |
   |       GND |----------->| GND             |
   |        D8 |----------->| I/O (Signal)    |
   +-----------+            +-----------------+
        |                         |
        +----[USB to PC]----------+
              (Power + Programming)
```

**Effect of Swapping VCC and GND:**

If VCC and GND are accidentally interchanged, the buzzer module receives **-5V at its VCC pin** and **0V at its GND pin**, effectively applying a **reverse-polarity voltage** to the onboard CMOS oscillator IC and piezo disc. Consequences include:

1. **No sound output** — the oscillator IC is reverse-biased and fails to start.
2. **Possible permanent damage** — the internal CMOS transistor junctions can break down due to reverse breakdown, leading to a short-circuit that may also damage the Arduino's 5V regulator if current exceeds **500 mA** typical protection threshold.
3. **Heating of components** — the piezo disc and oscillator IC will quickly heat up due to abnormal current flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> - Drawing the circuit *without arrowheads or pin labels* (-2 marks)
> - Writing *"it will not work"* as the only effect of reverse polarity (-3 marks; you must explain the **physical mechanism**: reverse bias, CMOS breakdown, and Arduino regulator stress)
> - Forgetting the **inter-letter gap of 600 ms** in the SOS timing (-1 mark)
> - Using `delay(1000)` as a "default" delay instead of computing it from the specification (-2 marks)

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **internal functional architecture** of an active buzzer module (KY-012), identifying the role of each stage from DC power input to sound output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an Arduino sketch to interface an active buzzer on pin D9 that **beeps twice per second** for **10 seconds** and then remains silent. Use a `for` loop with a counter to implement the timing. Calculate the exact delay values required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Internal Architecture of Active Buzzer (Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Power Input VCC and GND] --> B[Reverse Polarity Protection Diode]
    B --> C[Voltage Regulator and Decoupling Capacitor]
    C --> D[Astable Multivibrator Oscillator]
    D --> E[Piezo Ceramic Disc Transducer]
    E --> F[Acoustic Horn and Sound Output]
    G[GND Reference] -.-> B
    G -.-> C
    G -.-> D
    G -.-> E
```

**Stage-by-Stage Explanation (7 Marks Distribution):**

1. **DC Power Input (VCC, GND) — 1 mark:** Receives +5V DC supply from the Arduino's 5V pin. Typical current draw is 10–30 mA.
2. **Reverse Polarity Protection Diode — 1 mark:** A series Schottky diode (e.g., 1N5817) prevents damage if the user accidentally swaps VCC and GND. The diode blocks reverse current.
3. **Voltage Regulator + Decoupling Capacitor — 1 mark:** A small LDO regulator (or Zener clamp) ensures the oscillator IC receives a stable voltage. The decoupling capacitor (typically 0.1 μF) filters high-frequency noise from the supply rail.
4. **Astable Multivibrator Oscillator — 2 marks:** The heart of the active buzzer. It is a free-running oscillator built using CMOS inverters (e.g., CD4069) or a dedicated timer IC. The frequency is set by an external RC network: $f = \dfrac{1}{1.4 \cdot R \cdot C}$, typically giving 2.73 kHz.
5. **Piezo Ceramic Disc — 1 mark:** A PZT (lead zirconate titanate) disc that mechanically flexes when a voltage is applied across it. At 2.73 kHz, the rapid flexing produces pressure waves in the air.
6. **Acoustic Horn / Resonator — 1 mark:** A small plastic enclosure amplifies the piezo vibrations and directs the sound outward, increasing the perceived loudness (typically 85 dB at 10 cm).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Calculation of Delay Values:**

Requirement: Beep **twice per second** for **10 seconds** → Total beeps = 20 beeps in 10 seconds.

Each beep cycle consists of **1 beep (ON)** + **1 silence (OFF)** = **1 cycle per second / 2 = 0.5 seconds per cycle**.

Therefore, per cycle:
- ON duration = 250 ms
- OFF duration = 250 ms
- Cycle time = 500 ms ✓ (verifies 2 cycles per second)

**Arduino Sketch:**

```cpp
// PCCGL607 - Experiment 2 (Alternative Question B)
// Active Buzzer: 2 beeps/sec for 10 seconds

int buzzer = 9;

void setup() {
    pinMode(buzzer, OUTPUT);
    Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
    for (int i = 0; i < 20; i++) {      // 20 beeps in 10 seconds
        digitalWrite(buzzer, HIGH);
        delay(250);                     // ON for 250 ms
        digitalWrite(buzzer, LOW);
        delay(250);                     // OFF for 250 ms
    }
    
    // After 10 seconds, remain silent forever
    while (true) {
        digitalWrite(buzzer, LOW);
    }
}
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct calculation of ON/OFF delays (250 ms each) from the 2 Hz specification → **3 marks**
- Correct use of `for` loop with counter = 20 → **2 marks**
- Correct infinite silent loop after 10 s → **1 mark**
- Proper comments and `pinMode` setup → **1 mark**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Question B(b):**
> - Using `delay(1000)` inside the loop without dividing — results in **1 beep per 2 seconds**, not 2 beeps per second. **(-3 marks)**
> - Using `for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++)` — should be 20, not 10. **(-2 marks)**
> - Forgetting `pinMode(buzzer, OUTPUT)` in `setup()`. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] An **active buzzer** has a built-in oscillator → only needs DC ON/OFF from Arduino, no `tone()` function.
- [x] A **passive buzzer** has no internal oscillator → requires `tone(pin, frequency)` to generate sound.
- [x] The **3 pins** of a standard active buzzer module are: **VCC, GND, I/O (Signal)**.
- [x] **No current-limiting resistor is required** when interfacing a 5V active buzzer to Arduino, because its quiescent current (~25 mA) is below the ATmega328P's 40 mA pin limit.
- [x] The Arduino digital pin's absolute maximum source current is **40 mA**; recommended safe limit is **20 mA**.
- [x] **Swapping VCC and GND** will damage the buzzer's internal CMOS IC and may stress the Arduino's 5V regulator.
- [x] For a **2-beeps-per-second** pattern, use `delay(250); delay(250);` inside a 20-iteration `for` loop (for 10-second total).
- [x] For Morse-code SOS: short = **200 ms**, long = **600 ms**, inter-element = **200 ms**, inter-letter = **600 ms**, inter-word = **1400 ms**.
- [x] The Arduino `delay()` function is **blocking** — it halts the CPU; for non-blocking patterns, use the `millis()` function.
- [x] Always add a **comment header** in your lab record code: course code, experiment number, objective, date, and student name.
- [x] **KTU Viva Quick-Fire:** *"Can an active buzzer play music?"* → **No**, because its frequency is fixed by the internal oscillator. Music requires a *passive* buzzer driven by `tone()`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In your lab exam, you may be asked to **modify** a given buzzer program — e.g., change the beep frequency, add a pushbutton trigger, or replace the active buzzer with a passive one. Always read the question's specification carefully and re-derive your delay values from first principles using $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing Relay with Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL607 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB USING ARDUINO
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing Relay with Arduino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Relay** is an *electromechanically operated switch* in which an electromagnetic coil, when energized by a low-power control signal, physically actuates a set of metallic contacts to open or close a high-power circuit. In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)** and **Arduino-based embedded systems**, a relay module acts as a **galvanically isolated power interface** that enables a low-voltage microcontroller (operating at **5 V DC** or **3.3 V DC**) to control high-voltage, high-current loads such as AC mains appliances, motors, solenoids, and incandescent lamps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCGL607 - Module 2):** Interfacing a single-channel or multi-channel electromechanical relay module with an Arduino Uno/Nano board to switch an external load (e.g., a bulb or DC motor) using a digital GPIO pin. Students are expected to demonstrate the *turn-ON* and *turn-OFF* action and verify isolation between the control and load circuits.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Push-Button Robot Arm"

Imagine you want to switch on a heavy-duty industrial ceiling fan, but you can only move your little finger. You cannot directly touch the high-voltage switch. So, you use a long, insulated robotic arm that responds to your finger tap.

- **Your finger tap** = The Arduino's tiny **5 V digital output pin** (max **40 mA**).
- **The robotic arm's motor** = The relay's **electromagnetic coil**.
- **The actual high-voltage switch** = The relay's **Common (COM), Normally Open (NO), and Normally Closed (NC)** mechanical contacts.
- **The robotic arm's power source** = A separate **external power supply** for the load (e.g., 230 V AC mains).

When your finger taps (Arduino sends `HIGH`), the robotic arm moves (coil energizes) and physically flips the switch (contacts change state), turning ON the fan. The fan's power never enters your finger — *complete electrical isolation* is achieved.

## 1.3 Standard Hardware Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Symbol/Unit | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Arduino GPIO High Voltage | $V_{OH}$ | **5.0 V** |
| Arduino GPIO Source Current | $I_{OH}$ | **40 mA** (max per pin) |
| Relay Coil Operating Voltage | $V_{coil}$ | **5 V DC** |
| Relay Coil Current | $I_{coil}$ | **70 – 100 mA** |
| Contact Rating (Resistive) | $I_{contact}$ | **10 A @ 250 V AC** or **10 A @ 30 V DC** |
| Operate Time | $t_{on}$ | **≤ 10 ms** |
| Release Time | $t_{off}$ | **≤ 5 ms** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the coil current matters:** The Arduino Uno can source only **40 mA** per pin, but a typical 5 V relay coil demands **70 – 100 mA**. This is precisely why we *never* drive a relay directly from an Arduino pin — we use a **transistor driver** or an **optocoupler-isolated module** to amplify the control signal safely.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw) Relay

An SPDT relay has **five terminals**:

1. **Coil Terminal 1** & **Coil Terminal 2** — Where the DC energizing voltage is applied.
2. **COM (Common)** — The pole that connects to *either* NO or NC depending on coil state.
3. **NO (Normally Open)** — Open circuit when the coil is de-energized; closes when energized.
4. **NC (Normally Closed)** — Closed circuit when the coil is de-energized; opens when energized.

### Operational Logic Truth Table

| Coil State (Arduino Pin) | COM ↔ NC | COM ↔ NO | Load Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| `LOW` (0 V) | **Closed** | Open | Load OFF (if wired to NO) |
| `HIGH` (5 V) | Open | **Closed** | Load ON (if wired to NO) |

## 2.2 The Transistor Driver Circuit — Why It Is Mandatory

A bare 5 V relay coil cannot be energized directly by an Arduino pin for three engineering reasons:

- **Current Mismatch:** Arduino pin max ≈ **40 mA**; coil demand ≈ **70 – 100 mA**.
- **Voltage Sag:** Driving a coil directly causes the ATmega328P supply rail to droop, causing the MCU to brown-out reset.
- **Back-EMF Destruction:** When the coil is de-energized, the collapsing magnetic field induces a reverse voltage spike of **100 V – 300 V**, which can instantly destroy the Arduino pin.

The **standard 2N2222A NPN BJT driver circuit** solves all three problems.

## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Use `\vert` for divisions to protect table syntax)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{CC} = V_{R_B} + V_{BE}$ | Kirchhoff's Voltage Law on the base loop | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $R_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{I_B}$ | Base current-limiting resistor | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 3 | $I_B = \dfrac{I_C}{\beta_{DC}}$ | Required base current for saturation | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | $I_C = I_{coil}$ | Collector current equals coil current | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $P_{coil} = V_{coil} \times I_{coil}$ | Continuous power dissipation of coil | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $V_{flyback} = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Induced reverse voltage on switch-off | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $R_{L,load} = \dfrac{V_{load}}{I_{load}}$ | Resistive load sizing | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 8 | $f_{PWM,max} \le \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot t_{on}}$ | Maximum safe PWM switching frequency | Hertz (Hz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The same driver topology is used in industrial PLCs, automotive ECUs, and smart-home hubs (Tuya, Sonoff). The **flyback diode** (typically **1N4007** for 5 V coils, or **UF4007** for fast-switching applications) is non-negotiable — it provides a freewheeling path that clamps the back-EMF to approximately **−0.7 V**, protecting the transistor from avalanche breakdown.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Relay Interfacing

- **Home Automation:** Switching 230 V AC lamp holders from an Alexa/Google Home command.
- **Industrial Control:** Motor starters, solenoid valves, and conveyor belt actuators in factories.
- **Agriculture:** Water-pump control in IoT-enabled drip irrigation systems.
- **Energy Management:** Load shedding in smart-grid IoT nodes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Design of the Base Resistor $R_B$

**Given Specifications (typical KTU lab relay module):**

- Arduino supply voltage: $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$
- Relay coil current: $I_C = I_{coil} = 100\text{ mA} = 0.1\text{ A}$
- Transistor (2N2222A) DC current gain: $\beta_{DC} = 100$ (typical, worst-case for saturation design)
- Base-emitter voltage drop: $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$ (silicon NPN)

**Step 1 — Calculate the required base current for saturation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B &= \frac{I_C}{\beta_{DC}} \\
    &= \frac{0.1 \text{ A}}{100} \\
    &= 0.001 \text{ A} \\
    &= 1 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We divide the collector current by the DC current gain because, in the *active region*, $I_C = \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B$. To guarantee the transistor enters **saturation** (fully ON, $V_{CE} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$), we must supply at least this much base current.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL on the base-emitter loop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CC} &= V_{R_B} + V_{BE} \\
V_{R_B} &= V_{CC} - V_{BE} \\
       &= 5 \text{ V} - 0.7 \text{ V} \\
       &= 4.3 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The Arduino pin sources $V_{CC}$ on the high side; this voltage drops across the resistor $R_B$ and the base-emitter junction in series.

**Step 3 — Compute the base resistor using Ohm's Law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_B &= \frac{V_{R_B}}{I_B} \\
    &= \frac{4.3 \text{ V}}{0.001 \text{ A}} \\
    &= 4300 \text{ }\Omega \\
    &= 4.3 \text{ k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Select a standard E12-series resistor (overdrive for saturation margin):**

Choose **$R_B = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$**. This forces $I_B \approx 4.3 \text{ mA}$, which is **4.3× the minimum** required — comfortably overdriving the transistor into hard saturation, ensuring lowest $V_{CE(sat)}$ and least power dissipation.

**Verification of final $I_B$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B^{actual} &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} \\
             &= \frac{5 - 0.7}{1000} \\
             &= 0.0043 \text{ A} = 4.3 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Justification

| Component | Chosen Value | Justification (Why this value?) |
|---|---|---|
| Transistor | **2N2222A** (NPN) | $I_C(max) = 800 \text{ mA}$; $V_{CEO} = 40 \text{ V}$; cheap, ubiquitous. |
| Base Resistor $R_B$ | **1 k$\Omega$** | Calculated as 4.3 k$\Omega$; derated to 1 k$\Omega$ for saturation margin. |
| Flyback Diode $D_1$ | **1N4007** | $I_F = 1 \text{ A}$ (≫ coil current), $V_R = 1000 \text{ V}$ (≫ back-EMF). |
| Relay Coil | **5 V DC, 100 mA** | Matches Arduino rail voltage; current within driver capability. |
| LED Indicator Resistor | **330 $\Omega$** | $(5\text{V} - 2\text{V}) / 330\Omega \approx 9 \text{ mA}$ — safe for standard LED. |
| GPIO Pin | **D7** (Digital) | General-purpose IO; no PWM required for simple ON/OFF. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Arduino Uno MCU] -->|Digital Pin D7 HIGH 5V| B[Base Resistor RB 1k Ohm]
    B --> C[NPN Transistor 2N2222A Base]
    C -->|Collector sinks 100mA| D[Relay Coil 5V DC]
    D --> E[Flyback Diode 1N4007 reverse biased]
    D --> F[Electromagnetic Field Generated]
    F --> G[Common Contact moves to NO]
    G -->|Closes circuit| H[AC Load 230V Bulb turns ON]
    H -->|Load current path| I[Neutral Line]

    J[Arduino sets D7 LOW 0V] --> K[Transistor OFF]
    K --> L[Coil de energises]
    L --> M[Spring returns COM to NC]
    M --> N[AC Load turns OFF]

    subgraph Control_Side
        A
        B
        C
    end

    subgraph Power_Side
        D
        E
        G
        H
    end
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Complete Wiring Pin Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Arduino_Uno
        A1[5V Pin] --- A2[GND Pin] --- A3[Digital Pin D7]
    end
