```mermaid
    subgraph Relay_Module
        R1[VCC Terminal] --- R2[GND Terminal] --- R3[IN Signal Pin]
        R4[COM Contact] --- R5[NO Contact] --- R6[NC Contact]
    end

    subgraph Load_Circuit
        L1[230V AC Live] --- L2[Bulb Filament] --- L3[Back to COM]
    end

    A1 -->|5V DC| R1
    A2 -->|Common Ground| R2
    A3 -->|Control Signal| R3
    L1 --> R4
    R5 --> L2
    L3 --> A2

    X[Flyback Diode 1N4007] -.->|Across coil| R1
    Y[Transistor Driver] -.->|Between IN and GND| R3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Operational State Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Coil_DeEnergised
    Coil_DeEnergised: Coil De-energised
    Coil_DeEnergised: COM connected to NC
    Coil_DeEnergised: Load OFF

    Coil_DeEnergised --> Coil_Energised : Arduino writes HIGH to D7
    Coil_Energised: Coil Energised
    Coil_Energised: Magnetic field pulls armature
    Coil_Energised: COM connects to NO
    Coil_Energised: Load ON

    Coil_Energised --> Coil_DeEnergised : Arduino writes LOW to D7
    Coil_DeEnergised --> [*]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define a relay. Why is a relay module preferred over a transistor for switching AC mains loads?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A relay is an **electromagnetic switch** that uses a low-power control signal to mechanically actuate contacts that switch a higher-power circuit. (1 mark)
It provides **galvanic isolation** between the low-voltage DC control side (Arduino at 5 V) and the high-voltage AC load side (230 V mains). (1 mark)
Unlike a transistor (which is limited by $V_{CE}$ breakdown and thermal dissipation for AC loads), a relay's mechanical contacts can switch AC or DC equally well and tolerate large inrush currents. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`
**What is the function of the flyback diode connected across the relay coil?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
When the relay coil is de-energized, the collapsing magnetic field induces a **high-voltage reverse spike** ($V = -L \cdot di/dt$) of 100–300 V across the coil. (1 mark)
This spike, if unclamped, would **avalanche-breakdown the driving transistor** or the Arduino pin. (1 mark)
The **1N4007 flyback diode**, connected in **reverse bias** across the coil, becomes forward-biased during the spike and provides a freewheeling current path, clamping the voltage to approximately **−0.7 V** and protecting the driver. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the interfacing of a 5 V SPDT relay module with an Arduino Uno to switch a 230 V AC bulb. Label the NO, NC, and COM terminals.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the value of the base resistor $R_B$ required to drive a relay coil of 5 V, 100 mA using a 2N2222A transistor (assume $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7 \text{ V}$). Justify the chosen E12-standard value.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) [7 marks] — Circuit Description & Pin Functions:**

The Arduino digital pin **D7** is connected to the **IN (signal) pin** of the relay module through a base resistor $R_B = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$. The transistor's emitter is tied to **GND**, and its collector drives one terminal of the **5 V relay coil**; the other coil terminal connects to the Arduino **5 V rail**. A **1N4007 flyback diode** is placed across the coil, cathode to 5 V and anode to collector.

For the AC load side: the **230 V Live wire** is connected to the relay's **COM** terminal, and the **bulb's one terminal** is connected to the **NO** terminal; the bulb's other terminal returns to the **Neutral** line. (3 marks for diagram; 2 marks for NO/NC/COM labelling; 2 marks for safe wiring explanation)

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Required base current:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B &= \frac{I_C}{\beta} = \frac{100 \text{ mA}}{100} = 1 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

Step 2 — Voltage drop across $R_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{R_B} &= V_{CC} - V_{BE} = 5 - 0.7 = 4.3 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

Step 3 — Resistor value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_B &= \frac{4.3 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ mA}} = 4.3 \text{ k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

Step 4 — Standard selection: A **1 k$\Omega$ E12 resistor** is chosen to provide a 4× overdrive margin, ensuring hard saturation of the transistor. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **electromechanical relays (EMR)** and **solid-state relays (SSR)**. Mention two advantages and two disadvantages of each in an IoT application.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write the complete Arduino sketch to blink a 230 V bulb connected via a relay on pin **D8** with a 1-second ON and 2-second OFF cycle. Explain each function call.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) [7 marks] — EMR vs SSR Comparison:**

| Parameter | Electromechanical Relay (EMR) | Solid-State Relay (SSR) |
|---|---|---|
| Switching Element | Moving metallic contacts | TRIAC / MOSFET / IGBT |
| Isolation | Galvanic (coil-contact) | Opto-isolated (LED-phototriac) |
| Lifetime | $10^5$ – $10^6$ operations | $10^9$ operations (no moving parts) |
| Switching Speed | 5–15 ms | < 1 ms |
| Audible Click | Yes | Silent |
| Leakage Current | Zero | 1–10 mA (off-state) |
| Cost | Low (₹40–80) | Higher (₹200–500) |

(2 marks for table, 2 marks for advantages each — 1 EMR advantage + 1 SSR advantage, 1 mark each × 2; 2 marks for disadvantages)

**EMR Advantages:** Low cost, zero off-state leakage.
**EMR Disadvantages:** Slow, contact bounce, mechanical wear.
**SSR Advantages:** Silent, fast, long life.
**SSR Disadvantages:** Generates heat, off-state leakage, more expensive.

**(b) [7 marks] — Arduino Sketch:**

```cpp
// Pin Assignment
const int relayPin = 8;

void setup() {
    pinMode(relayPin, OUTPUT);   // Configure D8 as output
}

void loop() {
    digitalWrite(relayPin, HIGH); // Energize coil -> bulb ON
    delay(1000);                  // Wait 1 second
    digitalWrite(relayPin, LOW);  // De-energize coil -> bulb OFF
    delay(2000);                  // Wait 2 seconds
}
```

**Explanation of each function:** (2 marks for sketch, 5 marks for explanation)
- `pinMode(relayPin, OUTPUT);` — Configures pin D8 as a digital output so the ATmega328P can drive it. (1 mark)
- `digitalWrite(relayPin, HIGH);` — Sets the pin to 5 V, saturating the transistor, energizing the relay coil, and closing the NO contact to switch ON the bulb. (1 mark)
- `digitalWrite(relayPin, LOW);` — Sets the pin to 0 V, switching off the transistor, releasing the armature, and turning the bulb OFF. (1 mark)
- `delay(1000);` and `delay(2000);` — Provide blocking time delays of 1 s and 2 s respectively to create the required blink pattern. (1 mark)
- `void setup()` runs once; `void loop()` runs infinitely. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never write the bulb's neutral directly to Arduino GND.** The AC mains neutral must return to the mains neutral rail, not the DC ground. Connecting them creates a **ground loop and shock hazard** — this is the #1 cause of board damage in KTU lab evaluations and will cost you **3 marks outright**.
> 2. **Forgetting the flyback diode** in the circuit diagram loses 1–2 marks, even if the rest of the answer is perfect.
> 3. **Writing `pinMode` with a number instead of a named constant** (e.g., `pinMode(8, OUTPUT)` without a comment) is acceptable but considered *non-idiomatic*; using `const int relayPin = 8;` demonstrates professional coding practice and earns full marks under KTU's "Code Quality" sub-criterion.
> 4. **Unit mismatches in numerical problems** (mA vs A, k$\Omega$ vs $\Omega$) lead to **automatic 1-mark deduction** in the final answer verification step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **relay** is an *electromechanically operated switch* that provides **galvanic isolation** between low-power control and high-power load circuits. *(High-Yield)*
- The **Arduino Uno GPIO pin can source only 40 mA**, which is **insufficient** to drive a 5 V relay coil (70–100 mA) — a **transistor driver** is mandatory. *(High-Yield)*
- The **flyback diode (1N4007)** is placed in **reverse bias** across the relay coil to suppress the back-EMF spike during switch-off. *(High-Yield)*
- The three contact terminals are **COM (Common), NO (Normally Open), and NC (Normally Closed)**. Wiring the load to **NO** gives "active-HIGH" logic. *(High-Yield)*
- The base resistor formula is: $R_B = (V_{CC} - V_{BE}) \cdot \beta / I_C$. A standard **1 k$\Omega$** is typically chosen to overdrive the transistor into **hard saturation**. *(High-Yield)*
- **SPDT** = Single Pole Double Throw (one input, two possible output paths). Used for switching between two loads. *(Medium-Yield)*
- **DPDT** = Double Pole Double Throw — used to reverse the polarity of a DC motor. *(Medium-Yield)*
- The **operate time** of a relay is typically **≤ 10 ms**, making it unsuitable for high-frequency PWM (>50 Hz) applications — use a **MOSFET** or **SSR** instead. *(High-Yield)*
- In KTU lab records, the **Arduino sketch must be included** along with the **circuit diagram** and a **photograph of the working hardware** to earn full marks. *(Exam Tip)*
- **Safety first:** Always disconnect AC mains before probing the relay contacts with a multimeter; the COM-NO terminals carry **230 V AC** when the relay is energized. *(Lab Safety)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Intrusion Detection System with Arduino and PIR Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGL607 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB USING ARDUINO > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Building Intrusion Detection System with Arduino and PIR Sensor # 📘 Module Experiment 2: Building Intrusion Detection System with Arduino and PIR Sensor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Intrusion Detection System (IDS):** A security framework that monitors a physical environment for unauthorized entry by analyzing sensor data in real-time and triggering an alarm/actuator response when an anomaly (motion/presence) is detected. In the context of IoT and the KTU PCCGL607 syllabus, an Intrusion Detection System built using Arduino and a **PIR (Passive Infrared) Sensor** is a fundamental embedded application that demonstrates the integration of **sensing**, **microcontroller-based decision-making**, and **actuation** within the IoT stack. > [!IMPORTANT] > **PIR Sensor (Passive Infrared Sensor):** An electronic sensor that measures infrared (IR) light radiating from objects in its field of view. It is termed "passive" because it does not emit any energy itself; it only detects the IR energy emitted by living beings and warm objects. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡 Imagine a **security guard sitting in a dark room** at the entrance of a bank. He cannot see (no active light emission), but he can **feel the heat** radiating from a person walking past him. The moment someone's body heat crosses his detection range, he raises the alarm. A **PIR sensor behaves exactly like this security guard**: - It does not "shine" any light/energy (passive). - It "feels" the infrared radiation (heat) emitted by the human body. - The moment a warm body moves across its detection zones, the sensor output flips state, which the Arduino interprets as an **intrusion event**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Detection Metrics for PIR (HC-SR501 Module):** > - **Operating Voltage:** **5V DC** > - **Detection Range:** **3 to 7 meters** (adjustable via onboard potentiometer) > - **Detection Angle:** **Up to 120°** > - **Output:** **Digital TTL (HIGH/LOW)** ### 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024 Curriculum This experiment is a cornerstone of the **IoT Lab (PCCGL607)** because it cohesively demonstrates: 1. **Perception Layer** (Sensing): PIR sensor as physical-world data collector. 2. **Network Layer**: Arduino Uno as the edge processor (mini IoT gateway). 3. **Application Layer**: Alarm (Buzzer) + Visual Indicator (LED) + Serial Monitor Logging. 4. **Real-world mapping**: Mirrors commercial burglar alarms, smart-home security systems, and motion-activated CCTV cameras. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physics Behind PIR: Pyroelectric Effect The heart of the PIR sensor is a **pyroelectric crystal** (typically Lithium Tantalate - LiTaO₃). The operating principle is based on three physics concepts: 1. **Blackbody Radiation:** All objects above absolute zero emit infrared radiation. The human body, being at ~310 K, emits IR strongly in the **8–14 µm wavelength band**. 2. **Pyroelectric Effect:** Certain crystals generate a temporary surface voltage when their temperature changes due to incident IR radiation. 3. **Fresnel Lens:** A segmented plastic lens on the sensor's front that divides the field of view into multiple detection zones. As a person walks, they cross these zones, causing alternating heating/cooling of the crystal and producing a fluctuating voltage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Fresnel Lens?** It amplifies the IR signal (by focusing it) and creates multiple alternating zones. A stationary person does NOT trigger the alarm — only **MOTION** causes the alternating zones to be crossed, creating a fluctuating signal. This is why PIR sensors detect *movement* of warm bodies, not static presence. ### 2.2 HC-SR501 PIR Sensor Pinout (KTU Exam Favorite 🔥) The HC-SR501 is the most commonly used PIR module in the KTU lab. It has **3 pins**: | Pin Name | Function | Connection to Arduino | |----------|----------|----------------------| | **VCC** | Power Supply (5V–20V DC) | Arduino **5V** pin | | **GND** | Ground Reference | Arduino **GND** pin | | **OUT** | Digital Output (TTL 3.3V) | Any **Digital I/O pin** (e.g., D2) | > [!NOTE] > The HC-SR501 has **two onboard potentiometers**: > - **Sx (Sensitivity):** Adjusts detection range (3m–7m). > - **Tx (Time Delay):** Adjusts how long the OUT pin stays HIGH after trigger (5s–5 min). It also has a **trigger mode jumper**: - **L (Single Trigger):** Output stays HIGH for the Tx time, then resets. - **H (Repeatable Trigger):** Output stays HIGH as long as motion is continuously detected. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Notes | |-----------|-----------------|-------|-------| | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Radiated Power) | $P = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A \cdot T^{4}$ | Watts (W) | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \mathrm{W/m^{2}K^{4}}$ | | Peak IR Wavelength (Wien's Law) | $\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}$ | meters (m) | $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{m \cdot K}$, for human $\lambda_{max} \approx 9.4 \ \mu m$ | | PIR Detection Range | $R_{max} = k \cdot \sqrt{P_{IR}}$ | meters (m) | Empirical; $k$ depends on Fresnel lens geometry | | Digital Output Logic | $V_{out} = HIGH \ \text{if motion detected}$ | Volts | TTL level (3.3V on HC-SR501) | | Arduino ADC Reference | $V_{ref} = 5.0 \ \mathrm{V}$ (default) | Volts | For any analog sensor variant | | Sensor Response Time | $\tau_{r} \approx 0.5 \ \mathrm{s}$ to $2 \ \mathrm{s}$ | seconds | HC-SR501 typical settling time | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL PIPING RULE:** In the table above, the absolute value of $x$ is represented as $\vert x \vert$, **NOT** $\vert x \vert$. The vertical pipe $\vert$ inside a markdown table cell would break the table parser. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX within tables. ### 2.4 Why PIR Over Other Motion Sensors? | Sensor Type | Active/Passive | Cost | Power Use | Best For | |-------------|----------------|------|-----------|----------| | **PIR** | Passive (IR detect) | Low | **Very Low** | Indoor human motion | | Ultrasonic | Active (sonar) | Medium | Medium | Distance measurement | | Microwave (Doppler) | Active (RF) | High | High | Through-wall detection | | Camera (CV) | Active (image) | Very High | Very High | Face/object recognition | **PIR wins** for KTU-style labs because it is **low-cost, low-power, simple digital interface, and ideal for human-body IR signature (~9.4 µm)**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of IR Peak Wavelength for Human Body This derivation explains **why** the PIR sensor's filter window is tuned to 8–14 µm. **Given:** - Human skin surface temperature: $T = 305 \ \mathrm{K}$ (approx 32°C) **Wien's Displacement Law** states that the peak wavelength of blackbody emission is inversely proportional to absolute temperature: $$ \lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T} $$ **Substituting the values:** $$ \lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{m \cdot K}}{305 \ \mathrm{K}} $$ $$ \lambda_{max} = 9.502 \times 10^{-6} \ \mathrm{m} $$ $$ \boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx 9.5 \ \mu m} $$ **Conversion Logic:** This result (~9.5 µm) falls perfectly within the 8–14 µm atmospheric IR transmission window, which is why PIR sensor modules use an **IR-pass filter** (often silicon-coated) that blocks visible light and other IR bands, but allows the human body's IR signature to pass through to the pyroelectric crystal. ### 3.2 Derivation of Trigger Logic (Boolean Decision Matrix) The Arduino processes the PIR output using a **state-machine logic**. Let $S(t)$ be the sensor state at time $t$, and $A(t)$ be the alarm state. **Logical Rule Set:** $$ S(t) = \begin{cases} HIGH, & \text{if } \Delta V_{pyro}(t) > V_{threshold} \\ LOW, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Alarm Activation Function:** $$ A(t) = \begin{cases} ON, & \text{if } S(t) = HIGH \\ OFF, & \text{if } S(t) = LOW \end{cases} $$ **With Debounce/Timer (Repeatable Trigger Mode):** $$ A(t) = ON \ \text{for} \ \Delta t \geq T_{delay} $$ where $T_{delay}$ is set by the onboard potentiometer (default ~5 seconds). ### 3.3 Detailed Analytical Step-by-Step (Arduino Processing Logic) **Step 1: Hardware Initialization** ```text Set pinMode(PIR_PIN, INPUT); Set pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT); Set pinMode(BUZZER_PIN, OUTPUT); Initialize Serial at 9600 baud. ``` **Step 2: Sensor Polling** The Arduino continuously reads the digital pin at the start of every loop iteration: $$ V_{read} = digitalRead(PIR\_PIN) $$ **Step 3: State Classification** $$ \text{State} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{INTRUSION}, & V_{read} = HIGH \\ \text{NORMAL}, & V_{read} = LOW \end{cases} $$ **Step 4: Actuation Decision** $$ \text{Action} = \begin{cases} \text{LED = HIGH; Buzzer = HIGH; print("⚠ INTRUSION!")}, & \text{if INTRUSION} \\ \text{LED = LOW; Buzzer = LOW; print("Zone Clear")}, & \text{if NORMAL} \end{cases} $$ **Step 5: Logging to Serial Monitor** The Arduino IDE's Serial Monitor acts as the **Application Layer** of the IoT stack, providing timestamped logs of every intrusion event for forensic analysis. ### 3.4 Complete Reference Arduino Sketch (Annotated) ```cpp // ===================================================== // KTU PCCGL607 - Experiment 2 // Intrusion Detection System using PIR + Arduino // ===================================================== int pirPin = 2; // PIR OUT connected to Digital Pin 2 int ledPin = 13; // Onboard LED indicator int buzzerPin = 8; // Active Buzzer unsigned long lastTriggerTime = 0; const unsigned long cooldown = 5000; // 5-second cooldown void setup() { pinMode(pirPin, INPUT); pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); pinMode(buzzerPin, OUTPUT); Serial.begin(9600); Serial.println("System Armed... Monitoring Zone..."); delay(2000); // PIR calibration warm-up time } void loop() { int pirState = digitalRead(pirPin); if (pirState == HIGH) { unsigned long now = millis(); // Debounce: only trigger once per cooldown window if (now - lastTriggerTime > cooldown) { Serial.println("⚠ INTRUSION DETECTED!"); digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH); digitalWrite(buzzerPin, HIGH); delay(500); // Beep duration digitalWrite(buzzerPin, LOW); lastTriggerTime = now; } } else { Serial.println("Zone Clear - No Motion"); digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW); digitalWrite(buzzerPin, LOW); } delay(300); // Polling interval (300 ms) } ``` ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Response Behavior Table | Condition | PIR OUT | LED | Buzzer | Serial Print | System State | |-----------|---------|-----|--------|--------------|--------------| | Initial power-on (calibration) | LOW | OFF | OFF | "System Armed" | **WARM-UP** | | No human in range | LOW | OFF | OFF | "Zone Clear" | **NORMAL** | | Human enters range | HIGH | ON | ON (500ms) | "INTRUSION DETECTED!" | **ALARM** | | Human stays stationary | HIGH | ON | OFF (cooldown) | (suppressed) | **ALARM (cooldown)** | | Human leaves after 5s | LOW | OFF | OFF | "Zone Clear" | **NORMAL** | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System Block-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeP[PIR Sensor
HC-SR501] -->|Digital Signal HIGH or LOW| nodeA[Arduino Uno
ATmega328P MCU] nodeA -->|GPIO D13 HIGH| nodeL[LED Indicator
Visual Alarm] nodeA -->|GPIO D8 HIGH| nodeB[Active Buzzer
Audible Alarm] nodeA -->|UART TX or USB| nodeS[Serial Monitor
Log Output] nodeA -->|Decision Logic| nodeT[Threshold Comparator
Inbuilt Code Logic] style nodeP fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeA fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeL fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style nodeB fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style nodeS fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style nodeT fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Circuit Wiring Diagram (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph POWER["Power Supply 5V DC"] VCC[5V Rail] GND[Ground Rail] end subgraph SENSOR["PIR Module HC-SR501"] P_VCC[VCC Pin] P_OUT[OUT Pin] P_GND[GND Pin] P_FRESNEL[Fresnel Lens
Multi-Zone IR Window] P_FRESNEL --> P_OUT end subgraph ARDUINO["Arduino Uno R3"] A_5V[5V Pin] A_GND[GND Pin] A_D2[Digital Pin 2] A_D8[Digital Pin 8] A_D13[Digital Pin 13] A_TX[USB / UART TX] end subgraph ACTUATORS["Output Actuators"] LED[LED Anode Pin 13] BZ[Buzzer Positive Pin 8] end VCC --> P_VCC GND --> P_GND P_VCC --> A_5V P_GND --> A_GND P_OUT --> A_D2 A_D13 --> LED A_D8 --> BZ A_TX --> SERIAL[Serial Monitor PC] style POWER fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333 style SENSOR fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#333 style ARDUINO fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333 style ACTUATORS fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#333 style SERIAL fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.3 Sequential Operation Flowchart (Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD ST[Start: Arduino Power ON] --> WAIT[Wait 2 seconds for PIR Calibration] WAIT --> ARM[System Armed: Print 'System Armed' to Serial] ARM --> LOOP{Read PIR Pin} LOOP -->|HIGH| ALARM[Turn ON LED and Buzzer] LOOP -->|LOW| CLEAR[Turn OFF LED and Buzzer] ALARM --> LOG1[Print 'INTRUSION DETECTED' on Serial] CLEAR --> LOG2[Print 'Zone Clear' on Serial] LOG1 --> DELAY1[Delay 300 ms] LOG2 --> DELAY1 DELAY1 --> LOOP style ST fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style WAIT fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,color:#000 style ARM fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,color:#fff style LOOP fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style ALARM fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style CLEAR fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style LOG1 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style LOG2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style DELAY1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 IoT Stack Mapping (KTU 2024 NEP Alignment) ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Perception Layer
PIR Sensor Fresnel Lens] --> L2[Network or Edge Layer
Arduino Uno MCU] L2 --> L3[Application Layer
Alarm and Serial Monitor] L1 -.IR Radiation.-> ENV[Physical World
Human Intruder] L3 -.Notification.-> USER[End User] style L1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333 style L2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333 style L3 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#333 style ENV fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#333 style USER fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#333 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]** **Define a PIR sensor. Why is it called 'passive'?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A PIR (Passive Infrared) sensor is an electronic device that detects infrared radiation emitted by objects in its field of view. It is called **"passive"** because it does not emit any form of energy (like ultrasonic or microwave sensors); instead, it merely *receives* IR radiation naturally emitted by warm objects such as the human body. The core component is a **pyroelectric crystal** that generates a voltage when its temperature changes due to incident IR radiation. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP Set B]** **List the three pins of the HC-SR501 PIR module and their functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The HC-SR501 has three pins: 1. **VCC** – Power supply input (5V–20V DC), connected to Arduino 5V. 2. **GND** – Ground reference, connected to Arduino GND. 3. **OUT** – Digital output (TTL HIGH/LOW), connected to any Arduino digital input pin (commonly D2). **(3 marks)** --- ### 📝 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *Design and explain an Intrusion Detection System using Arduino and a PIR sensor. Describe the working principle, circuit connections, and provide the complete Arduino sketch with proper comments.* **(a) [7 Marks] Working Principle and Circuit Diagram** **Step 1 – Sensor Physics:** A PIR sensor uses a **pyroelectric crystal** that generates a voltage when IR radiation strikes it. The HC-SR501 module contains a **Fresnel lens** that divides the detection area into multiple zones. As a human (body temp ~305 K, peak IR ~9.5 µm) moves across these zones, the crystal experiences alternating heating/cooling, producing a fluctuating voltage that is amplified and converted to a **digital HIGH** signal. **(3 marks)** **Step 2 – Circuit Connections:** | Component | Pin | Arduino Pin | |-----------|-----|-------------| | PIR VCC | Power | 5V | | PIR GND | Ground | GND | | PIR OUT | Signal | D2 | | LED Anode | Positive | D13 | | Buzzer | Signal | D8 | **(2 marks)** **Step 3 – Working Logic:** When the PIR OUT goes HIGH, Arduino detects an intrusion, switches ON the LED (visual alert) and the Buzzer (audible alert), and prints `"⚠ INTRUSION DETECTED!"` on the Serial Monitor. When the OUT pin returns LOW, all alarms are deactivated. **(2 marks)** **(b) [7 Marks] Complete Arduino Sketch** ```cpp int pirPin = 2; int ledPin = 13; int buzzerPin = 8; void setup() { pinMode(pirPin, INPUT); pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); pinMode(buzzerPin, OUTPUT); Serial.begin(9600); Serial.println("System Armed..."); delay(2000); } void loop() { int state = digitalRead(pirPin); if (state == HIGH) { Serial.println("⚠ INTRUSION DETECTED!"); digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH); digitalWrite(buzzerPin, HIGH); delay(1000); digitalWrite(buzzerPin, LOW); } else { Serial.println("Zone Clear"); digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW); digitalWrite(buzzerPin, LOW); } delay(300); } ``` **Explanation of Code:** - `setup()` configures pin modes and begins serial communication. A **2-second delay** allows PIR self-calibration. - `loop()` continuously polls `digitalRead(pirPin)`. - Conditional `if-else` triggers actuators and logs events. - `delay(300)` acts as a polling interval to avoid flooding the serial output. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024] (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** > *Explain the role of the Fresnel lens in a PIR sensor. How does it enable motion detection? Discuss the limitations of PIR sensors and how they can be overcome using sensor fusion.* **(a) [7 Marks] Fresnel Lens and Motion Detection** **Role of Fresnel Lens:** A Fresnel lens is a thin, lightweight, segmented lens mounted on the front of the PIR sensor. It performs **two critical functions**: 1. **IR Focusing:** It collects and focuses IR radiation from a wide field of view onto the small pyroelectric crystal, amplifying the weak IR signal. **(2 marks)** 2. **Multi-Zone Creation:** It divides the detection area into alternating zones (visible/invisible). As a moving object crosses zones, the crystal alternately heats and cools, generating a fluctuating electrical signal. **(3 marks)** 3. **Motion-Only Detection:** A *stationary* warm object does NOT cause zone-crossing, so no signal fluctuation occurs — the sensor ignores it. This makes PIR sensors inherently **motion-only detectors**. **(2 marks)** **(b) [7 Marks] Limitations and Sensor Fusion** **Limitations of PIR:** - Cannot detect **stationary** intrudous (only motion). **(2 marks)** - **False triggers** from sunlight, HVAC vents, or pets. **(1 mark)** - Limited **range** (typically 3–7 m). **(1 mark)** **Sensor Fusion Overcomes These:** By combining PIR with **complementary sensors**, robust security is achieved: - **PIR + Ultrasonic (HC-SR04):** Ultrasonic adds distance verification. - **PIR + Camera (ESP32-CAM):** Visual confirmation and image capture. - **PIR + Microwave (Doppler):** Detects motion through walls/obstacles. - **PIR + Reed Switch (Door):** Adds perimeter breach detection. The Arduino/MCU uses a **weighted decision algorithm**: $$ \text{Decision} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot S_i > \tau $$ where $w_i$ is the weight of sensor $i$, $S_i$ is its output, and $\tau$ is the alarm threshold. This fusion dramatically reduces false positives and enhances reliability. **(3 marks)** --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Lab Exams:** > > 1. **Forgot the 2-second calibration delay** after power-on. The PIR module needs ~30–60 seconds *minimum* (some require 60s) to stabilize. Drawing a circuit without showing this delay loses 1 mark. **(−1 mark)** > > 2. **Wrong pin declared:** Students often connect PIR OUT to an **analog pin** but use `digitalRead()`. While it works, marking schemes prefer digital pins explicitly labeled. Always state "Digital Pin D2". **(−0.5 mark)** > > 3. **No comments in code:** KTU evaluates code clarity. Missing comments = −1 to −2 marks. > > 4. **Confusing PIR with IR sensor:** PIR = motion sensor (digital); IR obstacle sensor = proximity (often analog/digital). Examiners WILL deduct marks for this confusion. **(−2 marks)** > > 5. **Missing GND connection:** Forgetting to connect PIR GND to Arduino GND is a circuit-deduction error. **(−1 mark)** > > 6. **Serial.begin() missing or wrong baud:** Most errors compile but produce garbled text on Serial Monitor. **(−0.5 mark)** > > 7. **No "void loop(){}" structure:** A common slip in viva — the loop function is mandatory; without it, code runs only once. --- ### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **PIR = Passive Infrared** — does **NOT** emit energy; only detects IR from warm bodies. - ✅ Core physics: **Pyroelectric effect** + **Fresnel lens multi-zone detection**. - ✅ Human body peak IR wavelength (Wien's Law): **λ_max ≈ 9.5 µm**. - ✅ HC-SR501 has **3 pins**: VCC, GND, OUT. - ✅ Output is **digital TTL**: HIGH = motion, LOW = no motion. - ✅ PIR detects **MOTION only** (not static presence). - ✅ **2-second minimum warm-up delay** needed after power-on in code. - ✅ Use `pinMode(pin, INPUT)` for PIR OUT pin in `setup()`. - ✅ Polling interval of **300 ms** is industry-standard for de-bouncing. - ✅ Arduino acts as **Edge Processor** in IoT stack; PIR = **Perception Layer**; LED + Buzzer = **Actuator Layer**; Serial Monitor = **Application Layer**. - ✅ Wien's Displacement constant: **b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K**. - ✅ Stefan-Boltzmann constant: **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴**. - ✅ Detection range HC-SR501: **3 to 7 meters** (adjustable). - ✅ Sensor fusion (PIR + Ultrasonic/Camera) overcomes single-sensor limitations. - ✅ Boolean logic in code: `if (digitalRead(pirPin) == HIGH) { ALARM } else { CLEAR }`. - ✅ Always include **Serial.println()** for log output — earns viva credit. ---

topics.title: Obstacle Detection with Distance Using Ultrasonic Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL607 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB USING ARDUINO
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Obstacle Detection with Distance Using Ultrasonic Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultrasonic Obstacle Detection System:** An embedded sensing system that employs a piezoelectric ultrasonic transducer (commonly the **HC-SR04** module) to emit a high-frequency acoustic pulse (**40 kHz**, well above the human audible range of **20 kHz**) and measures the round-trip time taken by the echo to return after reflecting from a target object. By computing half of this round-trip travel time against the velocity of sound in air, the system derives the absolute distance to the obstacle without physical contact, enabling non-intrusive spatial awareness in IoT edge nodes.

In the context of the KTU 2024 PCCGL607 syllabus, this experiment is classified under **"Sensor Interfacing and Actuator Control"** — a foundational learning outcome that maps directly to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Interface various sensors and actuators with Arduino/ESP development boards and develop IoT applications."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing inside a long, dark cave and you shout "Hello!". By listening carefully to the **echo** that bounces back from the cave wall, you instinctively estimate how far the wall is. If the echo returns almost instantly, the wall is close. If it takes a noticeable delay, the wall is far away.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bat Principle:** Bats do exactly this in nature using sound. They emit ultrasonic chirps and listen for the echo to hunt insects in total darkness. The **HC-SR04 sensor mimics this biological sonar principle** but does it with electronic precision.

- **You** = Arduino Uno (the brain)
- **Your shout** = **40 kHz** ultrasonic pulse from the *Trigger* pin
- **The cave wall** = Any physical obstacle (wall, object, person)
- **The echo you hear** = Returning pulse on the *Echo* pin
- **The time gap between shout and echo** = Round-trip time ($t$) used to calculate distance

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of sound in air (at 20°C) | **343 m/s** (≈ 0.0343 cm/µs) | Foundation of distance calculation |
| HC-SR04 operating frequency | **40 kHz** | Above human hearing threshold (**20 kHz**) |
| Operating voltage | **5V DC** | Compatible with Arduino Uno digital pins |
| Measurement range | **2 cm – 400 cm** | Valid operational envelope |
| Accuracy | **±3 mm** | Sufficient for robotic/IoT applications |
| Beam angle | **15°** | Cone of detection coverage |
| Trigger pulse width | **10 µs** | Minimum HIGH pulse to initiate sensing |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Always remember the magic number **0.0343 cm/µs** (or equivalently **29.1 µs/cm**). This single constant is the bridge between *time* and *distance* in every ultrasonic experiment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Six Logical Steps

The HC-SR04 sensor operates through a tightly synchronized sequence orchestrated by the Arduino. Each step is a discrete event that the firmware (sketch) must trigger and time precisely.

1. **Initialization Phase (Setup):** The Arduino configures the *Trig* pin as `OUTPUT` and the *Echo* pin as `INPUT`. Serial communication is initiated at **9600 baud** for live debugging.

2. **Trigger Pulse Generation:** The Arduino pulls the *Trig* pin HIGH for exactly **10 microseconds**, then pulls it LOW. This emulates a manual "clap" to wake the sensor module.

3. **Burst Emission:** Internally, the HC-SR04 fires **eight 40 kHz ultrasonic pulses** in a forward-facing cone. The piezoelectric transmitter crystal vibrates at this resonance frequency.

4. **Free Propagation:** The acoustic wavefront travels through air at the speed of sound (**v = 343 m/s**) until it strikes an obstacle and partially reflects (echo).

5. **Echo Reception:** The receiver crystal detects the returning wavefront and raises the *Echo* pin HIGH for a duration precisely equal to the **round-trip time of flight**.

6. **Computation & Output:** The Arduino uses the `pulseIn()` function to measure this HIGH duration, then applies the distance formula to convert time into centimeters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why divide by 2?** The measured time is the *round-trip* (sensor → obstacle → sensor). Since we only need the *one-way* distance, we must halve it. This is the single most common mistake in KTU lab exams — students forget the division by 2.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Constant | Mathematical Form | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-way distance | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | meters (m) | Core distance equation |
| 2 | Distance in cm (practical) | $d_{cm} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{58.2}$ | centimeters (cm) | Arduino-friendly shortcut |
| 3 | Distance in inches | $d_{in} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{148}$ | inches (in) | Imperial unit conversion |
| 4 | Speed of sound at 20°C | $v = 343 \, \text{m/s} = 0.0343 \, \text{cm/µs}$ | m/s, cm/µs | Conversion constant |
| 5 | Temperature compensation | $v = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T$ | m/s | Adjusts for air temperature (°C) |
| 6 | Trigger pulse width | $T_{trig} \geq 10 \, \mu s$ | µs | Minimum activation pulse |
| 7 | Measurement cycle | $T_{cycle} \geq 60 \, \text{ms}$ | ms | Recommended delay between readings |
| 8 | Maximum range (theoretical) | $d_{max} = \dfrac{v}{2 \cdot f_{pulse\_min}}$ | m | Limited by sensor hardware |

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation of the "58.2" divisor:**
> The round-trip distance covered in 1 µs = $2 \times 0.0343 = 0.0686$ cm.
> Therefore, time required for 1 cm round-trip = $\dfrac{1}{0.0686} \approx 14.55$ µs.
> For one-way 1 cm, we double this: $2 \times 14.55 \approx 29.1$ µs.
> But the standard Arduino shortcut uses $\dfrac{t}{58.2}$ where **58.2 = 2 × 29.1**, corresponding to *round-trip* time per cm. Always verify your code uses the correct divisor.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ultrasonic obstacle detection paradigm is foundational to:

- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs):** Wall-following and cliff-detection in domestic vacuum robots (e.g., Roomba).
- **Automotive Reverse Parking Assist:** Production cars use ultrasonic sensors in rear bumpers.
- **Smart Parking Systems:** IoT-enabled street parking that detects vehicle presence in slots.
- **Industrial Liquid Level Sensing:** Measuring tank fill levels without contact with corrosive fluids.
- **Drone Altitude Hold:** Quadcopters use downward-facing ultrasonic sensors for low-altitude stabilization.
- **Proximity-Activated IoT Devices:** Automatic doors, hand-sanitizer dispensers, smart dustbins.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Distance Equation from First Principles

This is the **high-yield derivation** that KTU examiners frequently test. Understand each transition, not just memorize the result.

**Given:**
- Speed of sound in air at standard conditions: $v = 343 \text{ m/s}$
- Round-trip time of flight measured by the sensor: $t$ (in seconds)
- Required: One-way distance $d$ from sensor to obstacle

**Step 1: Establish the kinematic relationship for the round-trip path.**

The acoustic pulse travels a total path length equal to *twice* the one-way distance (it must go to the obstacle AND return). The total distance covered is:

$$
d_{total} = 2d
$$

**Step 2: Apply the fundamental kinematic equation (distance = speed × time).**

For uniform motion in air (sound travels at a near-constant speed in a given medium):

$$
d_{total} = v \cdot t
$$

**Step 3: Equate the two expressions for total distance.**

$$
2d = v \cdot t
$$

**Step 4: Solve algebraically for the one-way distance $d$.**

$$
d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}
$$

This is the **fundamental equation** in SI units (meters, seconds, m/s).

**Step 5: Convert to centimeter-microsecond units for Arduino compatibility.**

The Arduino `pulseIn()` function returns time in **microseconds (µs)**. To work in centimeters:

- Convert $v$ from m/s to cm/µs: $v = 343 \text{ m/s} = 34300 \text{ cm/s} = 0.0343 \text{ cm/µs}$
- Substitute into the master equation:

$$
d_{cm} = \dfrac{0.0343 \cdot t_{\mu s}}{2} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{58.3}
$$

**Step 6: Round and verify the practical Arduino divisor.**

Using the more precise $v = 0.0344$ cm/µs:

$$
d_{cm} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{\dfrac{2}{0.0344}} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{58.14} \approx \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{58.2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Practical Form (memorize this):**
> $$d_{cm} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{58.2}$$
> where $t_{\mu s}$ is the value returned by Arduino's `pulseIn(EchoPin, HIGH)` function.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Question)

**Problem:** The `pulseIn()` function on the Echo pin returns a HIGH duration of **1750 µs**. An object is detected at room temperature (**20°C**). Calculate the distance to the obstacle in (a) centimeters and (b) meters.

**Part (a) — Distance in cm:**

$$
d_{cm} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{58.2}
$$

$$
d_{cm} = \dfrac{1750}{58.2}
$$

$$
d_{cm} \approx 30.07 \text{ cm}
$$

**Part (b) — Distance in meters:**

Convert centimeters to meters by dividing by 100:

$$
d_{m} = \dfrac{30.07}{100} = 0.3007 \text{ m}
$$

**Verification using the master SI equation:**

$$
d = \dfrac{343 \cdot 1750 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \dfrac{343 \cdot 0.00175}{2} = \dfrac{0.60025}{2} = 0.3001 \text{ m}
$$

The two methods agree (minor rounding error in 58.2 divisor). ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Scenario | Recommended Approach | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time obstacle avoidance (robot) | Use $\dfrac{t}{58.2}$ formula | Computationally lightweight for Arduino |
| High-precision industrial sensing | Use full $v$ with temperature compensation | Accounts for environmental drift |
| Far-range detection (>2 m) | Average multiple readings | Reduces acoustic noise variance |
| Detecting soft surfaces (cloth, foam) | Use lower frequency sensor or IR fallback | Sound absorption degrades echo strength |
| Confined space (tube, pipe) | Account for multipath reflections | False echoes can give erroneous readings |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid)

This diagram illustrates the complete signal and data flow from the physical world to the user display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Arduino Uno R3] -->|10 us Trig Pulse| B[HC-SR04 Trig Pin]
    B --> C[40 kHz Burst Emitter]
    C --> D[Air Medium Propagation]
    D --> E[Obstacle Surface]
    E -->|Reflected Echo| F[40 kHz Receiver]
    F --> G[HC-SR04 Echo Pin]
    G -->|HIGH duration = t_us| A
    A -->|d = t/58.2| H[Serial Monitor Display]
    A -->|Logic HIGH if d<threshold| I[Buzzer or LED Alert]
    A -->|AnalogWrite PWM| J[Servo Motor Sweep]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Pinout & Wiring Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ARDUINO_UNO[ARDUINO UNO R3]
        P5V[5V Pin]
        PGND[GND Pin]
        PD9[Digital Pin 9 - Trig OUTPUT]
        PD10[Digital Pin 10 - Echo INPUT]
    end
    subgraph HCSR04[HC-SR04 ULTRASONIC SENSOR]
        HVCC[VCC]
        HGND[GND]
        HTRIG[TRIG]
        HECHO[ECHO]
    end
    P5V --> HVCC
    PGND --> HGND
    PD9 --> HTRIG
    HECHO --> PD10
```

## 4.3 Sensor Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Trigger Input 10us] --> K2[Oscillator Circuit]
    K2 --> K3[40 kHz Piezo Transmitter]
    K3 --> K4[Acoustic Wavefront]
    K4 --> K5[Obstacle]
    K5 --> K6[Reflected Wavefront]
    K6 --> K7[40 kHz Piezo Receiver]
    K7 --> K8[Amplifier and Comparator]
    K8 --> K9[Echo Output Pin HIGH]
```

## 4.4 Firmware Execution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Setup] --> S1[Configure Trig as OUTPUT]
    S1 --> S2[Configure Echo as INPUT]
    S2 --> S3[Begin Serial at 9600 baud]
    S3 --> L0[Enter Loop]
    L0 --> L1[Set Trig LOW for 2 us]
    L1 --> L2[Set Trig HIGH for 10 us]
    L2 --> L3[Set Trig LOW]
    L3 --> L4[Call pulseIn Echo HIGH timeout 30000 us]
    L4 --> L5[Compute distance = t / 58.2]
    L5 --> L6{Is distance less than 20 cm}
    L6 -->|YES| L7[Activate Buzzer and Red LED]
    L6 -->|NO| L8[Deactivate Alert]
    L7 --> L9[Print to Serial Monitor]
    L8 --> L9
    L9 --> L10[Delay 100 ms]
    L10 --> L0
```

## 4.5 Reference Arduino Sketch (Tested for UNO R3)

```cpp
/*
 * Experiment: Obstacle Detection with Distance Using Ultrasonic Sensor
 * Board: Arduino Uno R3
 * Sensor: HC-SR04
 * Course: PCCGL607 - IoT Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)
 */

const int trigPin = 9;
const int echoPin = 10;
const int buzzer = 8;
const int ledRed = 7;

long duration;
float distanceCm;

void setup() {
  pinMode(trigPin, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(echoPin, INPUT);
  pinMode(buzzer, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(ledRed, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(9600);
  Serial.println("HC-SR04 Obstacle Detection Initialized...");
}

void loop() {
  // Step 1: Clear the trigger pin
  digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW);
  delayMicroseconds(2);

  // Step 2: Send the 10 us trigger pulse
  digitalWrite(trigPin, HIGH);
  delayMicroseconds(10);
  digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW);

  // Step 3: Read the echo pin; returns pulse duration in us
  duration = pulseIn(echoPin, HIGH, 30000);

  // Step 4: Convert duration to distance
  distanceCm = duration * 0.0343 / 2.0;

  // Step 5: Output via Serial
  Serial.print("Distance: ");
  Serial.print(distanceCm);
  Serial.println(" cm");

  // Step 6: Obstacle alert logic
  if (distanceCm > 0 && distanceCm < 20.0) {
    digitalWrite(ledRed, HIGH);
    digitalWrite(buzzer, HIGH);
    Serial.println("OBSTACLE DETECTED - ALERT!");
  } else {
    digitalWrite(ledRed, LOW);
    digitalWrite(buzzer, LOW);
  }

  delay(100);
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection Reference Table:**

| HC-SR04 Pin | Wire Color (typical) | Arduino Uno Pin |
|---|---|---|
| VCC | Red | **5V** |
| GND | Black | **GND** |
| TRIG | Yellow/Green | **D9** |
| ECHO | White/Blue | **D10** |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Q1. Define the term "Time of Flight" as applied to ultrasonic distance measurement. State the typical operating frequency of the HC-SR04 sensor. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Time of Flight (ToF) refers to the total time taken by an ultrasonic pulse to travel from the transmitter, strike an obstacle, and return to the receiver. The HC-SR04 operates at an ultrasonic frequency of **40 kHz**, which is well above the human audible threshold of 20 kHz, ensuring inaudibility to humans while maintaining excellent directional focus through a narrow **15°** beam cone. The ToF measurement enables non-contact distance calculation using the formula $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$.

---

### **Q2. Why is the measured time divided by 2 in the ultrasonic distance formula? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The HC-SR04 sensor measures the **round-trip** time — the acoustic wave must travel *from* the sensor to the obstacle *and back*. This means the measured time $t$ corresponds to the wave covering a distance of $2d$, not $d$. To extract the true one-way distance from sensor to obstacle, we must apply the relation $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$, where the factor of 2 converts round-trip distance into one-way distance. This division is the most critical step in the formula.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the Trigger and Echo pins in the measurement sequence.

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** An HC-SR04 sensor returns a `pulseIn()` value of **2328 µs**. Calculate the distance to the obstacle at **20°C** in (i) centimeters and (ii) meters. Show all derivation steps.

---

### **Model Answer — Q.A (a)**

**Working Principle:**

The HC-SR04 is a **non-contact** active ranging module operating on the **echolocation principle**. It uses a pair of piezoelectric transducers — one for transmission and one for reception — both tuned to **40 kHz**.

**Block Diagram Explanation:**

1. **Trigger Pin (Input to sensor):** The Arduino drives this pin HIGH for **≥ 10 µs**. This is the "start command" that tells the sensor's internal oscillator to fire a burst.
2. **Oscillator and Driver Circuit:** Generates **eight cycles of 40 kHz** electrical signal.
3. **Piezoelectric Transmitter:** Converts the electrical burst into an acoustic pressure wave.
4. **Air Medium:** The wave propagates spherically at **343 m/s** at 20°C.
5. **Obstacle:** Causes partial reflection of the wavefront.
6. **Piezoelectric Receiver:** Detects the weak returning echo and converts it back into an electrical signal.
7. **Amplifier and Comparator:** Boosts the tiny echo signal and triggers a clean digital pulse.
8. **Echo Pin (Output from sensor):** Goes HIGH for a duration exactly equal to the round-trip time of flight, then returns LOW.

**Role of Trigger Pin:** Acts as the **initiator** of one measurement cycle. Without a 10 µs HIGH pulse, the sensor remains idle.

**Role of Echo Pin:** Acts as the **messenger** of measurement data. The width of the HIGH pulse carries the time-of-flight information that the Arduino decodes using `pulseIn()`.

---

### **Model Answer — Q.A (b)**

**Given Data:**
- Measured pulse duration: $t = 2328 \, \mu s$
- Ambient temperature: $T = 20°C$
- Speed of sound: $v = 343 \, \text{m/s} = 0.0343 \, \text{cm/µs}$

**Step 1: Recall the fundamental distance equation.**

$$
d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values in SI units.**

$$
t = 2328 \, \mu s = 2328 \times 10^{-6} \, s
$$

$$
d = \dfrac{343 \cdot 2328 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \dfrac{0.798504}{2} = 0.399252 \, \text{m}
$$

**Part (i) — Distance in cm:**

$$
d_{cm} = 0.399252 \times 100 = 39.93 \, \text{cm}
$$

**Part (ii) — Distance in meters:**

$$
d_{m} = 0.399 \, \text{m} \approx 0.4 \, \text{m}
$$

**Verification using the Arduino shortcut:**

$$
d_{cm} = \dfrac{2328}{58.2} = 40.0 \, \text{cm} \quad \checkmark
$$

The minor difference (39.93 vs 40.0) arises from rounding of the 58.2 constant.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain how an HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor is interfaced with an Arduino Uno. List the required components and write the Arduino sketch to display the measured distance on the Serial Monitor.

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the limitations of ultrasonic sensors and compare them with infrared (IR) proximity sensors. Mention at least four real-world IoT applications where ultrasonic obstacle detection is preferred.

---

### **Model Answer — Q.B (a)**

**Required Components:**
- Arduino Uno R3 (1)
- HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor (1)
- Jumper wires (4)
- USB cable for power and Serial communication
- Breadboard (1)

**Circuit Connections:**

| HC-SR04 Pin | Arduino Uno Pin |
|---|---|
| VCC | 5V |
| GND | GND |
| TRIG | D9 |
| ECHO | D10 |

**Arduino Sketch:**

```cpp
const int trigPin = 9;
const int echoPin = 10;

long duration;
float distance;

void setup() {
  pinMode(trigPin, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(echoPin, INPUT);
  Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW);
  delayMicroseconds(2);
  digitalWrite(trigPin, HIGH);
  delayMicroseconds(10);
  digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW);

  duration = pulseIn(echoPin, HIGH);
  distance = duration * 0.0343 / 2;

  Serial.print("Distance: ");
  Serial.print(distance);
  Serial.println(" cm");

  delay(100);
}
```

**Working:** The sketch triggers the sensor, reads the echo pulse width, converts it to distance using the formula, and prints to the Serial Monitor every 100 ms.

---

### **Model Answer — Q.B (b)**

**Limitations of Ultrasonic Sensors:**

1. **Soft Surface Absorption:** Foam, fabric, and inclined surfaces absorb rather than reflect sound, causing detection failure.
2. **Temperature Sensitivity:** Speed of sound varies with temperature (~0.6 m/s per °C), introducing drift.
3. **Narrow Beam:** Cannot detect objects outside the **15°** cone.
4. **Cross-Sensitivity:** Multiple sensors operating nearby can interfere with each other's echoes.
5. **Slow Update Rate:** Sound's finite speed limits rapid scanning compared to light-based sensors.

**Ultrasonic vs. IR Proximity Sensor Comparison:**

| Parameter | Ultrasonic (HC-SR04) | IR Proximity (Sharp GP2Y0A21) |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing medium | Sound waves | Infrared light |
| Range | 2 – 400 cm | 10 – 80 cm |
| Accuracy | ±3 mm | ±1 cm |
| Soft object detection | Poor | Poor |
| Color/reflectivity dependence | None | High |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |
| Speed of response | Slower (ms) | Faster (µs) |

**Real-World IoT Applications:**

1. **Smart Dustbin:** Lid opens automatically when a hand approaches within 20 cm.
2. **Automatic Sanitizer Dispenser:** Touchless dispensing via proximity detection.
3. **Reverse Parking Alert in Vehicles:** Rear bumper sensors with audio feedback.
4. **Robotic Vacuum Cleaner:** Wall-following and cliff detection.
5. **Indoor Altitude Hold for Drones:** Stable low-altitude hovering.
6. **Water Tank Level Monitoring:** Non-contact measurement in overhead tanks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by 2** in the distance formula → 50% marks lost in numericals.
> 2. **Mixing up Trig and Echo pin directions** in the code (Trig=OUTPUT, Echo=INPUT) → full practical marks deducted.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion** of speed of sound (343 m/s vs 0.0343 cm/µs) → formula evaluation fails.
> 4. **No delay between trigger and echo measurement** → ghost readings from previous burst.
> 5. **Using `pulseIn` without timeout** → program hangs when no object is within range.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **HC-SR04** is a **40 kHz** ultrasonic ranging module with a 2–400 cm range and ±3 mm accuracy.
- [x] **Trigger pin** receives a **10 µs** HIGH pulse from Arduino to initiate one measurement cycle.
- [x] **Echo pin** outputs a HIGH pulse whose **width = round-trip time of flight** in microseconds.
- [x] **Speed of sound in air** at 20°C is **343 m/s = 0.0343 cm/µs**.
- [x] **Master formula:** $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ — never forget the division by 2.
- [x] **Arduino shortcut formula:** $d_{cm} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{58.2}$.
- [x] **Arduino functions used:** `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()`, `delayMicroseconds()`, `pulseIn()`.
- [x] **Wiring:** VCC→5V, GND→GND, TRIG→D9, ECHO→D10 (typical test configuration).
- [x] **Limitations:** soft surface absorption, temperature drift, narrow 15° beam, sensor cross-talk.
- [x] **Applications:** robotics, parking assist, level sensing, smart IoT devices, drones.
- [x] **Always include a `delay(100)` or longer** between successive measurements to prevent echo overlap.
- [x] **Always use a `timeout` in `pulseIn()`** to prevent code freezing when no obstacle is present.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Directional Control of DC Motor Using Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGL607 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB USING ARDUINO > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Directional Control of DC Motor Using Arduino ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from PCCGL607 Syllabus)** > "Directional Control of DC Motor using Arduino" is an IoT laboratory experiment in which the **polarity of the supply voltage** applied across the armature terminals of a **Brushed Permanent-Magnet DC (PMDC) motor** is electronically reversed using an **H-Bridge Motor Driver IC (typically L293D or L298N)**, which is in turn commanded by the **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) pins of an Arduino Uno** development board, thereby achieving **bidirectional (clockwise / anticlockwise) rotation** as well as **electrical braking and coasting** of the motor shaft. ### 1.1 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚂 Imagine a **railway track switching yard**. A single train (the DC motor) sits on a straight track. A railway signalman operates **two lever switches**: - If the **LEFT lever** is pulled, the train rolls towards the **left platform**. - If the **RIGHT lever** is pulled, the train rolls towards the **right platform**. - If **both levers are pulled (or neither is pulled)**, the train **stops at the platform**. In our experiment, the **train = DC motor**, the **signalman = Arduino Uno**, and the **two levers = two control inputs (IN1 & IN2)** of the **L293D H-Bridge IC**. The Arduino does not supply the high current needed to drive the motor directly — it merely "tells" the H-Bridge which way to route the heavy current coming from an external battery. > [!NOTE] > **Why can't Arduino drive the DC motor DIRECTLY?** > An Arduino GPIO pin can source/sink only **40 mA** (absolute maximum). Even a small toy DC motor draws **200 mA – 2 A**. Connecting the motor directly to the Arduino would **permanently destroy the ATmega328P microcontroller**. The L293D acts as a **current amplifier / buffer** between the low-power logic side (Arduino) and the high-power motor side (battery). ### 1.2 Apparatus / Hardware Components Required | # | Component | Specification | Function | |---|-----------|--------------|----------| | 1 | Arduino Uno | ATmega328P, 16 MHz | Logic controller | | 2 | L293D IC | Dual H-Bridge, 600 mA continuous | Motor driver | | 3 | DC Motor | 5 V – 12 V Brushed PMDC | Actuator | | 4 | External Battery | 9 V / 12 V DC | Motor power supply | | 5 | Breadboard | 830 tie-points | Prototyping | | 6 | Jumper Wires | M-M, M-F | Connections | | 7 | USB Cable | Type-B | Programming + 5 V logic supply | > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Exam Metric:** A 5 V or 12 V PMDC motor is conventionally used. The **L293D** can drive **two DC motors** simultaneously (M1 and M2) and is the most common IC in the KTU IoT Lab kit. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The H-Bridge Concept — The "Heart" of Directional Control An **H-Bridge** is a circuit consisting of **four switching elements** (transistors or MOSFETs) arranged in an "H" topology around the DC motor. The four switches are labelled **S1, S2, S3, S4** and the motor forms the cross-bar of the "H". **Why "H"?** If you draw the four switches at the four corners of a square and the motor connecting the two middle points, the resulting circuit literally looks like the capital letter **"H"** — with the motor as the horizontal bar. ### 2.2 H-Bridge Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) **Case 1 — Clockwise (Forward) Rotation:** - Close **S1 and S4**, keep **S2 and S3 open**. - Current path: $\;+\text{V}_{s} \rightarrow S1 \rightarrow \text{Motor}(+ \rightarrow -) \rightarrow S4 \rightarrow \text{GND}$ - Conventional current enters the **left** terminal of the motor and exits the **right** terminal. - Motor shaft rotates **Clockwise (CW)**. **Case 2 — Anticlockwise (Reverse) Rotation:** - Close **S2 and S3**, keep **S1 and S4 open**. - Current path: $\;+\text{V}_{s} \rightarrow S3 \rightarrow \text{Motor}(- \rightarrow +) \rightarrow S2 \rightarrow \text{GND}$ - Polarity across the motor is **reversed** → motor rotates **Anticlockwise (CCW)**. **Case 3 — Fast Brake (Active Stop):** - Close **S1 and S2 (or S3 and S4)** simultaneously. - Both motor terminals are shorted to either **+V** or **GND** → back-EMF damps rotation rapidly. - The motor **brakes** to a halt. **Case 4 — Coast (Free Run Stop):** - Open all four switches. - Motor terminals float → motor **coasts** to a stop due to friction. > [!WARNING] > **NEVER close S1 & S2 (top pair) together, or S3 & S4 (bottom pair) together.** This creates a direct **short circuit** across the power supply (a "shoot-through") and will **instantly destroy** the L293D IC. ### 2.3 L293D IC — Pin Architecture The **L293D** is a **16-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)** IC containing **two independent H-Bridges**. The KTU syllabus uses **one half** to drive a single DC motor. | Pin | Name | Type | Function | |-----|------|------|----------| | 1 | EN1,2 | Input | Enable pin for Motor 1 (logic HIGH = active) | | 2 | IN1 | Input | Control input 1 | | 3 | OUT1 | Output | Motor terminal A | | 4, 5 | GND | Power | Logic & motor ground (must be common) | | 6 | IN2 | Input | Control input 2 | | 7 | OUT2 | Output | Motor terminal B | | 8 | V_s (V+ Motor) | Power | Motor supply (4.5 V – 36 V) | | 9 | EN3,4 | Input | Enable for Motor 2 (not used here) | | 10, 11, 12, 13 | IN/OUT for Motor 2 | Mixed | Second H-Bridge (optional) | | 16 | V_cc (Logic) | Power | 5 V from Arduino | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (PWM & Motor Dynamics) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table — it is the most-tested numerical content in Module 2.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | PWM Duty Cycle | % | | 2 | $V_{avg} = D \times V_{s}$ | Average DC voltage applied to motor | V | | 3 | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{1}{T_{PWM}}$ | PWM frequency (Arduino default ≈ 490 Hz on D3,D9) | Hz | | 4 | $V_{EMF} = K_{e} \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF of motor (opposes applied voltage) | V | | 5 | $N = N_{0}\left(1 - \dfrac{I_a \cdot R_a}{V_{s}}\right)$ | No-load to loaded speed drop | RPM | | 6 | $\tau = K_{t} \cdot I_a$ | Torque produced by armature current | N·m | | 7 | $P_{out} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical output power | W | | 8 | $P_{cu} = I_a^{2} \cdot R_a$ | Copper (heating) loss in armature | W | Where: - $D$ = Duty Cycle (0 to 1, or 0 % to 100 %) - $V_s$ = Supply voltage from battery to L293D Pin 8 - $K_e$ = Back-EMF constant (V·s/rad) - $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s) - $K_t$ = Torque constant (N·m/A) - $I_a$ = Armature current (A) - $R_a$ = Armature resistance ($\Omega$) ### 2.5 Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > H-Bridge directional control is the **fundamental building block** of: > - 🤖 **Robotics** (wheeled mobile robot differential drive) > - 🏭 **CNC Machines & 3D Printers** (stepper + DC servo drives) > - 🚗 **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** (traction motor inverter) > - 🪟 **Smart Home Automation** (motorized curtains, blinds, smart locks) > - 🏥 **Medical Devices** (infusion pumps, surgical robots) --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Average Voltage Across a DC Motor Driven by PWM When the Arduino generates a PWM signal on the **ENABLE (EN1)** pin of the L293D, the effective DC voltage seen by the motor is **not** the peak supply, but an **averaged value** that depends on the duty cycle. **Starting Point:** A PWM waveform is a periodic rectangular pulse train. Over one period $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$, the signal is at $V_s$ for duration $T_{on}$ and at $0\text{ V}$ for duration $T_{off}$. **Step 1 — Define the instantaneous voltage function:** $$v(t) = \begin{cases} V_{s}, & 0 \leq t \leq T_{on} \\ 0, & T_{on} < t \leq T_{on} + T_{off} \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the average value over one full period:** $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t)\,dt$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the piecewise definition:** $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\left[\int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_{s}\,dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0\,dt\right]$$ **Step 4 — The second integral vanishes, leaving:** $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\left[V_{s} \cdot T_{on} + 0\right] = V_{s} \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$ **Step 5 — Recognize the ratio $T_{on}/T$ as the duty cycle $D$:** $$\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{s}}$$ This is the **foundational equation** of PWM motor speed control. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem Statement (Model):** A 12 V DC motor is driven by an L293D whose EN1 pin receives a PWM signal from Arduino Pin 9. The PWM value written is `analogWrite(9, 153)`. Calculate: 1. The duty cycle $D$ 2. The average voltage applied to the motor 3. The motor's no-load speed if its rated speed at 12 V is **3000 RPM** **Step 1 — Convert the Arduino analogWrite value to duty cycle:** Arduino's `analogWrite()` accepts an integer from **0 to 255**, where 255 corresponds to 100 % duty cycle. $$D = \frac{153}{255} = 0.6 = 60\%$$ **Step 2 — Apply the $V_{avg}$ formula:** $$V_{avg} = 0.6 \times 12\text{ V} = 7.2\text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Assume linear speed–voltage relationship and find the speed:** The no-load speed of a PMDC motor is approximately proportional to the applied voltage (neglecting brush drop and friction at low loads). $$N_{actual} = N_{rated} \times \frac{V_{avg}}{V_{s}} = 3000 \times \frac{7.2}{12}$$ $$N_{actual} = 3000 \times 0.6 = 1800\text{ RPM}$$ **Final Answer:** $D = 60\%$, $V_{avg} = 7.2$ V, $N_{actual} = 1800$ RPM. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — H-Bridge Truth Table (L293D Logic) | IN1 (Pin 2) | IN2 (Pin 7) | EN1 (Pin 1) | Motor Action | Function | |:-----------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:-------------|:---------| | HIGH | LOW | HIGH | CW Rotation | Forward | | LOW | HIGH | HIGH | CCW Rotation | Reverse | | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | Brake (fast) | Active stop | | LOW | LOW | HIGH | Coast (free) | Passive stop | | X | X | LOW | Motor disabled | Hi-Z (tri-stated) | > **'X' = Don't care (logic level irrelevant)** ### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Arduino Program Logic Flow The following pseudocode expresses the **algorithmic intent** before the actual C++ sketch is written: ``` BEGIN program "DC_Motor_Direction_Control" // --- Pin Assignment Phase --- DECLARE motorPin1 = Digital Pin 9 DECLARE motorPin2 = Digital Pin 10 DECLARE enablePin = Digital Pin 11 DECLARE motorSpeed = 200 // out of 255 (≈ 78% duty) // --- Initialization Phase (setup) --- CONFIGURE motorPin1, motorPin2, enablePin as OUTPUT INITIALIZE Serial at 9600 baud // --- Infinite Control Loop (loop) --- REPEAT FOREVER: // (1) Clockwise phase for 3 seconds WRITE HIGH to motorPin1 WRITE LOW to motorPin2 WRITE motorSpeed to enablePin PRINT "Motor: CLOCKWISE" to Serial WAIT 3000 milliseconds // (2) Brake / Stop for 1 second WRITE HIGH to motorPin1 WRITE HIGH to motorPin2 WRITE 0 to enablePin PRINT "Motor: STOPPED" to Serial WAIT 1000 milliseconds // (3) Anticlockwise phase for 3 seconds WRITE LOW to motorPin1 WRITE HIGH to motorPin2 WRITE motorSpeed to enablePin PRINT "Motor: ANTICLOCKWISE" to Serial WAIT 3000 milliseconds // (4) Coast / Stop for 1 second WRITE LOW to motorPin1 WRITE LOW to motorPin2 WRITE 0 to enablePin PRINT "Motor: COAST" to Serial WAIT 1000 milliseconds END REPEAT END program ``` ### 3.5 Complete Arduino Sketch (C++) ```cpp /* ---------------------------------------------------------- * Experiment: Directional Control of DC Motor Using Arduino * Course: PCCGL607 - IoT Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme) * Module: 2 * ---------------------------------------------------------- */ const int IN1 = 9; // L293D Pin 2 (Input 1) const int IN2 = 10; // L293D Pin 7 (Input 2) const int EN1 = 11; // L293D Pin 1 (Enable 1) -- PWM capable const int SPEED = 200; // 0–255 (≈ 78% duty) void setup() { pinMode(IN1, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN2, OUTPUT); pinMode(EN1, OUTPUT); Serial.begin(9600); Serial.println("DC Motor Direction Control - Initialized"); } void loop() { // ---- Step 1: Clockwise (Forward) ---- digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(EN1, SPEED); Serial.println("Direction: CLOCKWISE"); delay(3000); // ---- Step 2: Active Brake ---- digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, HIGH); analogWrite(EN1, 0); Serial.println("State: BRAKE"); delay(1000); // ---- Step 3: Anticlockwise (Reverse) ---- digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, HIGH); analogWrite(EN1, SPEED); Serial.println("Direction: ANTICLOCKWISE"); delay(3000); // ---- Step 4: Coast (Free stop) ---- digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(EN1, 0); Serial.println("State: COAST"); delay(1000); } ``` > [!NOTE] > Pin **11** is used (not 9) for the Enable because on the Arduino Uno, **Timer-2** PWM pins (D3, D11) give a smoother 490 Hz signal with less audible whine than the 980 Hz default on D9/D10. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (H-Bridge + Arduino System) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Arduino Uno
ATmega328P] -->|Digital Pin 9
IN1| B1[L293D
H-Bridge IC] A2[Arduino Uno
ATmega328P] -->|Digital Pin 10
IN2| B1 A3[Arduino Uno
ATmega328P] -->|PWM Pin 11
EN1| B1 B1 -->|OUT1 OUT2| C[DC Motor
5V to 12V] D[External Battery
9V or 12V] -->|V_s to Pin 8| B1 D -->|Common GND| B1 B1 -->|GND Pins 4 5| E[Common Ground Rail] A -->|GND Pin| E D -->|Negative Terminal| E E --> F[0V Reference] classDef ctrl fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef driver fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef motor fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef power fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 class A,A2,A3 ctrl class B1 driver class C motor class D,E,F power ``` ### 4.2 H-Bridge Internal Switching Logic Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Arduino Command Received]) --> check1{IN1 Level} check1 -->|HIGH| check2{IN2 Level} check1 -->|LOW| check3{IN2 Level} check2 -->|LOW| cw[Close S1 and S4
Current flows left to right
Motor rotates CW] check2 -->|HIGH| brake[Close S1 and S2
Both terminals tied to V_s
Motor BRAKES] check3 -->|HIGH| ccw[Close S2 and S3
Current flows right to left
Motor rotates CCW] check3 -->|LOW| coast[All switches OPEN
Motor terminals float
Motor COASTS to stop] cw --> output[Motor Shaft Turns Clockwise] ccw --> output2[Motor Shaft Turns Anticlockwise] brake --> output3[Motor Brakes Rapidly] coast --> output4[Motor Slows to Natural Stop] classDef logic fill:#e7e6ff,stroke:#5b5bbf,color:#000 classDef action fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 class check1,check2,check3 logic class cw,ccw,brake,coast action class output,output2,output3,output4 outputNode ``` ### 4.3 Pin Connection Wiring Matrix (Circuit Schematic in Tabular Form) | L293D Pin | Signal | Connects To | Wire Colour (Suggested) | |:---------:|:------:|:-----------:|:-----------------------:| | 1 (EN1,2) | Enable | Arduino D11 (PWM) | Yellow | | 2 (IN1) | Direction input 1 | Arduino D9 | Orange | | 3 (OUT1) | Motor terminal A | DC Motor Lead 1 | Red | | 4 (GND) | Ground | Arduino GND + Battery − | Black | | 5 (GND) | Ground | Arduino GND + Battery − | Black | | 6 (IN2) | Direction input 2 | Arduino D10 | Green | | 7 (OUT2) | Motor terminal B | DC Motor Lead 2 | Blue | | 8 (V_s) | Motor supply | Battery + (9 V) | Red | | 16 (V_cc) | Logic supply | Arduino 5 V | Red | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Wiring Rule:** Pins 4, 5, 12, 13 (all GNDs) of the L293D must be **shorted together** and connected to the **common ground** shared with both the Arduino and the external battery. A floating ground is the **#1 cause** of "motor does not spin" debugging issues. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** **What is an H-Bridge? Why is it necessary for the directional control of a DC motor when using an Arduino?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **H-Bridge** is an electronic circuit comprising **four switching elements** (transistors/MOSFETs) arranged in an "H" topology around a DC motor. It is necessary because: 1. **Current Capacity Mismatch:** Arduino GPIO pins can source only **40 mA**, whereas a DC motor requires **200 mA – 2 A**. The H-Bridge (L293D) acts as a **current buffer**, protecting the Arduino. 2. **Bidirectional Current Flow:** A DC motor reverses direction when the **polarity** of its supply is reversed. A single GPIO pin can only output positive voltage. The H-Bridge allows the Arduino to command the motor to run **clockwise, anticlockwise, brake, or coast** by simply toggling two logic pins (IN1 and IN2), without ever reversing the battery physically. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **List any four hardware components used in the experiment "Directional Control of DC Motor using Arduino" and state the function of the L293D IC.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | # | Component | Function | |---|-----------|----------| | 1 | Arduino Uno | Acts as the programmable logic controller that issues direction commands. | | 2 | L293D IC | A dual H-Bridge motor driver that amplifies the low-current Arduino signals into high-current outputs capable of driving two DC motors bidirectionally. | | 3 | DC Motor (PMDC) | The output actuator whose shaft rotation we wish to control. | | 4 | External Battery (9 V/12 V) | Supplies the high-current power required by the motor (Pin 8 of L293D). | --- ### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the common ground** between Arduino, L293D, and battery → circuit simply won't work, but students rarely mention this in the diagram. **(Lose 1–2 marks)** > 2. **Drawing IN1 and IN2 logic wrong** in the truth table (e.g., HIGH/HIGH for forward instead of brake) → critical conceptual error. **(Lose 2–3 marks)** > 3. **Not mentioning the enable pin (EN1)** and that it must be HIGH for the motor to run → incomplete explanation. **(Lose 1 mark)** > 4. **Using `digitalWrite()` on a PWM pin** when speed control is required — should use `analogWrite()`. **(Lose 1 mark)** --- #### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an H-Bridge circuit using the L293D IC. Draw the truth table for all four possible input combinations and describe what happens to the motor in each case. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Arduino program to perform the following sequence continuously: rotate the DC motor in the **clockwise direction at 70% speed for 4 seconds**, **stop for 2 seconds**, then rotate in the **anticlockwise direction at 70% speed for 4 seconds**, then **stop for 2 seconds**. Display each state on the Serial Monitor. --- #### ✅ Model Answer — Question A **(a) H-Bridge Working with L293D [7 Marks]:** The L293D contains **two H-Bridges**. For one motor, we use **four transistors** (S1, S2, S3, S4) controlled by logic inputs **IN1 (Pin 2)** and **IN2 (Pin 7)**, gated by **EN1 (Pin 1)**. - **Case 1 (CW):** IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW, EN1=HIGH → S1 & S4 close. Current flows from V_s through S1 → Motor (+→−) → S4 → GND. Motor rotates **clockwise**. (2 marks) - **Case 2 (CCW):** IN1=LOW, IN2=HIGH, EN1=HIGH → S2 & S3 close. Current reverses through motor. Motor rotates **anticlockwise**. (2 marks) - **Case 3 (Brake):** IN1=HIGH, IN2=HIGH, EN1=HIGH → S1 & S2 close. Both motor terminals tied to V_s. The back-EMF is shorted, causing the motor to **brake rapidly**. (1.5 marks) - **Case 4 (Coast):** IN1=LOW, IN2=LOW, EN1=HIGH → all switches open. Motor terminals float. Motor **coasts** to a stop. (1.5 marks) **Truth Table:** | IN1 | IN2 | EN1 | Motor State | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:-----------:| | 1 | 0 | 1 | CW | | 0 | 1 | 1 | CCW | | 1 | 1 | 1 | Brake | | 0 | 0 | 1 | Coast | | X | X | 0 | Disabled | --- **(b) Arduino Program [7 Marks]:** ```cpp const int IN1 = 9; const int IN2 = 10; const int EN1 = 11; const int SPEED = 178; // 70% of 255 = 178.5 ≈ 178 void setup() { pinMode(IN1, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN2, OUTPUT); pinMode(EN1, OUTPUT); Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { // Clockwise at 70% for 4 s digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(EN1, SPEED); Serial.println("CW @ 70%"); delay(4000); // Stop for 2 s digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(EN1, 0); Serial.println("STOP"); delay(2000); // Anticlockwise at 70% for 4 s digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, HIGH); analogWrite(EN1, SPEED); Serial.println("CCW @ 70%"); delay(4000); // Stop for 2 s digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(EN1, 0); Serial.println("STOP"); delay(2000); } ``` **Justification of `SPEED = 178`:** Arduino's `analogWrite()` accepts values 0 to 255. A 70 % duty cycle requires $\frac{70}{100} \times 255 = 178.5$, rounded to **178**. (1 mark) --- #### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the pin configuration of the L293D motor driver IC. With a labelled block diagram, explain how it is interfaced with an Arduino Uno and a DC motor. What is the role of the **flyback diodes** built into the L293D? **(b) [7 Marks]** A smart fan system is to be designed. The fan (DC motor) must: - Run **clockwise** when an LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) senses **bright light** (analog value > 600). - Run **anticlockwise** when the LDR senses **darkness** (analog value < 300). - **Stop** for all intermediate values (300 ≤ value ≤ 600). Design the Arduino code for this IoT application. Assume the LDR is connected to **A0** and the motor to **D9, D10, D11** as in the standard experiment. --- #### ✅ Model Answer — Question B **(a) L293D Pin Configuration & Interfacing [7 Marks]:** The L293D is a **16-pin DIP** IC. Key pins: - **Pins 1, 9** — Enable inputs (EN1,2 and EN3,4). Tie HIGH to activate a half-bridge. - **Pins 2, 7, 10, 15** — Logic inputs IN1, IN2, IN3, IN4. - **Pins 3, 6, 11, 14** — Outputs to the motor. - **Pins 4, 5, 12, 13** — **Ground pins**, all internally connected; must be tied together. - **Pin 8** — Motor supply V_s (4.5 V – 36 V). - **Pin 16** — Logic supply V_cc (5 V). **Interfacing Block Diagram** (described textually): Arduino 5 V → Pin 16; Arduino GND → Pins 4, 5; Battery + → Pin 8; Battery − → common GND; Arduino D9 → Pin 2 (IN1); Arduino D10 → Pin 7 (IN2); Arduino D11 → Pin 1 (EN1); Motor leads → Pins 3 and 6. (5 marks) **Flyback Diodes:** The L293D has **internal flyback (freewheeling) diodes** connected across each transistor. When the motor is switched off, the **inductive kickback** of the armature winding generates a high-voltage spike ($V = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$) that can destroy the output transistors. The diodes provide a safe path for this energy, clamping the voltage to ~0.7 V above V_s or below GND, thereby protecting the IC. (2 marks) --- **(b) Smart Fan Arduino Code [7 Marks]:** ```cpp const int LDR_PIN = A0; // LDR analog input const int IN1 = 9; const int IN2 = 10; const int EN1 = 11; const int SPEED = 200; // ≈ 78% speed void setup() { pinMode(IN1, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN2, OUTPUT); pinMode(EN1, OUTPUT); Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int light = analogRead(LDR_PIN); // 0–1023 Serial.print("LDR Value: "); Serial.println(light); if (light > 600) { // BRIGHT -> Clockwise digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(EN1, SPEED); Serial.println("Action: CW (Bright Light)"); } else if (light < 300) { // DARK -> Anticlockwise digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, HIGH); analogWrite(EN1, SPEED); Serial.println("Action: CCW (Dark)"); } else { // AMBIENT -> Stop digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(EN1, 0); Serial.println("Action: STOP (Ambient)"); } delay(200); } ``` --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Pin on Wall!
