📌)** - ✅ **DC motor direction = polarity of armature voltage.** Reverse polarity → reverse rotation. - ✅ **Arduino cannot drive motors directly** (40 mA limit vs. 200+ mA motor draw) → **always use a motor driver IC**. - ✅ **L293D** is a **dual H-Bridge** in a 16-pin DIP package; it includes **internal flyback diodes**. - ✅ **Pin 1 = EN1 (Enable)** → must be **HIGH** for motor to run; can be **PWM-modulated** for speed control. - ✅ **Pins 2, 7 = IN1, IN2** → logic inputs; they decide **direction** (HIGH/LOW = CW; LOW/HIGH = CCW). - ✅ **Pins 3, 6 = OUT1, OUT2** → connect to the two motor terminals (polarity does not matter at the motor). - ✅ **Pin 8 = V_s** → motor battery (4.5 V – 36 V); **Pin 16 = V_cc** → 5 V from Arduino. - ✅ **Pins 4, 5, 12, 13 = GND** → all four must be **shorted together** and tied to the **common ground** with Arduino. - ✅ **H-Bridge Truth Table:** IN1/IN2 = 1/0 → CW; 0/1 → CCW; 1/1 → Brake; 0/0 → Coast. - ✅ **Forbidden state:** Never close the two transistors on the **same leg** (S1+S2 or S3+S4) — that creates a **short circuit (shoot-through)** and destroys the IC. - ✅ **`digitalWrite(pin, HIGH/LOW)`** for direction; **`analogWrite(pin, 0–255)`** for speed (PWM duty cycle). - ✅ **PWM Formula (MUST memorize):** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{s}$, where $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$. - ✅ **Arduino PWM default frequency:** ~490 Hz on D3, D9, D10, D11; ~980 Hz on D5, D6. - ✅ **Conversion:** `analogWrite(p, x)` where $x \in [0, 255]$ corresponds to $D = \dfrac{x}{255}$. - ✅ **Motor braking types:** **Active brake** (regenerative, fast) vs. **Coast** (free run, slow). - ✅ **Common debugging tips:** (1) Check common ground; (2) Check battery voltage; (3) Verify EN1 is HIGH; (4) Test motor with direct battery first. ---

topics.title: Measurement of Sound Using Sound Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGL607 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB USING ARDUINO > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Sound Using Sound Sensor # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sound Sensor (Acoustic Transducer Module):** A sound sensor is an electromechanical/electronic transducer module that converts acoustic pressure waves (vibrations in an air medium) into a corresponding electrical voltage signal. In IoT laboratory contexts, it typically refers to modules such as the **KY-038** or **KY-037** which contain an **electret condenser microphone (ECM)** front-end, a charge amplifier, and a **LM393 comparator** stage that provides simultaneous **analog** (continuous waveform) and **digital** (threshold-triggered HIGH/LOW) outputs to a microcontroller such as the Arduino UNO. A sound sensor module is essentially the "ears" of an embedded system. It allows a digital, non-living device (Arduino) to *perceive* acoustic phenomena in its environment and react to them programmatically. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The "Ear Microphone" Metaphor** > Imagine you are standing in a noisy classroom with earplugs that have a tiny digital readout. Every time a sound wave reaches your eardrum (the electret microphone diaphragm), the earplugs convert the air pressure vibrations into a number on a screen. A **loud clap** produces a **big number**, while a **whisper** produces a **small number**. The Arduino's **analog input pin** acts exactly like that digital readout — it continuously translates the air pressure into a numerical value between **0 and 1023**, which the program can read and interpret. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following standard metrics are universally used in acoustic measurement and **must** be memorized for the lab exam: > > - **Reference Sound Pressure:** $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}$ (threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz). > - **Standard ADC Reference Voltage (Arduino UNO):** $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\ \text{V}$ (default). > - **ADC Resolution:** **10-bit** (values from 0 to $2^{10}-1 = 1023$). > - **Sound Pressure Level Unit:** **decibel (dB SPL)**. > - **Threshold of Pain:** **120 dB SPL**. > - **Quiet Library Ambient Level:** approximately **30–40 dB SPL**. ## 1.4 Types of Output Pins on a Typical Sound Sensor Module > [!NOTE] > A standard KY-038 sound sensor module exposes **four** functional pins to the Arduino: > > 1. **VCC** — Power supply (typically **+5V** from Arduino). > 2. **GND** — Common ground. > 3. **A0 (Analog Output)** — A continuous voltage waveform proportional to instantaneous sound amplitude; read via Arduino's `analogRead()`. > 4. **D0 (Digital Output)** — A binary HIGH/LOW logic signal that triggers when the sound amplitude exceeds a user-adjustable threshold set by the onboard potentiometer. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Stage-by-Stage Breakdown The signal path of a sound sensor module can be understood as a four-stage pipeline: - **Stage 1 — Acoustic-to-Mechanical Conversion (Electret Microphone):** The microphone's internal diaphragm vibrates sympathetically with incident sound waves. These mechanical vibrations modulate the capacitance of a permanently charged "electret" material, producing a tiny **microvolt-level** alternating voltage at the microphone terminals. - **Stage 2 — Charge-to-Voltage Amplification (Internal JFET/Op-Amp):** The millivolt signal is too weak to be sampled by the Arduino. An internal **JFET source follower** (or a small op-amp) amplifies the signal up to the **0–5V** range that the Arduino's ADC can read. - **Stage 3 — Comparator Stage (LM393 IC):** The amplified analog signal is fed into one input of an **LM393 dual comparator**. The other input is a DC reference voltage derived from a potentiometer. When the analog signal exceeds this reference, the comparator output switches from **HIGH → LOW** (active-low digital output is common). - **Stage 4 — Microcontroller Sampling (Arduino):** The Arduino's **ATmega328P** ADC samples the analog waveform at a default rate of approximately **9.6 kHz** (configurable via the `prescaler`). The digital pin can trigger hardware interrupts (e.g., on `FALLING` edge) for low-latency event detection. ## 2.2 Why Use a Sound Sensor in IoT? > [!NOTE] > Real-world engineering applications of acoustic sensing include: > - **Smart home automation:** clap-controlled switching of lights/appliances. > - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** noise-level monitoring in factories (occupational safety). > - **Smart cities:** gunshot detection, traffic noise mapping. > - **Healthcare IoT:** baby cry monitors, snoring detectors. > - **Security systems:** glass-break detection, intrusion alarms. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every** formula required for solving numerical and analytical questions on this experiment. All boundaries and units are explicitly stated. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary / Range | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | ADC Digital Code | $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{ref}}} \times (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$ | $N$ = digital code, $V_{\text{in}}$ = input voltage (V), $V_{\text{ref}}$ = reference (V), $n$ = resolution (bits) | $0 \le N \le 1023$ for $n = 10$ | | 2 | ADC Voltage Recovery | $V_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{N}{1023} \times V_{\text{ref}}$ | $N$ = `analogRead()` value, $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\ \text{V}$ | Output: $0\ \text{V}$ to $5\ \text{V}$ | | 3 | Sound Pressure Level (SPL) | $\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | $P$ = measured pressure (Pa), $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | Typical range: $0\ \text{dB}$ to $140\ \text{dB}$ | | 4 | Sound Intensity Level (SIL) | $\text{SIL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $I$ = intensity (W/m²), $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ | Linear-to-log conversion | | 5 | Sensor dB Mapping (Empirical) | $dB = m \cdot V_{\text{out}} + c$ | $m$ = slope (dB/V), $c$ = intercept (dB), from calibration | Determined experimentally | | 6 | Sampling Frequency (Arduino Default) | $f_s = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{128 \times (ADC\_PRESCALAR)}$ | $f_{\text{clk}} = 16\ \text{MHz}$ | Default ≈ $9.615\ \text{kHz}$ | | 7 | Nyquist Criterion | $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$ | $f_{\text{max}}$ = highest signal frequency | Prevents **aliasing** | | 8 | LED/Buzzer Activation Logic | $\text{state} = (V_{\text{out}} > V_{\text{threshold}})$ | Boolean condition | `HIGH` or `LOW` | ## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Formula - **Formula 1 & 2** form an **invertible pair** — they let you move between the **digital domain** (the integer the Arduino reads) and the **analog voltage domain** (the physical world). - **Formula 3** is the **logarithmic decibel definition** that compresses the enormous human-audible pressure range (factor of $10^7$) into a tractable 0–140 dB scale. - **Formula 5** is the **practical workhorse** for IoT labs because the cheap KY-038 sensor is **not laboratory-grade**; it must be **calibrated** against a reference SPL meter to produce a usable linear voltage-to-dB mapping. - **Formula 7** is the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem** — a cornerstone of digital signal processing that explains why we cannot detect frequencies above half the sampling rate. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Recovering Voltage from ADC Reading **Problem Statement:** The Arduino's `analogRead(A0)` returns a digital value `N = 612`. The reference voltage is **5.0 V**. Determine the actual analog voltage at the sensor's output pin. **Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.** The Arduino UNO uses a 10-bit successive-approximation ADC. The general relationship between input voltage and digital output is: $$V_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{N}{2^n - 1} \times V_{\text{ref}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the known numerical values.** Here, $N = 612$, $n = 10$, and $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\ \text{V}$. Substituting: $$V_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{612}{2^{10} - 1} \times 5.0$$ $$V_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{612}{1023} \times 5.0$$ **Step 3 — Perform the arithmetic explicitly.** $$V_{\text{in}} = 0.59824 \times 5.0$$ $$V_{\text{in}} = 2.9912\ \text{V}$$ **Step 4 — State the final result with units and physical interpretation.** > **Result:** $V_{\text{in}} \approx 2.99\ \text{V}$. > > **Interpretation:** The sound sensor's analog output is sitting at approximately **60 % of full-scale** (since $612/1023 \approx 0.598$), which suggests a moderately loud ambient acoustic environment. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Converting Sensor Voltage to Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL) **Problem Statement:** After two-point calibration of the KY-038 sensor against a professional SPL meter, the empirical linear mapping is determined to be: $$dB = 40 \cdot V_{\text{out}} + 20$$ If the Arduino reports a peak sensor voltage of $V_{\text{out}} = 1.75\ \text{V}$ during a hand-clap event, what is the corresponding Sound Pressure Level in **dB SPL**? **Step 1 — Identify the appropriate formula.** We use the empirically calibrated linear mapping (Formula 5 in the cheat sheet): $$\text{SPL} = m \cdot V_{\text{out}} + c$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the calibration constants and measured voltage.** $$\text{SPL} = (40\ \text{dB/V}) \times (1.75\ \text{V}) + 20\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the multiplication.** $$\text{SPL} = 70\ \text{dB} + 20\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 4 — State the final result with units and physical meaning.** > **Result:** $\text{SPL} = 90\ \text{dB SPL}$. > > **Interpretation:** A reading of **90 dB SPL** is comparable to the sound of a **lawnmower** or **heavy traffic** at close range. Prolonged exposure at this level can cause hearing damage, which is why such monitoring is critical in occupational safety IoT systems. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Threshold Detection for Digital Output (LM393 Stage) **Problem Statement:** The onboard potentiometer on the KY-038 is adjusted such that the comparator reference voltage is $V_{\text{ref,comp}} = 1.20\ \text{V}$. Determine the digital output state of the D0 pin if the analog signal from the microphone momentarily rises to $V_{\text{in,peak}} = 1.45\ \text{V}$. **Step 1 — State the comparator decision rule.** The LM393 is configured in **active-LOW** mode on most KY-038 boards, meaning: - If $V_{\text{in}} > V_{\text{ref,comp}}$ → **D0 goes LOW** (event detected). - If $V_{\text{in}} < V_{\text{ref,comp}}$ → **D0 stays HIGH** (no event). **Step 2 — Compare the two voltages.** $$V_{\text{in,peak}} = 1.45\ \text{V} \quad \text{vs.} \quad V_{\text{ref,comp}} = 1.20\ \text{V}$$ $$1.45\ \text{V} > 1.20\ \text{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Condition satisfied.}$$ **Step 3 — Conclude the digital state and Arduino action.** > **Result:** The D0 pin transitions from **HIGH to LOW**. > > **Arduino action:** A `digitalRead(D0) == LOW` condition evaluates to `true`. In a typical program, this triggers an event handler — for example, toggling a relay, lighting an LED, or sending an MQTT alert over Wi-Fi (e.g., via ESP8266). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Numerical Patterns | Scenario | Input Given | Required Output | Key Formula Used | |---|---|---|---| | Voltage from raw ADC | $N = 410$ | $V_{\text{in}}$ in volts | $V_{\text{in}} = N/1023 \times 5.0$ | | ADC code from voltage | $V_{\text{in}} = 3.3\ \text{V}$ | $N$ (integer) | $N = (V_{\text{in}}/5.0) \times 1023$ | | SPL from pressure | $P = 0.02\ \text{Pa}$ | dB SPL | $20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$ | | Pressure from SPL | SPL $= 60\ \text{dB}$ | $P$ in Pa | $P = P_0 \times 10^{60/20}$ | | dB from calibrated $V_{\text{out}}$ | $V_{\text{out}} = 1.10\ \text{V}$ | dB | $dB = m V + c$ | | Threshold detection | $V_{\text{in}}$, $V_{\text{th}}$ | Boolean state | Comparator rule | | Sampling rate calculation | Prescaler $= 32$ | $f_s$ in Hz | $f_s = 16 \times 10^6 / (128 \times 32)$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **complete signal and data flow** from the acoustic event in the environment to the user-facing IoT action. This is a **functional architecture** rather than a literal breadboard photo, and it covers every subsystem a student would need to describe in a viva. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Acoustic Source
Clap, Voice, Noise] --> B[Electret Condenser
Microphone ECM] B --> C[JFET Pre-Amplifier
Internal Stage] C --> D[Analog Signal
V out proportional to SPL] D --> E[Arduino UNO
Analog Pin A0] D --> F[LM393 Comparator
Threshold Stage] F --> G[Digital Signal
HIGH or LOW] G --> H[Arduino UNO
Digital Pin D2] E --> I[ATmega328P
ADC 10-bit] H --> J[Interrupt Service
Routine ISR] I --> K[Serial Monitor
Voltage and dB] J --> L[Actuator Layer
LED, Buzzer, Relay] K --> M[Cloud Upload
ThingSpeak, Blynk, MQTT] L --> N[User Notification
Smartphone, Dashboard] M --> N ``` ## 4.2 Circuit Connection Schematic (Mermaid Block View) > [!IMPORTANT] > The diagram below maps the **physical wiring** between the KY-038 sound sensor, the Arduino UNO, and the output actuator. Memorize the pin mapping — it is a frequent viva question. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SensorModule[KY-038 Sound Sensor Module] P1[VCC Pin] P2[GND Pin] P3[A0 Analog Pin] P4[D0 Digital Pin] P5[Onboard Potentiometer
Sets Comparator Threshold] end subgraph ArduinoBoard[Arduino UNO Board] AP1[5V Rail] AP2[GND Rail] AP3[Analog Input A0] AP4[Digital Pin D2] AP5[PWM Pin D9] end subgraph ActuatorBlock[Actuator and Indicator Stage] LED1[LED Indicator] BZ1[Active Buzzer] RES1[Current Limiting
Resistor 220 ohm] end P1 --> AP1 P2 --> AP2 P3 --> AP3 P4 --> AP4 P5 -.->|Adjusts Reference| P4 AP5 --> RES1 RES1 --> LED1 AP5 --> BZ1 ``` ## 4.3 Software Flow — Arduino Program Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Start Program] --> S1[Initialize Serial 9600 baud] S1 --> S2[Configure Pin A0 as INPUT] S1 --> S3[Configure Pin D2 as INPUT PULLUP] S1 --> S4[Configure Pin D9 as OUTPUT for LED] S1 --> S5[Configure Pin D8 as OUTPUT for Buzzer] S4 --> S6[Loop begins] S5 --> S6 S2 --> S6 S3 --> S6 S6 --> S7[analogRead A0 raw value N] S7 --> S8[Compute V = N / 1023.0 times 5.0] S8 --> S9[Map V to dB using calibration equation] S9 --> S10[Print V and dB to Serial Monitor] S10 --> S11[digitalRead D2 state] S11 --> S12{Is D2 LOW?} S12 -->|Yes| S13[Turn ON LED and Buzzer] S12 -->|No| S14[Turn OFF LED and Buzzer] S13 --> S15[Delay 100 ms for debounce] S14 --> S15 S15 --> S6 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q: List the four functional pins of the KY-038 sound sensor module and state the purpose of each.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. **VCC** — Accepts +5 V DC from the Arduino to power the module's amplifier and comparator. (0.75 mark) > 2. **GND** — Common ground reference between the module and the Arduino. (0.75 mark) > 3. **A0 (Analog Output)** — Emits a continuous voltage waveform proportional to the instantaneous sound amplitude; read via `analogRead()`. (1 mark) > 4. **D0 (Digital Output)** — Emits a binary HIGH/LOW logic signal based on whether the sound amplitude exceeds the threshold set by the onboard potentiometer; read via `digitalRead()`. (0.5 mark) --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q: Define Sound Pressure Level (SPL) and write its mathematical formula. Mention the value of the reference pressure $P_0$.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is a logarithmic measure of the RMS sound pressure of a sound wave relative to a reference pressure. It is expressed in **decibels (dB SPL)**. > - **Formula (1 mark):** > $$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$$ > - **Reference pressure (0.5 mark):** $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}$ (the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert ADC counts to voltage:** A raw `analogRead()` value is NOT in volts. Marks are deducted if students report "voltage = 512" without applying $V = N/1023 \times 5$. > 2. **Confusing analog and digital pins:** The A0 and D0 pins are NOT interchangeable. A0 reads continuously; D0 reads thresholded binary. > 3. **Omitting the reference pressure in SPL formula:** Examiners will deduct at least 1 mark if $P_0$ is missing. > 4. **Skipping calibration discussion:** An answer about "measuring sound in dB" without acknowledging sensor calibration is considered incomplete. > 5. **Wrong baud rate / wrong pin mode declarations** in Arduino code fetch deductions in practical exams. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q: Design an Arduino-based sound monitoring system using the KY-038 sensor.** **(a)** Draw the circuit connection diagram, list the required components, and explain the working of the LM393 comparator stage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write the complete Arduino program to (i) read the analog sound value, (ii) convert it to voltage, (iii) compute a calibrated dB reading, and (iv) trigger an LED + buzzer alarm when the dB exceeds 75 dB. Show the expected Serial Monitor output for a clap event. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Circuit Design and LM393 Explanation (7 marks) **Components Required (1.5 marks):** - Arduino UNO board (1) - KY-038 sound sensor module (1) - 5 mm LED (1) - Active 5 V buzzer (1) - 220 Ω current-limiting resistor (1) - Breadboard and jumper wires **Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** The Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.2** above serves as the answer; the student should reproduce it (or an equivalent Fritzing-style drawing) on paper. Key connections: - KY-038 **VCC → Arduino 5V** - KY-038 **GND → Arduino GND** - KY-038 **A0 → Arduino A0** - KY-038 **D0 → Arduino D2** (interrupt-capable) - LED **anode → 220 Ω → Arduino D9**; LED cathode → GND - Buzzer **positive → Arduino D8**; negative → GND **LM393 Comparator Working (3.5 marks):** The LM393 is a dual differential comparator IC. In the KY-038 module, one of its comparators is wired as follows: - **Inverting input (−):** Connected to a DC reference voltage set by the onboard potentiometer (the user-adjustable threshold $V_{\text{th}}$). - **Non-inverting input (+):** Connected to the amplified microphone signal $V_{\text{mic}}$. - **Output:** Pulled HIGH through an external resistor. The output is **active-LOW**, meaning it remains HIGH during silence and drops to LOW the instant $V_{\text{mic}} > V_{\text{th}}$. > **Mathematical Decision Rule:** > $$D_0 = \begin{cases} \text{LOW} & \text{if } V_{\text{mic}} > V_{\text{th}} \\ \text{HIGH} & \text{if } V_{\text{mic}} \le V_{\text{th}} \end{cases}$$ This comparator stage is what allows the cheap analog microphone to act as a **digital event detector** suitable for triggering interrupts on the Arduino. #### Part (b) — Arduino Program and Expected Output (7 marks) **Complete Arduino Sketch (5 marks):** ```cpp /* Experiment 2: Measurement of Sound Using Sound Sensor Compatible Board: Arduino UNO Sensor Module: KY-038 */ const int sensorAnalogPin = A0; // Analog output from sound sensor const int sensorDigitalPin = 2; // Digital output from sound sensor const int ledPin = 9; // LED indicator const int buzzerPin = 8; // Active buzzer // Calibration constants determined experimentally const float m_db = 40.0; // slope in dB per Volt const float c_db = 20.0; // intercept in dB const float thresholdDb = 75.0; // alarm threshold void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); pinMode(sensorAnalogPin, INPUT); pinMode(sensorDigitalPin, INPUT); pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); pinMode(buzzerPin, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW); digitalWrite(buzzerPin, LOW); Serial.println("Sound Monitoring System Initialized"); } void loop() { int rawAnalog = analogRead(sensorAnalogPin); float voltage = (rawAnalog / 1023.0) * 5.0; float soundDb = (m_db * voltage) + c_db; Serial.print("Raw ADC = "); Serial.print(rawAnalog); Serial.print(" | Voltage = "); Serial.print(voltage, 3); Serial.print(" V | Sound Level = "); Serial.print(soundDb, 1); Serial.println(" dB SPL"); int digitalState = digitalRead(sensorDigitalPin); if (soundDb > thresholdDb || digitalState == LOW) { digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH); digitalWrite(buzzerPin, HIGH); Serial.println(">>> ALARM: Loud sound detected! <<<"); } else { digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW); digitalWrite(buzzerPin, LOW); } delay(150); } ``` **Expected Serial Monitor Output for a Hand-Clap Event (2 marks):** ``` Sound Monitoring System Initialized Raw ADC = 045 | Voltage = 0.220 V | Sound Level = 28.8 dB SPL Raw ADC = 048 | Voltage = 0.235 V | Sound Level = 29.4 dB SPL Raw ADC = 312 | Voltage = 1.525 V | Sound Level = 81.0 dB SPL >>> ALARM: Loud sound detected! <<< Raw ADC = 720 | Voltage = 3.520 V | Sound Level = 160.8 dB SPL >>> ALARM: Loud sound detected! <<< Raw ADC = 102 | Voltage = 0.498 V | Sound Level = 39.9 dB SPL ``` > **Explanation of the output sequence:** The Arduino first prints a quiet ambient reading (~29 dB), then a sudden spike (clap onset) at 81 dB triggers the alarm, followed by a louder decay echo at 160 dB (sensor saturation region) before settling back to ambient. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q: Analyze the acoustic measurement principles behind sound-sensor-based IoT systems.** **(a)** Explain the operational principle of an electret condenser microphone. Derive the relationship between the ADC digital output code $N$ and the input voltage $V_{\text{in}}$ for a 10-bit ADC with a 5 V reference. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hand-clap produces a sound pressure of $P = 0.063\ \text{Pa}$ at the sensor. Calculate the corresponding Sound Pressure Level in dB SPL. If the sensor's voltage output during the same event is $V_{\text{out}} = 1.20\ \text{V}$ and the calibrated mapping is $dB = 35 \cdot V_{\text{out}} + 25$, compute (i) the dB reading from the sensor and (ii) the percentage error relative to the theoretical SPL. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Electret Microphone Principle and ADC Derivation (7 marks) **Electret Condenser Microphone (ECM) Working (4 marks):** An ECM consists of two plates that form a **capacitor**: a fixed back-plate and a flexible diaphragm, with a permanently charged dielectric material (the **electret**) between them. When sound waves strike the diaphragm, it vibrates, changing the **separation distance $d$** between the plates. Since capacitance is given by: $$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$ a change in $d$ produces a proportional change in $C$. Because the electret holds a fixed charge $Q$, the voltage across the capacitor changes according to $V = Q/C$. Thus, acoustic pressure is **transduced** into an **alternating voltage** that mirrors the waveform of the original sound. **Derivation of ADC Equation (3 marks):** A 10-bit ADC divides the reference voltage $V_{\text{ref}}$ into $2^{10} = 1024$ equal quantum steps of size $\Delta V$: $$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{1023}$$ The digital output code is the integer count of these steps that fit below $V_{\text{in}}$: $$N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{ref}}/1023} \right\rfloor$$ Inverting this relation to recover $V_{\text{in}}$ from $N$: $$V_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{N}{1023} \times V_{\text{ref}}$$ For the Arduino UNO with $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\ \text{V}$: $$V_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{N}{1023} \times 5.0\ \text{V}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical SPL Calculation and Error Analysis (7 marks) **Step 1 — Compute theoretical SPL from pressure (2 marks):** Using the SPL formula with $P_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}$: $$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{0.063}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(3150)$$ $$\text{SPL} = 20 \times 3.4983 = 69.97\ \text{dB SPL} \approx 70\ \text{dB SPL}$$ **Step 2 — Compute sensor-derived dB (2 marks):** Applying the calibration $dB = 35 \cdot V_{\text{out}} + 25$ with $V_{\text{out}} = 1.20\ \text{V}$: $$dB_{\text{sensor}} = 35 \times 1.20 + 25 = 42 + 25 = 67\ \text{dB SPL}$$ **Step 3 — Compute percentage error (3 marks):** The percentage error is defined as: $$\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert dB_{\text{sensor}} - dB_{\text{theoretical}} \vert}{dB_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$$ > **Critical KTU pitfall:** Always use the $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation to denote **absolute value**; writing it as a vertical pipe `|` inside a markdown table will break the table syntax. In our cheat sheet we used $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ for the same reason. Substituting the numerical values: $$\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{\lvert 67 - 70 \rvert}{70} \times 100 = \dfrac{3}{70} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{Error} = 4.29\ \%$$ > **Conclusion:** The sensor reading of 67 dB is within **4.29 %** of the theoretical 70 dB, which is an acceptable engineering accuracy for a hobby-grade KY-038 module. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute Preparation** > > - **Module used:** KY-038 (electret mic + LM393 comparator + potentiometer). > - **Two outputs:** **A0** (analog waveform) and **D0** (digital threshold trigger). > - **Power:** +5 V on VCC, GND on GND. > - **Arduino ADC:** 10-bit, $2^{10} = 1024$ steps, default $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\ \text{V}$. > - **Voltage recovery formula:** $V_{\text{in}} = (N / 1023) \times 5.0$ — always report in **volts**, not raw counts. > - **SPL formula:** $\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}(P / P_0)$ with $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$. > - **Reference pressure $P_0$** must be quoted explicitly; missing it loses a mark. > - **Active-LOW digital output:** D0 is HIGH during silence and **goes LOW** on a sound event. > - **Calibration is mandatory** for dB accuracy; an empirical linear model $dB = m \cdot V_{\text{out}} + c$ is acceptable. > - **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{\text{max}}$ — Arduino's default ~9.6 kHz limits faithful capture to audio below ~4.8 kHz (speech fundamentals, not full HiFi). > - **Debounce delay** (~100–200 ms) is required in the loop to prevent spurious alarm retriggering. > - **Common applications:** clap switch, baby cry detector, gunshot detection, industrial noise compliance monitoring, smart-home voice control. > - **Common viva questions:** "What does the LM393 do?", "Why is the digital output active-LOW?", "What is the role of the potentiometer?", "How would you improve the accuracy of your dB readings?". > - **Examiner's tip:** Always mention **calibration**, **units**, and **boundary conditions** in any analytical answer for full marks. > - **Code conventions:** Use `INPUT` for analog pins (not strictly required but good practice), `INPUT_PULLUP` for the digital pin if the board's internal pull-up is desired, and always end `Serial.print` statements with `\n` (use `Serial.println`).

topics.title: Development of Smart Incubator Using W1209 and Bulb
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL607 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB USING ARDUINO
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Development of Smart Incubator Using W1209 and Bulb

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Smart Incubator** is an autonomous, closed-loop temperature-controlled enclosure designed to maintain a narrow, biologically optimal thermal range (typically **37.5 °C ± 0.5 °C** for avian embryo development) without continuous human intervention. In the KTU 2024 IoT Lab context, a *Smart Incubator* is realized as an **embedded feedback system** that uses the **W1209 digital temperature control module** (acting as the controller) and an **incandescent bulb** (acting as the heating actuator) to regulate internal chamber temperature.

The **W1209** is a self-contained, programmable, single-channel **thermostat module** manufactured around the **STM8S003F3 microcontroller** with an integrated **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) thermistor probe**, a **3-digit 7-segment LED display**, three push-buttons (Set, +, −), and an onboard **electromechanical relay** (typically rated at **10 A / 250 V AC** or **30 V DC**). The module implements **ON/OFF (Bang-Bang) control** with user-defined **Set Point (T_S)** and **Hysteresis (H)** parameters, making it a classic example of a **discrete-event embedded controller** in IoT pedagogy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCGL607 – Experiment 2):**
> "Design and develop a temperature-regulated smart incubator prototype using the W1209 thermostat module interfaced with a 230 V AC incandescent bulb via its onboard relay. The system should maintain a pre-set incubation temperature by automatically switching the bulb ON/OFF based on the chamber's measured temperature."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cluck Thermostat"

Imagine a hen sitting on her eggs. She is **naturally warm-blooded** (≈ 41 °C body temperature) and instinctively shifts her weight or stands up briefly when the eggs get too hot, then settles back down when they cool. The **W1209 + Bulb incubator** mimics this *exact* behaviour:

- The **hen's body** = the **incandescent bulb** (heat source).
- The **hen's beak touching the egg** = the **NTC thermistor probe** (temperature sensor).
- The **hen's brain deciding to sit or stand** = the **W1209's STM8 microcontroller** (decision-maker).
- The **hen's legs pressing down or lifting** = the **relay contacts closing or opening** (switching element).

The hen never gives the eggs *more* heat when they are already warm — she just turns the heat source **ON** when cold and **OFF** when warm. This is precisely **ON/OFF (Bang-Bang) control**, the simplest and most robust form of automatic temperature regulation.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Hardware Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Voltage (W1209) | **DC 12 V** | Must be supplied by a regulated adapter |
| Relay Contact Rating | **10 A / 250 V AC** | Maximum bulb load ≈ **2000 W** resistive |
| Temperature Sensing Range | **−50 °C to +110 °C** | Covers all biological and industrial use cases |
| Display Resolution | **0.1 °C** | Sufficient for incubator precision |
| NTC Probe Type | **10 kΩ @ 25 °C, B = 3435 K** | Standard epoxy-encapsulated waterproof bead |
| Bulb Rating (typical) | **25 W – 60 W incandescent, 230 V AC** | Provides gentle, uniform radiant heat |
| Incubation Set Point | **37.5 °C – 37.8 °C** | Standard for chicken/quail eggs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Emphasis:** In your lab record, always specify the **set temperature (T_S)**, the **hysteresis value (H)**, the **relay switching thresholds**, and the **safety considerations** (insulation, ventilation, fire hazard from continuous bulb operation). A mere block diagram is **not** sufficient for full marks — include the **hysteresis band explanation**.

## 1.4 Component Inventory for the KTU Lab Prototype

1. **W1209 Thermostat Module** — 1 unit (controller + display + relay + sensor in one board)
2. **Incandescent Bulb (25 W / 60 W, 230 V AC)** — 1 unit (heating load)
3. **Bulb Holder / Socket (B22 / E27)** — 1 unit
4. **Insulated Enclosure (thermocol box / wooden box with glass wool lining)** — 1 unit
5. **12 V DC Adapter (≥ 500 mA)** — 1 unit (powers the W1209)
6. **2-Pin Mains Plug and Wire (1.5 sq. mm copper)** — for AC bulb circuit
7. **Optional: Arduino Uno** — for serial-logging chamber temperature via an external NTC (if log is required in the experiment)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of the W1209 — Step-by-Step Logic

The W1209 continuously executes the following firmware loop, refreshed approximately **every 500 ms**:

1. **Acquire:** The internal ADC of the STM8S003F3 reads the voltage divider formed by the NTC thermistor and a fixed **10 kΩ reference resistor**. The measured voltage is converted to a 10-bit digital value.
2. **Linearise:** The firmware applies a **Steinhart–Hart approximation** (or a pre-loaded lookup table) to convert the resistance reading into degrees Celsius.
3. **Compare:** The measured temperature **T_now** is compared with the stored Set Point **T_S** and Hysteresis **H**.
4. **Actuate:** The relay coil is energised or de-energised via a transistor driver (typically an S8050 NPN), which in turn closes or opens the SPDT contact.
5. **Display:** The 3-digit 7-segment LED displays **T_now** in real time; pressing the SET button momentarily reveals **T_S**, then **H**.

## 2.2 The Two Switching Modes of the W1209

The W1209 supports **two control modes**, toggled by long-pressing the "−" button (refer to the data-sheet mnemonic: "**C**" vs "**H**"):

### Mode 1 — Cooling Mode (default from factory, indicator "C")
Used for refrigeration, fans, or chillers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } T_{\text{now}} \geq T_S \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{Relay ON (actuator cools the chamber)} \\
\text{If } T_{\text{now}} \leq T_S - H \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{Relay OFF}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Mode 2 — Heating Mode (indicator "H") — **REQUIRED FOR INCUBATOR**
Used for heaters, incubators, and heat mats.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } T_{\text{now}} \leq T_S \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{Relay ON (bulb heats the chamber)} \\
\text{If } T_{\text{now}} \geq T_S + H \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{Relay OFF}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Lab Setup Step:** For the incubator experiment, the W1209 **MUST be configured in Heating Mode (H)**. If left in Cooling Mode (C), the bulb will switch ON when the chamber is *hot* and OFF when *cold* — the exact opposite of what an incubator requires. This is a **classic viva question** and a **frequent observation-mark deduction**.

## 2.3 Understanding Hysteresis — The "Dead-Band" Concept

**Hysteresis (H)** is the temperature *dead-band* introduced deliberately around the set point to **prevent rapid relay cycling** (chattering). Without hysteresis, if the temperature oscillates around exactly 37.5 °C, the relay would switch **tens of times per minute**, drastically reducing its mechanical life and causing electrical arcing.

### Numerical Example (Incubator at 37.5 °C with H = 0.5 °C)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Threshold (Bulb ON)}  &= T_S - 0.5 = 37.0 \;^\circ\text{C} \\
\text{Upper Threshold (Bulb OFF)} &= T_S + 0.5 = 38.0 \;^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the bulb turns **ON** when the chamber cools to **37.0 °C** and stays ON until it reaches **38.0 °C**, after which it switches OFF and remains OFF until the chamber cools back to 37.0 °C. The effective control band is **(T_S − H) to (T_S + H)**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relation | LaTeX Form | Application in Experiment |
|---|---|---|
| Steinhart–Hart Equation | $T = \dfrac{1}{A + B \ln R + C (\ln R)^3}$ | NTC resistance-to-temperature conversion (firmware-internal) |
| NTC Resistance (Beta) | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | Thermistor modelling (R₀ = 10 kΩ, B = 3435 K) |
| Voltage Divider Output | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{cc}} \cdot \dfrac{R_{\text{NTC}}}{R_{\text{ref}} + R_{\text{NTC}}}$ | ADC input voltage to STM8 |
| Lower Switching Temp | $T_{\text{LOW}} = T_S - H$ | Bulb energises |
| Upper Switching Temp | $T_{\text{HIGH}} = T_S + H$ | Bulb de-energises |
| Relay Contact Life (cycles) | $\approx 100{,}000$ at rated load | Long-term durability check |
| Heat Power of Bulb | $P = V_{\text{rms}}^2 / R_{\text{filament}}$ | Approx. 25–60 W for prototype |
| Energy per Hour | $E = P \times t$ | Estimating daily kWh consumption |

> [!NOTE]
> All temperatures are in **degrees Celsius** unless otherwise stated. The Steinhart–Hart constants **A, B, C** are pre-burned into the W1209 firmware by the manufacturer; the student does **not** need to compute them, but should be able to *name* and *explain* the equation during viva.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same architecture (digital thermostat + relay + heating element) is used in:

- **Domestic geysers and water heaters** (with mechanical thermostat replaced by W1209-style digital controllers).
- **Aquarium heaters** for tropical fish breeding.
- **Pharmaceutical incubators** for bacterial culture growth.
- **3D-printer heat beds** (replacing the relay with a MOSFET for DC loads).
- **Greenhouse seedling propagators** in precision agriculture.

The W1209 is the **de-facto teaching module** in IoT and embedded-system laboratories across Indian universities because it bundles sensor + controller + actuator-driver + HMI (display + buttons) into a **< ₹150** package, allowing students to learn the *complete* feedback loop without writing a single line of code.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculating the Bulb's Heating Effect (Steady-State Energy Balance)

Consider a closed incubator of internal volume **V = 0.02 m³** (a 30 cm × 30 cm × 25 cm thermocol box), with walls of thermal resistance **R_th** and a 25 W incandescent bulb as the sole heat source. The chamber's air has an effective heat capacity approximated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{air}} &= \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V \cdot c_{p,\text{air}} \\
&= 1.2 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \times 0.02 \;\text{m}^3 \times 1005 \;\text{J/(kg·K)} \\
&= 24.12 \;\text{J/K}
\end{aligned}
$$

When the bulb is ON, the rate of temperature rise in the chamber is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dT}{dt} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{bulb}} - Q_{\text{loss}}}{C_{\text{air}}} \\
&= \dfrac{25 \;\text{W} - Q_{\text{loss}}}{24.12 \;\text{J/K}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an *idealised adiabatic* enclosure (Q_loss = 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dT}{dt} &= \dfrac{25}{24.12} \\
&\approx 1.036 \;\text{K/s} \\
&\approx 62.2 \;^\circ\text{C/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, due to conductive losses through the thermocol walls (typical U-value ≈ 3.0 W/m²·K for 2-inch thermocol), the actual rate is **5–10 °C/min**, comfortably within the W1209's 500 ms sampling period.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **not required** in the exam answer-script, but **explaining it orally** during viva earns full marks and demonstrates genuine conceptual clarity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hysteresis Selection Trade-off

The student's lab record must justify the **choice of hysteresis value**. Two extreme cases:

### Case A — Hysteresis too small (H = 0.1 °C)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{LOW}}  &= T_S - 0.1 = 37.4 \;^\circ\text{C} \\
T_{\text{HIGH}} &= T_S + 0.1 = 37.6 \;^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Pros:** Very tight temperature band, ideal for sensitive bio-experiments.
- **Cons:** Relay cycles **excessively** (potentially 30–50 times per hour); mechanical relay life is exhausted in weeks. Also, the bulb's thermal inertia causes **overshoot**, so the system never settles.

### Case B — Hysteresis too large (H = 5.0 °C)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{LOW}}  &= T_S - 5.0 = 32.5 \;^\circ\text{C} \\
T_{\text{HIGH}} &= T_S + 5.0 = 42.5 \;^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Pros:** Relay cycles only a few times per hour; long life.
- **Cons:** Temperature swings ±5 °C — **lethal for embryos** (egg viability drops sharply outside 35–39 °C).

### Optimal Choice (KTU Recommended)

For the prototype, the engineering compromise is:

$$
H_{\text{opt}} = 0.5 \;^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{to} \quad 1.0 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

This yields roughly **8–15 relay cycles per hour** with a temperature band of ±1 °C — acceptable for *demonstration* purposes and well within the embryo's tolerance if insulated properly.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relay Contact Derating

The W1209 relay is rated at **10 A / 250 V AC resistive**. For an inductive load (e.g., a CFL with inductive ballast) the rating must be **derated to 30–50%**. For an *incandescent bulb*, the inrush current at switch-on can be **10–15× the steady-state current** due to the cold tungsten filament.

For a 60 W, 230 V bulb:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{steady}} &= \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{60}{230} \approx 0.26 \;\text{A} \\
I_{\text{inrush}} &\approx 15 \times I_{\text{steady}} \approx 3.9 \;\text{A} \quad \text{(cold filament)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **3.9 A < 10 A**, the relay is **safely rated** for the bulb. However, using a bulb of > 2000 W (e.g., a halogen work-light) **must be avoided** without an external contactor.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Decision Table

| Subsystem | Chosen Component | Justification | Alternative / Rejected |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature Sensor | W1209 onboard NTC (10 kΩ, B = 3435) | Factory-calibrated, waterproof, integrated | LM35 (analog, needs ADC) — rejected for wiring complexity |
| Controller | STM8S003F3 inside W1209 | Pre-programmed, no code needed | Arduino + relay (more flexible but needs coding) — used in Exp 3 |
| Display | 0.36" 3-digit 7-seg red LED | Built-in, no extra wiring | LCD 16×2 (needs I²C + code) — overkill for this experiment |
| Actuator | 25 W / 60 W incandescent bulb | Cheap, gentle radiant heat, fits relay rating | Ceramic PTC heater — rejected: too hot, fire risk |
| Power Supply (Control) | 12 V DC, 1 A adapter | Matches W1209 input rating | 5 V phone charger — rejected: W1209 needs 12 V |
| Power Supply (Load) | 230 V AC mains | Standard Indian domestic supply | 12 V DC heating pad — rejected: poor heat distribution |
| Enclosure | Thermocol box (30×30×25 cm) | Excellent insulation (R ≈ 4 m²·K/W) | Cardboard — rejected: poor insulation, fire hazard |
| Safety Add-on | Glass-fuse 2 A in series with bulb | Protects against short circuits | MCB — rejected: expensive, needs panel mounting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Viva Favourite Question:** *"Why is a thermocol box used and not a metal one?"* — Answer: Metal is a thermal conductor and would dissipate the bulb's heat to the surroundings, forcing the bulb to stay ON continuously, drastically shortening its life and inflating power bills. Thermocol's cellular structure traps air, providing low thermal conductivity (≈ 0.03 W/m·K).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete feedback loop** of the smart incubator. The arrows represent **signal flow**, not just physical wiring.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[12V DC Adapter] -->|DC 12V Power| B[W1209 Module]
    C[NTC Thermistor Probe] -->|Analog Voltage to ADC| B
    B -->|3-digit 7-seg display| D[Local Operator HMI]
    B -->|Relay Coil Drive| E[SPDT Relay Contact]
    E -->|230V AC Switched| F[Incandescent Bulb 25W]
    F -->|Radiant Heat| G[Incubator Chamber Air]
    G -->|Convective Coupling| C
    B -.->|LED Indicator Red| H[Heating ON indicator]
    B -.->|LED Indicator Green| I[Power ON indicator]
    J[SET PLUS MINUS Buttons] -->|User Setpoint Input| B
```

## 4.2 Detailed Wiring Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MAINS[230V AC Mains]
        M1[Live L]
        M2[Neutral N]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL[Low-Voltage DC Side]
        AD1[12V DC Adapter Output +]
        AD2[12V DC Adapter Output -]
    end
    subgraph W1209MOD[W1209 Module PCB]
        W1[K0 Terminal Relay Common]
        W2[K1 Terminal Relay NO Normally Open]
        W3[VCC 12V Input]
        W4[GND Input]
        W5[NTC Probe Connector]
        W6[Onboard Relay Contacts SPDT]
    end
    subgraph BULB[Incandescent Bulb]
        BL1[Filament Terminal A]
        BL2[Filament Terminal B]
    end

    M1 -->|Wire 1| W1
    W6 -->|Internal W1209 trace| W2
    W2 -->|Switched Live| BL1
    BL2 -->|Neutral Path| M2
    AD1 --> W3
    AD2 --> W4
    W5 -.->|Two-wire probe| PROBE[NTC 10k Bead inside Chamber]
```

## 4.3 Operational Flowchart — W1209 Firmware Logic (Heating Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Power ON 12V Applied]) --> INIT[Initialise ADC GPIO Display]
    INIT --> READ[Read NTC voltage via ADC]
    READ --> CONV[Convert ADC value to Temperature in deg C]
    CONV --> DISP[Display T_now on 7-seg LED]
    DISP --> CHECKBTN{Is SET button pressed?}
    CHECKBTN -->|Yes| SHOWSET[Show T_S then H on display]
    SHOWSET --> READ
    CHECKBTN -->|No| COMPARE{T_now less than or equal to T_S minus H or T_S}
    COMPARE -->|Yes| TURNON[Energise Relay Coil]
    TURNON --> LEDRED[Illuminate Red LED]
    LEDRED --> DELAY[Wait 500 ms sampling period]
    COMPARE -->|No T_now greater than T_S| TURNOFF[De-energise Relay Coil]
    TURNOFF --> LEDOFF[Red LED off]
    LEDOFF --> DELAY
    DELAY --> READ
```

## 4.4 Physical Layout Diagram — Top View of the Incubator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TOP[Top View of Thermocol Box]
        T1([NTC Probe suspended from lid center]) 
        T2([25W Bulb in holder mounted at one corner])
        T3([W1209 Module glued to outer wall with display visible outside])
        T4([Small ventilation hole 5mm diameter at opposite corner])
        T5([Egg tray with 6 egg cups in center bottom])
    end
    T2 -->|Heat rises| T1
    T1 -->|Senses air temperature| T3
    T3 -->|Switches| T2
    T4 -->|Air circulation| T5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Tip:** Position the **NTC probe at the same height as the eggs**, *not* near the bulb's hot air plume. Placing it too close to the bulb causes the relay to switch OFF prematurely, leaving the eggs cold — a classic "wrong-placement" mistake observed in many student prototypes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Differentiate between the Heating Mode (H) and Cooling Mode (C) of the W1209 thermostat. Which mode is used in an egg incubator and why?**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

In **Heating Mode (H)**, the W1209 energises its relay when the measured temperature **T_now ≤ T_S** and de-energises it when **T_now ≥ T_S + H**. This is used in **incubators, geysers, and heat mats** where the load is a heater. In **Cooling Mode (C)**, the relay turns ON when **T_now ≥ T_S** — used for refrigeration and fans. For an egg incubator, **Heating Mode (H) is mandatory** because the bulb must turn ON when the chamber cools below 37.5 °C and turn OFF once the eggs have warmed the air above the set point.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Define the term "Hysteresis" in the context of a digital thermostat. Why is it necessary, and what happens if H = 0?**

**Model Answer (≈ 70 words):**

**Hysteresis (H)** is the deliberate *dead-band* of temperature introduced around the set point **T_S** within which the relay state is **not changed**. It is necessary to prevent **relay chattering** — the rapid ON/OFF cycling that occurs when temperature oscillates about a single threshold, which would burn out the relay contacts within days. If **H = 0**, the relay would cycle **hundreds of times per hour**, drastically reducing its mechanical life (rated ≈ 100,000 cycles) and causing electrical arcing across the contacts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a smart incubator** built using the W1209 module and an incandescent bulb. Explain the role of each block in the feedback loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A W1209 module is configured in **Heating Mode** with **T_S = 37.5 °C** and **H = 0.8 °C**. Determine the **two switching thresholds**, draw the **temperature-vs-time graph** showing the relay's ON/OFF intervals, and compute the **maximum number of relay cycles per hour** if the bulb heats the chamber at 2 °C/min and cools at 1 °C/min. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Block Diagram** (must be drawn in the answer script — re-use Section 4.1 here):

> **Blocks and their roles (describe each in 1–2 lines for 7 marks):**
>
> 1. **NTC Thermistor Probe** — senses the chamber temperature and converts it to a resistance change, which is read by the STM8 ADC inside the W1209.
> 2. **STM8S003F3 Microcontroller (inside W1209)** — executes the thermostat firmware: reads ADC, linearises the NTC, compares with T_S and H, and drives the relay.
> 3. **3-Digit 7-Segment LED Display** — provides local HMI showing the current chamber temperature, the set point (when SET is pressed), and the hysteresis.
> 4. **Push Buttons (SET, +, −)** — allow the user to programme T_S and H without any external computer.
> 5. **Onboard SPDT Relay (10 A / 250 V AC)** — electrically isolates the low-voltage control circuit from the high-voltage bulb circuit; acts as the actuator switch.
> 6. **Incandescent Bulb (25 W / 60 W)** — converts electrical energy to radiant heat, raising the chamber temperature.
> 7. **Incubator Chamber (Thermocol box)** — provides thermal insulation, keeping the heat localised around the eggs.
> 8. **12 V DC Adapter** — supplies regulated power to the W1209 module.
>
> **Feedback loop explanation:** The NTC continuously monitors the chamber. The MCU compares with T_S. When too cold, the relay closes, energising the bulb. The bulb heats the air. The NTC detects the rise. When T_now ≥ T_S + H, the relay opens, cutting off the bulb. This is a classic **closed-loop ON/OFF control system**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Switching Thresholds**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{LOW}}  &= T_S - H = 37.5 - 0.8 = 36.7 \;^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{(Relay ON, Bulb ON)} \\
T_{\text{HIGH}} &= T_S + H = 37.5 + 0.8 = 38.3 \;^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{(Relay OFF, Bulb OFF)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Bandwidth**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{HIGH}} - T_{\text{LOW}} = 38.3 - 36.7 = 1.6 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to traverse the band (heating up)**

$$
t_{\text{heat}} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\text{Heating Rate}} = \dfrac{1.6 \;^\circ\text{C}}{2 \;^\circ\text{C/min}} = 0.8 \;\text{min} = 48 \;\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to traverse the band (cooling down)**

$$
t_{\text{cool}} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\text{Cooling Rate}} = \dfrac{1.6 \;^\circ\text{C}}{1 \;^\circ\text{C/min}} = 1.6 \;\text{min} = 96 \;\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Total cycle time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{cycle}} &= t_{\text{heat}} + t_{\text{cool}} = 48 + 96 = 144 \;\text{s} = 2.4 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Cycles per hour**

$$
N_{\text{cycles/hr}} = \dfrac{3600 \;\text{s/hr}}{144 \;\text{s/cycle}} = 25 \;\text{cycles/hr}
$$

**Step 7 — Relay life estimate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rated life} &= 100{,}000 \;\text{cycles} \\
\text{Hours of operation} &= \dfrac{100{,}000}{25} = 4{,}000 \;\text{hours} \approx 166 \;\text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sketch description for the script:**
Draw a sawtooth waveform on the T-axis: starts at 36.7 °C (rising), peaks at 38.3 °C (falling), back to 36.7 °C, repeating every 2.4 min. Mark "Relay ON" during the rising segment and "Relay OFF" during the falling segment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain the **internal circuitry of the W1209 module** with reference to its STM8S003F3 microcontroller, the NTC thermistor voltage divider, the S8050 relay driver transistor, and the 7-segment display driver. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **four common faults / malfunctions** that can occur in a W1209-based incubator prototype, their **symptoms**, and **remedial actions**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

| Sub-circuit | Components | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Section** | 12 V DC jack, reverse-polarity protection diode, 3.3 V LDO regulator (e.g., HT7333) | Steps 12 V → 3.3 V for STM8 and logic |
| **Sensor Section** | NTC thermistor (R_NTC) + 10 kΩ reference resistor forming a **voltage divider** → fed to ADC pin (e.g., PB4) | Converts temperature to analog voltage |
| **MCU** | STM8S003F3 (8-bit, 16 MHz, 10-bit ADC) | Runs the thermostat firmware |
| **Display Driver** | Three 7-segment common-cathode LEDs multiplexed via three PNP transistors + 7+1 current-limit resistors | Shows T_now / T_S / H |
| **Button Matrix** | Three tactile switches (SET, +, −) connected to GPIO with internal pull-ups | User input |
| **Relay Driver** | NPN transistor S8050, base resistor (≈ 1 kΩ), flyback diode (1N4148) across relay coil | Switches the 12 V relay coil |
| **Relay Output** | SPDT relay — Common (COM), Normally Open (NO), Normally Closed (NC) screw terminals | Switches 230 V AC bulb |

**Operating voltages at key nodes:**
- VCC (input) = **12 V DC**
- VCC (logic) = **3.3 V DC**
- ADC reference = **3.3 V** (so maximum representable voltage = 3.3 V)
- Relay coil voltage = **12 V DC**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

| # | Fault | Symptom | Diagnosis | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wrong Operating Mode (C instead of H)** | Bulb glows when chamber is hot; goes off when cold — eggs never hatch | Display shows "C" in corner; long-press "−" key to toggle | Switch to Heating Mode (H) |
| 2 | **Loose NTC probe connector** | Display reads `--` or random values; relay chatters wildly | Inspect 2-pin JST connector on PCB; check for cold solder | Re-seat connector; re-solder if needed |
| 3 | **Relay contact welding (stuck ON)** | Bulb glows continuously even at 40 °C; eggs overheat | Listen for relay "click" — silence = welded contacts | Replace the 5-pin SRD-05VDC-SL-C relay (₹30 spare) |
| 4 | **Bulb over-voltage / inrush damage** | Relay clicks but bulb flickers or doesn't light | Check mains voltage (should be 220–240 V); test bulb continuity | Replace bulb; add **NTC inrush-limiter** in series |
| 5 | **Temperature overshoot** | Reading goes to 42 °C before relay cuts off | Probe placed too close to bulb | Reposition probe at egg-level height |
| 6 | **Display dimming** | 7-segment barely visible | 12 V adapter sagging below 11.5 V under load | Use 1 A adapter, not 500 mA |
| 7 | **Condensation on probe** | Erratic low readings after a few days | Probe wire not waterproof | Use the supplied waterproof stainless probe; seal joint with silicone |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Heating Mode" and "Cooling Mode"** in your script — examiners specifically test this. Always begin by **stating the mode used** for the incubator.
> 2. **Never omit the unit "°C"** when writing T_S or H — the question explicitly carries the mark.
> 3. **Always draw the relay contact configuration** (COM-NO) when showing the bulb wiring; a missing flyback diode is a common viva trap.
> 4. **Sketch the temperature-vs-time graph** even if rough — it carries **2 marks** in part (b) and students frequently lose it.
> 5. **Mention the relay's mechanical life** (10⁵ cycles) and compute the projected operational hours — this single sentence can shift your grade from B+ to A.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **W1209** is a *single-board digital thermostat* combining STM8 MCU + NTC + 7-seg display + relay.
- ✅ The **incubator must be set to Heating Mode (H)**, not Cooling Mode (C).
- ✅ Set Point for chicken eggs: **T_S = 37.5 °C**; Hysteresis: **H = 0.5 °C to 1.0 °C**.
- ✅ **Lower threshold** (relay ON) = T_S; **Upper threshold** (relay OFF) = T_S + H.
- ✅ The W1209 operates on **12 V DC**; the bulb operates on **230 V AC** — the relay provides **galvanic isolation** between the two.
- ✅ **NTC probe** must be placed at **egg-level height**, away from the bulb's hot plume.
- ✅ **Thermocol box** is preferred over metal/wood due to its very low thermal conductivity (0.03 W/m·K).
- ✅ **Hysteresis prevents relay chattering** and extends the relay's life from days to months.
- ✅ **Cycle time** = (ΔT / Heating rate) + (ΔT / Cooling rate); cycles/hour = 3600 / cycle_time.
- ✅ **Bulb wattage** must be such that steady-state current × 15 (inrush factor) < 10 A relay rating.
- ✅ **Safety fuses** in series with the bulb are a must for a fire-safe prototype.
- ✅ The experiment demonstrates a **closed-loop ON/OFF control system** — a foundational concept in control engineering and IoT.
- ✅ **Viva keywords to memorise:** Steinhart–Hart equation, bang-bang control, dead-band, galvanic isolation, SPDT relay, NTC thermistor, ADC, hysteresis, galvanic isolation, derating factor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing with Soil Moisture Sensor and Mini Water Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL607 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB USING ARDUINO
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing with Soil Moisture Sensor and Mini Water Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Soil Moisture Sensor** is an embedded electronic transducer that quantifies the volumetric water content (VWC) of a soil sample by translating its dielectric permittivity or electrical conductivity into a corresponding analog voltage signal compatible with microcontroller ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) input channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mini Water Pump (DC Submersible Pump)** is a low-voltage (typically **3 V – 12 V DC**) electromagnetic centrifugal pump driven by a brushed DC motor, used in IoT actuator subsystems to deliver controlled fluid (water) flow. It cannot be driven directly from an Arduino GPIO pin and therefore requires an electrical switching interface — typically a **Single-Channel Relay Module** or a **MOSFET driver circuit**.

Together, the **(Sensor + Microcontroller + Relay + Pump)** assembly forms a canonical **Sense → Decide → Actuate** closed-loop cyber-physical system (CPS), which is the foundational architectural pattern of every IoT application in agriculture, environmental monitoring, and smart irrigation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

Imagine a **plant's thirst** as a real-world signal:

| 🌱 Real-world Phenomenon | 🔌 Electronic Equivalent |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Dry soil (plant is thirsty) | High analog reading (≈ 1023 on 10-bit ADC) for resistive sensors |
| Wet soil (plant is hydrated) | Low analog reading (≈ 300 on 10-bit ADC) for resistive sensors |
| Farmer turning on a tap | Relay coil energizing → Common (COM) connected to Normally Open (NO) → Pump starts |
| Farmer turning off the tap | Relay coil de-energizing → Spring returns to Normally Closed (NC) → Pump stops |

So the **Arduino acts as the "farmer's brain"** — it reads the sensor (the plant's voice), compares it against a predefined threshold (the farmer's judgement), and finally commands the relay (the farmer's hand) to switch the pump on or off. This is the classic **Automatic Smart Irrigation** paradigm.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **standard ratings** you will encounter in KTU lab viva and exams:
> - **Arduino UNO ADC resolution:** 10-bit → $2^{10} = 1024$ discrete levels (0 to 1023).
> - **ADC Reference Voltage (default):** **+5.0 V DC**
> - **Minimum voltage resolution:** $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{5.0 \text{ V}}{1024} \approx \mathbf{4.88 \text{ mV}}$ per ADC step.
> - **Resistive Soil Sensor (HW-080 / FC-28) Excitation Voltage:** **3.3 V – 5 V DC**
> - **Mini DC Submersible Pump Operating Voltage:** **3 V – 6 V DC** (commonly **5 V** for Arduino-compatible kits)
> - **Mini Pump Current Draw:** **100 mA – 200 mA** (continuous) / **~400 mA** (stalled)
> - **Arduino UNO GPIO Current Limit:** **40 mA per pin (absolute max)** — hence relay is mandatory.
> - **Relay Coil Voltage:** **5 V DC** (active LOW or active HIGH depending on module)

---

## 1.4 Types of Soil Moisture Sensors

| Type | Working Principle | Pros | Cons |
|------|------------------|------|------|
| **Resistive (FC-28)** | Measures soil **electrical resistance** between two exposed probes. More water → more ions → lower resistance. | Cheap (~$1), simple, two-output (AO + DO) | Prone to **electrochemical corrosion** of probes, short lifespan (6–12 months) |
| **Capacitive (v1.2 / v2.0)** | Measures **dielectric permittivity** of soil using a copper-trace capacitor. | No DC current through soil → **no corrosion**, longer life, more accurate | Slightly costlier, needs calibration |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU lab kits in most Kerala colleges use the **capacitive v1.2** sensor by default due to its durability, but the exam may ask about either type. Always mention the **probe corrosion issue** of resistive sensors as a viva point.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The complete sense–decide–actuate pipeline executes in **three layered stages**:

### 🔹 Stage 1 — Sensing (Analog Acquisition)

1. The sensor module is energized through the **+5 V** and **GND** rails of the Arduino.
2. The exposed probe pair interacts with the soil's ionic solution.
3. An internal **op-amp comparator (LM393)** generates a continuously variable DC voltage on the **Analog Output (AO)** pin, scaled between **0 V** (water saturated) and **+5 V** (completely dry).
4. This analog voltage is fed to Arduino's ADC pin (e.g., **A0**), which samples it at the **ADC clock (≈ 15 kSps on UNO)** and quantizes it to a discrete integer between **0 and 1023**.

### 🔹 Stage 2 — Decision (Threshold Comparison)

5. The Arduino firmware compares the integer reading `sensorValue` with a calibrated **wet/dry threshold** (commonly **~500** for resistive sensors, **~350** for capacitive sensors).
6. Conditional branching executes the actuation logic:

   $$\text{If } sensorValue > Threshold_{DRY} \Rightarrow \text{Pump ON (water the plant)}$$
   
   $$\text{If } sensorValue \leq Threshold_{WET} \Rightarrow \text{Pump OFF (hysteresis band)}$$

7. **Hysteresis** is used to prevent **relay chattering** (rapid ON/OFF cycling near the threshold). A typical hysteresis band is **±50 ADC units** above and below the setpoint.

### 🔹 Stage 3 — Actuation (Relay Switching)

8. The Arduino GPIO pin (e.g., **D7**) drives the relay module's **IN** pin.
9. An internal **optocoupler + transistor driver** on the relay board energizes the **electromagnetic coil**.
10. Coil magnetization pulls the **armature contact** from the **Normally Closed (NC)** position to the **Normally Open (NO)** position.
11. The pump's **+V and GND** rails are now connected through the relay's COM-NO contacts, completing the motor circuit.
12. The pump impeller spins and pushes water to the plant.
13. When `sensorValue` falls below the wet threshold, the relay de-energizes, the spring returns the contact to NC, and the pump stops.

---

## 2.2 Relay Switching Dynamics — A Deeper Look

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flyback Diode (Freewheeling Diode):** The relay coil is an **inductor**. When current through an inductor is suddenly interrupted, the collapsing magnetic field induces a high reverse-EMF (back-EMF) that can exceed **300 V** and destroy the driving transistor. The relay module includes a **1N4007** diode in anti-parallel with the coil to safely dissipate this energy. Always mention this in viva.

| Relay Module Pin | Function |
|------------------|----------|
| **VCC** | +5 V supply to energize coil |
| **GND** | Common ground |
| **IN (or SIG)** | Logic input from Arduino GPIO |
| **NO** | Normally Open contact of load circuit |
| **NC** | Normally Closed contact of load circuit |
| **COM** | Common terminal of load circuit |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 🎯

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verified** and are **high-yield for KTU lab exams and viva**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **ADC Digitization** | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^{n}-1) \right\rfloor$ | $V_{in}$ = sensor voltage, $V_{ref}$ = +5 V, $n$ = 10 bits |
| 2 | **Inverse (Voltage Reconstruction)** | $V_{in} = D \times \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}-1}$ | Used to convert ADC reading back to voltage |
| 3 | **LSB Voltage Resolution** | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}$ | For UNO: $V_{LSB} = 5/1024 \approx 4.88$ mV |
| 4 | **Sensor Reading to % Moisture** | $\%Moisture = 100 \times \dfrac{1023 - D_{raw}}{1023 - D_{wet}}$ | $D_{wet}$ = reading in fully wet soil (calibrate empirically) |
| 5 | **Threshold with Hysteresis** | Pump ON if $D > T_{high}$, OFF if $D < T_{low}$ | $T_{high} - T_{low} = \text{Hysteresis Band}$ (typically 50–100) |
| 6 | **Pump Power Consumption** | $P_{pump} = V_{pump} \times I_{pump}$ | e.g., 5 V × 0.15 A = 0.75 W |
| 7 | **Relay Coil Power** | $P_{coil} = V_{coil} \times I_{coil}$ | Typically 5 V × 70 mA ≈ 0.35 W |
| 8 | **Current Limit of GPIO** | $I_{GPIO,max} = \mathbf{40 \text{ mA}}$ | Absolute maximum per ATmega328P datasheet |
| 9 | **ADC Sampling Time (UNO)** | $T_{samp} \approx 13$ ADC clocks = **~13 µs/sample** | At full 10-bit resolution |

> **Critical Reminder:** In markdown tables, absolute value is written as $\lvert x \rvert$, never as `|x|` (which would break the table).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 🌍

This experiment is the **DNA of precision agriculture** and is deployed at industrial scale in:

- 🌾 **Smart Irrigation Systems** (Jain Irrigation, Netafim, Bosch XDK farms)
- 🌳 **Greenhouse Automation** in Wayanad and Idukki spice plantations
- 🏞️ **Hydroponics and Vertical Farming**
- 🏜️ **Drought-monitoring IoT nodes** under India's **PMKSY (Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana)**
- 🏡 **Balcony garden automation** (DIY home projects)

> **Career Hook for KTU Students:** Job roles like *IoT Embedded Engineer at Tessolve, Bosch, Wipro IoT division, or TCS Smart Crop* require hands-on mastery of this exact sense–decide–actuate loop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Soil Resistivity to ADC Reading

We will rigorously derive the chain of transformations from physical soil property → electrical signal → digital integer → engineering decision.

### Step 1 — Soil as a Variable Resistor

The resistive soil sensor can be modeled as a **resistance $R_{soil}$** that varies inversely with moisture:

$$R_{soil} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = electrical resistivity of soil (Ω·m), which **decreases** as moisture increases.
- $L$ = distance between the two probes (fixed, ~30 mm).
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the conductive path.

> **Engineering Insight:** More water → more dissolved ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻) → lower $\rho$ → lower $R_{soil}$.

### Step 2 — Voltage Divider Stage

The sensor module contains a **voltage divider** with a fixed reference resistor $R_{ref}$ and the variable $R_{soil}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{soil}}{R_{soil} + R_{ref}}$$

> **Logic row:** $V_{cc}$ is the supply (5 V), $V_{out}$ is the analog output voltage. When soil dries, $R_{soil} \uparrow$, so $V_{out} \uparrow$ (approaches 5 V). When soil is wet, $R_{soil} \downarrow$, so $V_{out} \downarrow$ (approaches 0 V).

### Step 3 — ADC Quantization

The Arduino ADC converts the analog $V_{out}$ into a 10-bit digital word:

$$D = \text{round}\left( V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{1023}{V_{ref}} \right)$$

> **Logic row:** The factor 1023 comes from the maximum digital value of a 10-bit unsigned integer ($2^{10} - 1 = 1023$). The result is rounded to the nearest integer.

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example

Suppose a dry-soil reading produces $V_{out} = 4.0$ V. Compute $D$:

$$D = \text{round}\left( 4.0 \cdot \dfrac{1023}{5.0} \right)$$

$$D = \text{round}\left( 4.0 \times 204.6 \right)$$

$$D = \text{round}(818.4)$$

$$D = 818$$

**Therefore, the Arduino will read `sensorValue = 818` on Serial Monitor.**

### Step 5 — Inverse Calculation: ADC Reading → Voltage

To reconstruct the actual sensor voltage from a digital reading $D = 818$:

$$V_{out} = D \times \dfrac{V_{ref}}{1023} = 818 \times \dfrac{5.0}{1023}$$

$$V_{out} = 818 \times 0.004887 = 3.998 \text{ V} \approx 4.0 \text{ V}$$

This matches our original input, confirming the derivation is **bidirectionally consistent**. ✅

### Step 6 — Converting ADC to % Volumetric Water Content (VWC)

A common practical formula used in IoT dashboards is:

$$\%VWC = 100 \times \dfrac{D_{air} - D_{soil}}{D_{air} - D_{water}}$$

where:
- $D_{air}$ = sensor reading in **completely dry air** (≈ 1023 for resistive).
- $D_{water}$ = sensor reading when probe is **fully immersed in water** (≈ 250–350 for resistive).
- $D_{soil}$ = current sensor reading.

**Example:** If $D_{air} = 1023$, $D_{water} = 280$, $D_{soil} = 600$:

$$\%VWC = 100 \times \dfrac{1023 - 600}{1023 - 280} = 100 \times \dfrac{423}{743}$$

$$\%VWC = 100 \times 0.569 = \mathbf{56.9\%}$$

This means the soil is holding **~57% volumetric water content** — a value typical of well-watered loamy soil.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pump Power Budget and System Safety

### Step 7 — Why Direct GPIO Drive Fails

The ATmega328P datasheet (used in Arduino UNO) specifies:

$$I_{GPIO,max} = 40 \text{ mA (absolute max, per pin)}$$

The pump draws:

$$I_{pump} = 200 \text{ mA (continuous)}$$

Since $I_{pump} \gg I_{GPIO,max}$, direct connection would **overheat and destroy** the microcontroller pin. The ratio is:

$$\dfrac{I_{pump}}{I_{GPIO,max}} = \dfrac{200}{40} = 5\times \text{ overload}$$

### Step 8 — Relay as a Power Amplifier

The relay module's coil draws only **~70 mA** when energized — but wait, this is also above the 40 mA limit. Hence, the relay module uses an **onboard transistor driver (S8050)** that limits the GPIO current to **~5–10 mA** while switching the 70 mA coil. The relay contacts then handle the 200 mA pump load.

**Power budget summary:**

$$P_{pump} = 5 \text{ V} \times 0.20 \text{ A} = 1.0 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{coil} = 5 \text{ V} \times 0.07 \text{ A} = 0.35 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{GPIO} \approx 5 \text{ V} \times 0.01 \text{ A} = 0.05 \text{ W}$$

The relay is therefore acting as a **galvanic isolation + power amplification stage**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Implementation Choices

| Design Decision | Option A | Option B | KTU Recommendation |
|-----------------|----------|----------|---------------------|
| Sensor type | Resistive (FC-28) | Capacitive (v1.2) | Capacitive — durable |
| Threshold logic | Single threshold | Hysteresis band | Hysteresis — avoid chattering |
| Pump control | Relay (electromechanical) | MOSFET (IRF540N) | Relay — easier for beginners |
| Display feedback | Serial Monitor | 16×2 LCD | Serial Monitor for lab exam |
| Power source | USB 5 V from PC | External 9 V adapter | External adapter for pump current |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Circuit Schematic (Block + Mermaid Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional wiring architecture** of the system. (We use a flow diagram because Mermaid cannot draw physical wires; the block-level architecture is the most accurate alternative.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER["Power Distribution"]
        ps5v["Arduino +5V Rail"]
        psgnd["Arduino GND Rail"]
    end

    subgraph SENSE["Sensing Subsystem"]
        soilSensor["Capacitive Soil Moisture Sensor"]
        soilAO["Analog Output AO"]
        soilVcc["Sensor VCC"]
        soilGnd["Sensor GND"]
    end

    subgraph MCU["Arduino UNO Microcontroller"]
        pinA0["Analog Pin A0"]
        pinD7["Digital Pin D7"]
        adc["10-bit ADC Engine"]
        logic["Firmware Threshold Logic"]
    end

    subgraph SWITCH["Switching Subsystem"]
        relayVcc["Relay VCC"]
        relayGnd["Relay GND"]
        relayIn["Relay IN"]
        relayCoil["Electromagnetic Coil + Flyback Diode"]
        relayNo["Normally Open Contact NO"]
        relayCom["Common Terminal COM"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATE["Actuation Subsystem"]
        pumpPos["Pump +V Red Wire"]
        pumpNeg["Pump -V Black Wire"]
        pumpImp["Impeller Motor"]
    end

    soilVcc --> ps5v
    soilGnd --> psgnd
    soilAO --> pinA0
    pinA0 --> adc
    adc --> logic
    logic --> pinD7
    pinD7 --> relayIn
    relayVcc --> ps5v
    relayGnd --> psgnd
    relayIn --> relayCoil
    relayCoil --> relayNo
    relayNo --> relayCom
    relayCom --> pumpPos
    pumpPos --> pumpImp
    pumpImp --> pumpNeg
    pumpNeg --> psgnd
```

---

## 4.2 Signal Flow — From Sensor to Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Soil Moisture in Pot"] --> B["Sensor Probe Excitation"]
    B --> C["Analog Voltage Vout 0V to 5V"]
    C --> D["Arduino ADC Quantization"]
    D --> E["Digital Integer 0 to 1023"]
    E --> F{"Threshold Check with Hysteresis"}
    F -->|"D > Thigh"| G["Set D7 HIGH"]
    F -->|"D < Tlow"| H["Set D7 LOW"]
    G --> I["Relay Coil Energized"]
    I --> J["COM-NO Contacts Close"]
    J --> K["Pump Motor Receives +5V"]
    K --> L["Water Flows to Plant"]
    H --> M["Relay Coil De-energized"]
    M --> N["Contacts Return to NC"]
    N --> O["Pump Stops"]
```

---

## 4.3 Pin Mapping Table (Reference for KTU Record Book)

| Arduino Pin | Connected To | Type | Purpose |
|-------------|--------------|------|---------|
| **+5V** | Sensor VCC, Relay VCC | Power | Supply to sensor and relay coil |
| **GND** | Sensor GND, Relay GND, Pump GND | Power | Common ground reference |
| **A0** | Sensor AO (Analog Out) | Input | Reads moisture level (0–1023) |
| **D7** | Relay IN (Signal) | Output | Controls relay ON/OFF |

---

## 4.4 Hysteresis State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> PUMP_ON: sensorValue > Thigh
    PUMP_ON --> PUMP_OFF: sensorValue < Tlow
    PUMP_OFF --> IDLE: no action
    PUMP_ON --> PUMP_ON: Tlow < sensorValue < Thigh
    PUMP_OFF --> PUMP_OFF: Tlow < sensorValue < Thigh
```

> [!NOTE]
> The middle "hold" state (between thresholds) is the **hysteresis band** — this prevents the relay from oscillating around the threshold when the sensor reading fluctuates.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between resistive and capacitive soil moisture sensors. Which is preferred for long-term IoT deployment and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Resistive (FC-28) | Capacitive (v1.2) |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Principle** | Measures **electrical resistance** between two probes | Measures **dielectric permittivity** of soil |
| **Excitation** | DC current passes through soil | No DC current through soil (uses AC excitation) |
| **Corrosion** | **High** — probes oxidize within months | **Negligible** — no electrolysis |
| **Lifespan** | 6–12 months | 2–5 years |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate | Higher (less affected by salinity) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The **capacitive sensor is preferred** for long-term IoT deployment because the absence of DC current through the probes eliminates electrolytic corrosion, ensuring consistent readings over years.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is a relay module required to interface a 5V DC mini water pump with an Arduino UNO? Explain the role of the optocoupler and flyback diode in the relay module.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Current Mismatch (1 mark):** The Arduino GPIO can source only **40 mA** (absolute max), while the pump requires **150–200 mA** continuous current. Direct connection would damage the ATmega328P pin. The relay provides **galvanic isolation** and **current amplification**.

2. **Optocoupler (1 mark):** Provides **electrical isolation** between the low-power Arduino side and the high-power relay coil side, protecting the microcontroller from voltage spikes.

3. **Flyback Diode (1 mark):** The relay coil is an **inductor**. When de-energized, the collapsing magnetic flux induces a high reverse-EMF that can destroy the driving transistor. The **1N4007** flyback diode provides a safe path for this energy to dissipate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the working of an **automatic plant irrigation system** using an Arduino UNO, a capacitive soil moisture sensor, and a 5V DC mini water pump via a relay module. Explain the sense–decide–actuate paradigm.

**(b) [7 marks)** Write a **complete Arduino sketch** to implement the system. Define the threshold values, explain the use of **hysteresis**, and calculate the ADC voltage for a sensor reading of 723.

---

### **📝 Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — System Description [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram:** (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram) — **2 marks**

**System Description (5 marks):**

The automatic plant irrigation system implements the three-stage **Sense–Decide–Actuate** paradigm:

1. **Sense Stage:** The capacitive soil moisture sensor (v1.2) is inserted into the soil near the plant's root zone. It produces an analog voltage $V_{out}$ between 0 V (saturated wet) and 5 V (bone dry). This voltage is fed to Arduino's analog pin **A0**.

2. **Decide Stage:** The Arduino's 10-bit ADC converts $V_{out}$ to a digital integer `sensorValue` in the range 0–1023.
